docs.summary: Freed people camped on the Tar River opposite Tarboro after Union occupation in 1865, naming Freedom Hill before incorporating as Princeville in 1885 — the oldest U.S. town chartered by African Americans. Despite repeated flooding, residents rebuilt schools, churches, and businesses anchoring Edgecombe County's Black civic life for generations.

notabilityBasis.note: origin of: Formerly enslaved people gathered on swampy land across the Tar River from Tarboro after the Civil War, adopting the name Freedom Hill from a knoll where Union soldiers addressed refugees; Turner Prince, a carpenter and landowner, gave the incorporated town its name. Cited from hpo.nc.gov.

incorporated as: Freedmen in Edgecombe County incorporated Princeville on February 20, 1885, renaming a community first settled as Freedom Hill in 1865 — recognized as the nation's oldest town chartered by African Americans. Cited from ncdncr.gov.

docs.summary: When Oxford, Alabama, redrew its limits in 1899 to exclude the Mooree Quarter, 125 African American residents incorporated Hobson City on August 16 — among the earliest U.S. municipalities governed entirely by Black citizens. Anniston steel wages once supported roughly 1,500 residents before deindustrialization narrowed the town's base.

notabilityBasis.note: incorporated as: After Oxford excluded the Mooree Quarter from its boundaries, 125 African American citizens incorporated Hobson City on August 16, 1899 — one of the earliest U.S. municipalities governed entirely by African Americans. Cited from encyclopediaofalabama.org.

center of: The Alabama Historical Commission's Black Heritage Council has documented and promoted Hobson City among the state's African American heritage places, including its listing on Alabama's Places in Peril. Cited from ahc.alabama.gov.

docs.summary: After the Civil War, formerly enslaved families in north Hays County purchased land along Onion Creek beginning in 1870, forming the rural freedom colony of Antioch with its own school, churches, and Masonic lodge. Descendants revived land ownership in the 1970s after mid-century out-migration, and a Texas Historical Commission marker now stands at Antioch Colony Park on Old Black Colony Road.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Anglo landowner Joseph F. Rowley began selling $5-per-acre parcels to formerly enslaved African Americans in 1870; Elias and Clarisa Bunton donated land for a school and church in 1874, anchoring the Antioch Colony farming community. Cited from thc.texas.gov.

documented by: The National Park Service documents Texas freedom colonies — including Antioch — as intentionally created post-Emancipation landowning clusters whose histories are being mapped by the Texas Freedom Colonies Project. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Along the North Loup River in Cherry County, DeWitty — later Audacious — became Nebraska's largest rural African American settlement after homesteaders filed claims under the 1904 Kinkaid Act. At its 1915 peak roughly 150 residents supported a church, store, post office, and the North Loup Sluggers before drought emptied the colony by 1936.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Settlers including formerly enslaved people and Canadian-born homesteaders began arriving in 1906–1907 under the Kinkaid Act's 640-acre claims; DeWitty reached about 150 residents by 1915 with a church, store, post office, and baseball team. Cited from history.nebraska.gov.

documented by: The National Park Service identifies DeWitty as the most populous and long-lived Black homestead colony in Nebraska, with residents claiming 29,402 acres across fifty-six homesteads by 1929. Cited from nps.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Jim Shankle and Stephen Alexander McBride purchased land in northern Newton County after Emancipation and built Shankleville into a freedom colony of farms, mills, stores, and churches. McBride College operated from 1883 to 1909, and descendants still gather for the Purple Hull Pea Festival that sustains the community's oral and material heritage.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: After Emancipation, Jim Shankle and Stephen Alexander McBride bought land in northern Newton County, forming the nucleus of Shankleville — a community of homes, churches, businesses, and McBride College (1883–1909). Cited from thc.texas.gov.

preserved by: The Texas Historical Commission recognizes ongoing preservation at Shankleville, including the Odom Homestead restoration and archaeological documentation associated with the freedom colony. Cited from thc.texas.gov.

docs.summary: Union-occupied New Bern became a refuge for enslaved people seeking freedom during the Civil War; in 1863 the Army consolidated camps into the Trent River settlement, later renamed James City for chaplain Horace James. The cohesive Black community persisted until an 1893 court defeat over land ownership, yet James City remains an unincorporated area of roughly 700 residents east of New Bern.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: U.S. Army Chaplain Horace James supervised the 1863 consolidation of Freedmen's camps into the Trent River settlement on Spaight family land; the community was renamed James City and remained a cohesive Black settlement into the 1890s. Cited from ncdncr.gov.

documented by: National Register documentation describes James City as a Freedmen's village that grew from a refugee camp across the Trent River from New Bern, where residents collectively pursued permanent land ownership. Cited from npgallery.nps.gov; loc.gov; nps.gov.

docs.summary: Organized in 1903 on forty acres owned by William Rentie and Phoebe McIntosh, Rentiesville grew as a flag stop on the MKT Railway with a school enrolling eighty-one children by 1904. Historian John Hope Franklin was born here in 1915; the town hosts the Dusk 'til Dawn Blues Festival near the Civil War Battle of Honey Springs.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Rev. N. A. Robinson, I. J. Foster, W. D. Robinson, and William Rentie organized the Rentiesville townsite company in 1903; the post office opened May 11, 1904, and F. P. Brinson became the first mayor in 1905. Cited from okhistory.org.
part of: Rentiesville is one of more than fifty All-Black towns established in Oklahoma and one of thirteen still existing at the beginning of the twenty-first century. Cited from okhistory.org.

docs.summary: Rooted in an 1850 Creek Nation manual-labor school, Tullahassee became a Freedmen's town when the Creek council transferred the site on October 24, 1881; it is considered the oldest of Oklahoma's surviving All-Black towns. Flipper-Key-Davis University operated from 1914 until 1936 as the state's only private Black institution of higher education.

notabilityBasis.note: documented by: Library of Congress documents Tullahassee in federal historic-place and archival records, providing independent authority corroboration for this settlement's documented history. Cited from loc.gov.

transferred to: On October 24, 1881, the Creek Nation council transferred the Tullahassee manual-labor school site to Creek Freedmen; the town incorporated in 1902 and is considered the oldest surviving All-Black town in Indian Territory. Cited from okhistory.org.

hosted: Flipper-Key-Davis University, established in 1914 on the site of the former Tullahassee Manual Labor School, was the only private Black institution of higher education in Oklahoma until it closed in 1936. Cited from okhistory.org.

docs.summary: The rural African American community of Sweet Home in Guadalupe County supported a church, Rosenwald school, and farm households south of Seguin from the late nineteenth century into the mid-twentieth. The 1924 Sweet Home Vocational and Agricultural High School — listed on the National Register — now serves homecomings and a community nutrition program under Sweet Home Baptist Church.

notabilityBasis.note: center of: Sweet Home was a once-thriving Black agricultural community whose population diminished after the school closed in 1963; the building is now owned by Sweet Home Baptist Church for homecomings and community programs. Cited from thc.texas.gov.

documented by: Library of Congress documents Sweet Home in federal historic-place and archival records, providing independent authority corroboration for this settlement's documented history. Cited from loc.gov.

listed on national register: Sweet Home Vocational and Agricultural School, built in 1924 as a four-teacher Rosenwald school about ten miles south of Seguin, was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1998. Cited from thc.texas.gov.

docs.summary: Founded in 1912 on 2,700 Lake County acres, Idlewild offered African American families a segregated-era resort for vacation and property ownership when most white resorts barred them. Known as Black Eden, it drew tens of thousands of visitors before travel and civil-rights changes reshaped leisure; the historic district is on the National Register.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Idlewild was established in 1912 as a resort community where African Americans could vacation and buy property during the era of official and unofficial segregation at white resorts. Cited from michigan.gov.

documented by: Library of Congress documents Idlewild in federal historic-place and archival records, providing independent authority corroboration for this settlement's documented history. Cited from loc.gov.
listed on national register: The Idlewild Historic District was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1979 for its significance to Black social history. Cited from npgallery.nps.gov.

docs.summary: Platted in 1903 along the Fort Smith and Western Railroad in Okfuskee County, Clearview supported newspapers, the Abe Lincoln Trading Company, and a brick school within its first decade. The Abe Lincoln Trading Company building is listed on the National Register, and Clearview remains one of thirteen surviving Oklahoma All-Black towns.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: J. A. Roper, Lemuel Jackson, and John Grayson platted Clearview in 1903 and formed the Lincoln Townsite Company; by 1904 the town had a hotel, print shop, brick school, and two churches. Cited from okhistory.org.

listed on national register: The Abe Lincoln Trading Company building in Clearview was nominated to the National Register of Historic Places during the Oklahoma Historical Society's survey of All-Black towns. Cited from okhistory.org.

docs.summary: In 1872 Henry Hopkins purchased forty-two acres in DeWitt County and established Hopkinsville, a freedom colony where formerly enslaved families built Antioch Baptist Church in 1873 and operated a school by the 1880s. The Texas Historical Commission marker documents a settlement that outlasted the school closure of 1956 and continues worship near Cuero.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Members of the Hopkins family, formerly enslaved on Judge Henry Clay Pleasants's plantation, returned after the Civil War; in 1872 Henry Hopkins bought forty-two acres and established the Hopkinsville colony one mile west of the marker site. Cited from thc.texas.gov.

organized: Residents organized Antioch Baptist Church in 1873 and began a school about ten years later; the school closed in 1956, but the congregation continues near Hopkinsville. Cited from thc.texas.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Established as Sewell in 1903 and renamed in 1912 for farmer and cotton buyer A. R. Brooks, Brooksville in Pottawatomie County drew Southern settlers through Rev. Jedson White's promotion and supported a Santa Fe depot, Rosenwald-funded Banneker School, and St. John's Baptist Church. The town incorporated in 1972 and remains one of Oklahoma's thirteen surviving All-Black towns.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Brooksville was established in 1903 as Sewell and renamed in 1912; Rev. Jedson White organized St. John's Baptist Church in 1906 and promoted the town throughout the South. Cited from okhistory.org.

hosted: With Rosenwald Fund support, Banneker School opened in 1924 as a four-room rock building with a 300-seat auditorium; George W. McLaurin taught there before his graduate-school desegregation case at the University of Oklahoma. Cited from okhistory.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: The Red Bird Investment Company recruited Southern families to a Wagoner County townsite whose 1907 grand opening drew more than six hundred attendees. Miller Washington High School, Red Bird City Hall, and commercial buildings from 1910 are documented in the National Register and Oklahoma Landmarks Inventory; Mayor I. W. Lane successfully challenged Wagoner County voting restrictions in 1938.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: The Barber and Ruffin families settled before 1900; Red Bird gained a post office in 1902, and the Red Bird Investment Company recruited families from across the South to an August 10, 1907, grand opening attended by more than six hundred people. Cited from okhistory.org.

documented by: Library of Congress documents Red Bird in federal historic-place and archival records, providing independent authority corroboration for this settlement's documented history. Cited from loc.gov; nps.gov.

listed on national register: Red Bird City Hall and Miller Washington High School are listed on the National Register of Historic Places; Sharp's Grocery/Masonic Hall and the Red Bird Drugstore (1910) appear in the Oklahoma Landmarks Inventory. Cited from okhistory.org.

docs.summary: Beginning as Twine on Creek Freedmen allotments west of Muskogee, Taft was renamed in 1904 and promoted as the fastest-growing Colored community in Oklahoma, supporting two newspapers and a broad commercial strip before 1910. Taft City Hall (1910) is listed on the National Register, and in 1973 Lelia Foley-Davis became the nation's first elected African American woman mayor.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Taft developed on Creek Freedmen allotments; renamed from Twine in 1904, it supported three general stores, a bank, hotels, and two newspapers before 1910, with Reaves Realty advertising it as the fastest-growing Colored community in Oklahoma. Cited from okhistory.org.

documented by: Library of Congress documents Taft in federal historic-place and archival records, providing independent authority corroboration for this settlement's documented history. Cited from loc.gov.

listed on national register: Taft City Hall, constructed in 1910, is listed on the National Register of Historic Places (NR 84003330) and is among the oldest surviving local-government buildings in Oklahoma's All-Black towns. Cited from okhistory.org.

docs.summary: Sarah Breedlove built a hair-care and cosmetics business that trained tens of thousands of Black sales agents and made her, by most historical accounts, the first female self-made millionaire in the United States. She commissioned Villa Lewaro, her Irvington-on-Hudson estate, as a showcase of Black achievement and a Harlem Renaissance gathering place.

notabilityBasis.note: documented by: Library of Congress holds archival materials documenting Madam C.J. Walker, corroborating biographical and career records cited in this catalog entry. Cited from loc.gov.

recognized as: the first female self-made millionaire in the United States, built on a hair-care and cosmetics business serving Black women customers across the U.S., Central America, and the Caribbean. Cited from nmaahc.si.edu.
built: Villa Lewaro, her 34-room Irvington, New York estate designed by architect Vertner Tandy and completed in 1918, designated a National Historic Landmark in 1976. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Maggie Lena Walker chartered the St. Luke Penny Savings Bank in Richmond, Virginia in 1903, becoming the first woman of any race to charter a bank in the United States. She served as its president for nearly three decades, helping Black Richmonders finance homes and small businesses.

notabilityBasis.note: home designated: a National Historic Site by Congress in 1978; the National Park Service opened her restored Jackson Ward home as a museum in 1985. Cited from nps.gov.
documented by: Library of Congress holds archival materials documenting Maggie L. Walker, corroborating biographical and career records cited in this catalog entry. Cited from loc.gov.
chartered: the St. Luke Penny Savings Bank in 1903, becoming the first woman of any race to charter a bank in the United States and serving as its first president. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Reginald F. Lewis engineered the 1984 leveraged buyout of Beatrice International Foods, and by 1987 the resulting TLC Beatrice International Holdings reported $1.8 billion in annual sales, making it the first Black-owned company with more than $1 billion in revenue. He attended Dunbar High School in Baltimore before Virginia State and Harvard Law School.

notabilityBasis.note: built: TLC Beatrice International Holdings into the first Black-owned company to top $1 billion in annual revenue, after his 1984 leveraged buyout of Beatrice International Foods. Cited from blackpast.org.

admitted to: Harvard Law School in 1965 without having formally applied, the only person in the school's history admitted that way, after impressing faculty in a Rockefeller Foundation summer program. Cited from reginaldflewis.com.

docs.summary: Ursula Burns rose from a Xerox internship to the company's presidency in 2007 and, in July 2009, became CEO — the first Black woman to lead a Fortune 500 company. She also became the first woman to succeed another woman, Anne Mulcahy, as head of a Fortune 500 firm.

notabilityBasis.note: led: Xerox's transformation from a photocopier manufacturer into an international business-services company during her 2009-2016 tenure as CEO and 2010-2017 tenure as chairman. Cited from britannica.com.
became: CEO of Xerox Corporation on July 1, 2009, the first African American woman to lead a Fortune 500 company. Cited from blackpast.org.

docs.summary: Kenneth Chenault joined American Express in 1981 and rose to chairman and CEO in 2001, becoming the company's first Black chief executive and, at the time, only the third African American to lead a Fortune 500 corporation. He led the company until 2018.

notabilityBasis.note: became: chairman and CEO of American Express on January 1, 2001, the company's first Black chief executive after two decades rising through its ranks. Cited from blackpast.org.
joined: Facebook's board of directors in 2018 as its first Black board member, after retiring as American Express CEO. Cited from qz.com.

docs.summary: Robert L. Johnson founded Black Entertainment Television in 1980 and took it public in 1991, the first Black-owned company listed on the New York Stock Exchange. When Viacom bought BET for roughly $3 billion in 2001, Johnson became the first Black American billionaire.

notabilityBasis.note: documented by: National Park Service holds archival materials documenting Robert L. Johnson, corroborating biographical and career records cited in this catalog entry. Cited from nps.gov.

founded: Black Entertainment Television in 1980 and served as its founder, chairman, and CEO; BET became the first Black-owned company to trade on the New York Stock Exchange in 1991. Cited from blackpast.org.
became: the first Black American billionaire after Viacom's 2001 acquisition of BET for approximately $3 billion, debuting on Forbes' list that year. Cited from cnbc.com.

docs.summary: Oprah Winfrey built a media empire from her Chicago-based Harpo Productions, which produced her nationally syndicated talk show. In 2003, Forbes named her the first Black woman billionaire, a title she held alone on the magazine's billionaires list for several years.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Harpo Productions, headquartered at its Chicago studio complex, which produced The Oprah Winfrey Show for 25 seasons before the studio closed in 2015 and was later demolished. Cited from achievement.org.
recognized as: the first Black woman billionaire, per Forbes' 2003 billionaires list, built on her ownership of Harpo Productions and The Oprah Winfrey Show. Cited from blackenterprise.com.

docs.summary: After 36 years in leadership roles at AT&T, Cynthia "Cynt" Marshall became CEO of the Dallas Mavericks in February 2018, the first Black woman to lead an NBA franchise. She was hired to overhaul the team's workplace culture after a misconduct investigation.

notabilityBasis.note: rebuilt: the Mavericks' leadership team so that roughly half its members were women or people of color, after finding no women or people of color in leadership when she arrived. Cited from forbes.com.
became: CEO of the Dallas Mavericks in February 2018, the first Black woman to serve as CEO of an NBA franchise. Cited from cnbc.com.

docs.summary: Cathy Hughes built Radio One from a single Washington, D.C. station she bought in 1980 into the nation's largest Black-owned broadcasting company. When Radio One went public on NASDAQ in 1999, Hughes became the first African American woman to head a publicly traded corporation.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Radio One in 1980 after purchasing Washington, D.C. station WOL-AM, building it into the company now known as Urban One, the largest Black-owned multimedia company in the U.S.. Cited from blackenterprise.com.
became: the first African American woman to head a publicly traded corporation in the United States when Radio One (now Urban One) listed on NASDAQ in 1999. Cited from blackpast.org.

docs.summary: A.G. Gaston built a $40 million Birmingham business empire spanning banking, insurance, construction, and hospitality, overcoming Jim Crow-era barriers from humble beginnings in Demopolis, Alabama. His A.G. Gaston Motel, opened in 1954, became a strategic headquarters for civil rights leaders during the 1963 Birmingham campaign.

notabilityBasis.note: built: a business empire worth an estimated $40 million by his later years, including the Booker T. Washington Insurance Company, Citizens Federal Savings and Loan, and a business college. Cited from encyclopediaofalabama.org.

provided: his A.G. Gaston Motel, opened July 1, 1954, as a safe headquarters where Martin Luther King Jr. and other civil rights leaders strategized during the 1963 Birmingham campaign. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: John H. Johnson built Johnson Publishing Company from a $500 loan against his mother's furniture into the largest Black-owned publishing company in the world, launching Negro Digest, Ebony, and Jet. notabilityBasis.note: founded: Ebony magazine in 1945 and Jet magazine in 1951, building Johnson Publishing Company into the leading 20th-century publisher of African American news and lifestyle magazines. Cited from blackpast.org.
became: in 1982 the first African American to appear on the Forbes 400 list of the wealthiest Americans. Cited from britannica.com.

docs.summary: Annie Turnbo Malone developed hair-care products for Black women and built the Poro Company into a national beauty empire, becoming a millionaire and training tens of thousands of sales agents. Her Poro College campus in St. Louis's Ville neighborhood combined a factory, cosmetology school, and community center.

notabilityBasis.note: built: the Poro Company hair-care business into a national operation that, by 1920, employed 300 people locally and 75,000 sales agents nationally. Cited from nmaahc.si.edu.

docs.summary: Earl G. Graves Sr. founded Black Enterprise magazine in 1970 to chronicle and support African American entrepreneurship, building it into the leading business publication and media company serving Black professionals. A Morgan State University economics graduate, he later had the university's business school named in his honor.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Black Enterprise magazine in August 1970 to chronicle African American entrepreneurship and build tools for Black business success, growing it into a multimedia company. Cited from blackpast.org.
honored by: his alma mater, Morgan State University, which named its business school the Earl G. Graves School of Business and Management after the 1958 economics graduate. Cited from morgan.edu.

docs.summary: Denied flight training in the United States because of her race and sex, Bessie Coleman traveled to France and, on June 15, 1921, earned an international pilot's license from the Fédération Aéronautique Internationale, becoming the first Black woman and first Black American to hold a pilot's license. She toured the U.S. as a barnstorming exhibition flier until her death in 1926.

notabilityBasis.note: buried at: Lincoln Cemetery in Alsip, Illinois, where Black aviators continue a tradition of dropping flowers during flyovers of her grave each year. Cited from blackpast.org.

earned: an international aviation pilot's license from the Fédération Aéronautique Internationale on June 15, 1921, becoming the first African American and first Native American to hold a pilot's license. Cited from si.edu.

docs.summary: Eugene Bullard fled Jim Crow Georgia as a teenager, eventually reaching France, where he flew combat missions for the French Air Service in World War I, becoming the first Black military pilot in history. Barred from flying for the United States, which excluded Black pilots at the time, he later ran nightclubs in Paris and was later decorated by France as a Knight of the Legion of Honor.

notabilityBasis.note: marked by: a 2007 historical marker erected by the Historic Columbus Foundation and Historic Chattahoochee Commission near the site of his childhood home. Cited from hmdb.org.

became: one of the first African American military pilots in history after earning his French pilot's license in 1917 and flying more than 20 combat missions for France's Aéronautique Militaire. Cited from si.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Willa Brown earned her U.S. pilot's license in 1937 and, with Cornelius Coffey, co-founded the Coffey School of Aeronautics in 1938, the first flight school owned and operated by African Americans. In 1941 she became the first Black officer in the Civil Air Patrol, and the school she ran trained pilots who went on to join the Tuskegee Airmen.

notabilityBasis.note: co founded: the Coffey School of Aeronautics at Harlem Airport in 1938 with Cornelius Coffey, the first private flight-training academy owned and operated by African Americans, which trained roughly 200 pilots including future Tuskegee Airmen. Cited from archives.gov.
became: the first African American officer in the Civil Air Patrol in 1941, commissioned as a lieutenant and named federal coordinator of its Chicago unit. Cited from faa.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Guion "Guy" Bluford, an Air Force veteran of 144 Vietnam combat missions with a doctorate in aerospace engineering, became the first African American in space aboard Space Shuttle Challenger's STS-8 mission, launched from Kennedy Space Center on August 30, 1983. He flew four shuttle missions between 1983 and 1992.

notabilityBasis.note: became: the first African American to fly in space as a mission specialist on STS-8, which launched from Launch Complex 39A on August 30, 1983, the Space Shuttle program's first night launch. Cited from nasa.gov.
flew: four Space Shuttle missions between 1983 and 1992 and was later inducted into the U.S. Astronaut Hall of Fame in 2010 and the National Aviation Hall of Fame in 2019. Cited from si.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

The entity received a major, named national or field-defining honor or hall-of-fame induction (e.g. Congressional Gold Medal, a national Hall of Fame, a Pulitzer, a National Medal). Local or purely commercial awards do not qualify alone.

docs.summary: Mae Jemison, a physician and engineer who previously served as a Peace Corps medical officer in West Africa, became the first African American woman in space as a mission specialist aboard Space Shuttle Endeavour's STS-47 mission, launched from Kennedy Space Center on September 12, 1992.

notabilityBasis.note: logged: 190 hours, 30 minutes in space over 127 orbits of Earth during the 8-day STS-47 mission, a cooperative flight with Japan's space agency. Cited from archives.gov.
became: the first African American woman to travel into space, serving as a mission specialist on STS-47, which launched from Launch Complex 39B on September 12, 1992. Cited from nasa.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Ronald McNair, a physicist with a doctorate from MIT, became the second African American in space when he flew aboard Space Shuttle Challenger's STS-41-B mission in 1984. He died in the Challenger disaster on January 28, 1986, during his second shuttle flight, STS-51-L.

notabilityBasis.note: became: the second African American in space, flying as a mission specialist aboard Challenger on STS-41-B from February 3-11, 1984. Cited from britannica.com.
honored by: his hometown of Lake City, South Carolina, which dedicated its public library as the Ronald E. McNair Life History Center in his memory. Cited from visitlakecitysc.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Charles Bolden, a Marine Corps aviator and four-time Space Shuttle astronaut who piloted the 1990 mission that deployed the Hubble Space Telescope, became NASA's first Black Administrator when the Senate confirmed his nomination by President Obama on July 15, 2009. He led the agency until 2017.

notabilityBasis.note: piloted: Space Shuttle Discovery's STS-31 mission in 1990, which deployed the Hubble Space Telescope, one of four shuttle flights he flew during a NASA career logging over 680 hours in space. Cited from nasa.gov.
became: the 12th Administrator of NASA and the first African American to hold the position, confirmed by the Senate on July 15, 2009 and serving until January 2017. Cited from si.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Frederick Gregory, a Vietnam combat rescue pilot and graduate of Washington, D.C.'s Anacostia High School, became the first Black astronaut to pilot a space shuttle when he flew Challenger's STS-51-B mission in 1985, and the first Black mission commander when he led Discovery's STS-33 in 1989.

notabilityBasis.note: became: the first Black astronaut to pilot a Space Shuttle, flying Challenger's STS-51-B mission in 1985, and the first to command a shuttle mission, leading Discovery's STS-33 in 1989. Cited from blackpast.org.
graduated from: Anacostia High School in Washington, D.C. in 1958, becoming the first astronaut born, raised, and educated in the nation's capital. Cited from si.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Robert Henry Lawrence Jr., a chemist with a Ph.D. from Ohio State and an Air Force test pilot, became the first African American selected as an astronaut when the U.S. Air Force chose him for its Manned Orbiting Laboratory program in June 1967. He died in a training-flight crash at Edwards Air Force Base six months later, before he could fly in space.

notabilityBasis.note: died: in an F-104 training-flight crash at Edwards Air Force Base on December 8, 1967, at age 32, before the Manned Orbiting Laboratory program flew any missions. Cited from britannica.com.

became: the first African American selected as an astronaut when the U.S. Air Force chose him for its Manned Orbiting Laboratory program in June 1967, shortly after completing Air Force Test Pilot School. Cited from nasa.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Victor Glover piloted SpaceX Crew Dragon's first operational flight, Crew-1, from Kennedy Space Center in November 2020, becoming the first Black astronaut to serve a long-duration mission aboard the International Space Station. In 2026 he piloted Artemis II, becoming the first Black astronaut to travel beyond low Earth orbit toward the Moon.

notabilityBasis.note: piloted: SpaceX's Crew-1 mission, which launched from Launch Complex 39A on November 16, 2020, making him the first Black astronaut to complete a long-duration stay aboard the International Space Station. Cited from nasa.gov.
became: the first Black astronaut to fly beyond low Earth orbit and travel to the vicinity of the Moon as pilot of the Artemis II lunar flyby mission. Cited from si.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Bernard Harris, a physician who studied astronaut bone loss at NASA Ames before joining the astronaut corps in 1991, became the first African American to perform a spacewalk on February 9, 1995, during Space Shuttle Discovery's STS-63 mission, the first Shuttle rendezvous with Russia's Mir space station.

notabilityBasis.note: became: the first African American to perform a spacewalk, on February 9, 1995, during the STS-63 mission that launched from Launch Complex 39B. Cited from nasa.gov.
flew: as payload commander on STS-63, the first Shuttle mission to rendezvous with Russia's Mir space station, and previously as a mission specialist on STS-55 in 1993. Cited from nasa.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: After Continental Airlines refused to hire him despite his qualifications, Marlon Green fought a six-year legal battle that reached the U.S. Supreme Court. The Court's 1963 ruling in Colorado Anti-Discrimination Commission v. Continental Air Lines forced the Denver-based carrier to hire him, opening major U.S. airline cockpits to Black pilots.

notabilityBasis.note: won: the U.S. Supreme Court case Colorado Anti-Discrimination Commission v. Continental Air Lines, Inc. (372 U.S. 714), decided April 22, 1963, which found Continental's refusal to hire him unlawfully discriminatory. Cited from si.edu.
flew: for Continental Airlines from 1965 to 1978 after the ruling forced his hiring, becoming one of the first Black pilots at a major U.S. carrier. Cited from blackpast.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity (case, law, person) is tied to a judicial decision that set binding or widely cited precedent affecting Black Americans' rights or status.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: A former Tuskegee Airmen flight instructor, Perry Young was hired by New York Airways in December 1956 and, on February 5, 1957, flew as co-pilot on a scheduled helicopter flight from LaGuardia Airport, becoming the first African American pilot for a scheduled U.S. commercial airline.

notabilityBasis.note: trained: more than 150 Tuskegee Airmen as one of about 40 Black flight instructors for the 99th Pursuit Squadron before his airline career. Cited from blackpast.org.

became: the first African American pilot of a regularly scheduled U.S. commercial airline on February 5, 1957, co-piloting a New York Airways Sikorsky S-58 helicopter flight from LaGuardia to Idlewild airports. Cited from si.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Katherine Johnson joined NACA's segregated West Area Computing unit at Langley Research Center in 1953 and spent 33 years calculating flight trajectories, including the launch window for John Glenn's 1962 orbital flight and the rendezvous paths for the Apollo Moon missions. President Obama awarded her the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2015.

notabilityBasis.note: calculated: the trajectory for John Glenn's 1962 Friendship 7 orbital flight, which Glenn asked to have verified by hand before he would fly, and later helped calculate the rendezvous paths for the Apollo lunar missions. Cited from nasa.gov.
honored with: the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2015 and the dedication of the Katherine G. Johnson Computational Research Facility at NASA Langley in September 2017. Cited from nasa.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Dorothy Vaughan, hired as a mathematician at NACA's segregated Langley laboratory in 1943, became acting supervisor of the West Area Computing unit in 1949 — the first African American supervisor at the agency. She later taught herself and her staff FORTRAN to prepare them for NASA's transition to electronic computing.

notabilityBasis.note: taught herself: the FORTRAN programming language in the early 1960s and trained her staff in it, helping the center transition from human computers to electronic computing, before retiring in 1971 after 28 years. Cited from blackpast.org.
became: the first African American supervisor at NACA in 1949, leading the segregated West Area Computing unit of Black women mathematicians until its integration in 1958. Cited from nasa.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Mary Jackson petitioned the City of Hampton to attend then-segregated night classes so she could qualify for promotion, and in 1958 became NASA's first Black female engineer, authoring research on airflow around aircraft at Langley for nearly two decades. In 2021 NASA renamed its Washington headquarters building in her honor.

notabilityBasis.note: honored by: NASA's renaming of its Washington, D.C. headquarters building as the Mary W. Jackson NASA Headquarters in a ceremony held February 26, 2021. Cited from nasa.gov.
documented by: Library of Congress holds archival materials documenting Mary Jackson, corroborating biographical and career records cited in this catalog entry. Cited from loc.gov.

became: NASA's first Black female engineer in 1958, after petitioning the City of Hampton for permission to attend segregated graduate-level night courses required for the promotion. Cited from nasa.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Percy Julian, grandson of enslaved people, became a pioneering research chemist who synthesized physostigmine and developed low-cost methods to produce synthetic cortisone and steroid hormones from soybeans, laying groundwork for the modern steroid drug industry. He held more than 130 patents and moved his family to Oak Park, Illinois in 1950.

notabilityBasis.note: achieved: the first chemical synthesis of the natural product physostigmine, recognized by the American Chemical Society in 1999 as a National Historic Chemical Landmark, and pioneered large-scale synthesis of cortisone and other steroid hormones from soybean sterols. Cited from britannica.com.

endured: a firebombing of his newly purchased home in Oak Park, Illinois on Thanksgiving Day 1950, before he and his family moved in as the neighborhood's first Black residents; he remained in the house until his death in 1975. Cited from cen.acs.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Charles Drew's research on blood chemistry and preservation at Columbia University led to his 1940 doctoral thesis on blood banking, and he directed the "Blood for Britain" program that shipped thousands of liters of plasma during World War II. As chair of surgery at Howard University College of Medicine, he trained a generation of Black surgeons.

notabilityBasis.note: led: the Howard University College of Medicine surgery department as chair and served as chief of surgery at Freedmen's Hospital, training a generation of Black surgeons after his blood-banking work. Cited from nmaahc.si.edu.
documented by: National Park Service holds archival materials documenting Charles Drew, corroborating biographical and career records cited in this catalog entry. Cited from nps.gov; loc.gov.

became: the first African American to earn a doctorate in medical science from Columbia University, in 1940, with a thesis on blood banking that established him as the field's leading authority. Cited from sciencehistory.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Patricia Bath, the first woman appointed to UCLA's Jules Stein Eye Institute faculty in 1974, invented the Laserphaco Probe for cataract treatment and in 1988 became the first African American female physician to receive a U.S. patent for a medical device. She was also the first woman in the U.S. to chair an ophthalmology residency program.

notabilityBasis.note: received: a U.S. patent in 1988 for the Laserphaco Probe, a laser device for cataract removal, becoming the first African American female physician to receive a patent for a medical purpose. Cited from uspto.gov.

became: in 1974 the first woman appointed to the faculty of UCLA's Jules Stein Eye Institute, and in 1983 the first woman in the United States to chair an ophthalmology residency program, at Drew-UCLA. Cited from uclahealth.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Daniel Hale Williams founded Provident Hospital in Chicago in 1891, the first Black-owned hospital and nursing school in the United States, open to patients and staff of all races. There on July 10, 1893, he performed one of the world's first successful open-heart surgeries, repairing a stab wound to a patient's pericardium without antibiotics or blood transfusion.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Provident Hospital and Training School for Nurses in 1891, the first Black-owned hospital in the United States and the country's first interracial hospital and nursing school. Cited from abc7chicago.com.

performed: one of the world's first successful open-heart surgeries at Provident Hospital on July 10, 1893, repairing a stab-wound patient's pericardium and internal mammary artery; the patient lived another 20 years. Cited from columbiasurgery.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Ernest Everett Just, a Dartmouth-trained biologist who earned a University of Chicago PhD in zoology in 1916, spent roughly two decades of summers researching cell fertilization and marine invertebrate development at the Marine Biological Laboratory in Woods Hole, Massachusetts. In 1915 he received the first NAACP Spingarn Medal for his scientific achievement.

notabilityBasis.note: conducted: pioneering research at the Marine Biological Laboratory on the role of the cell surface in fertilization and development, working nearly every summer at Woods Hole for about two decades starting in 1909. Cited from embryo.asu.edu.
received: the first NAACP Spingarn Medal in 1915, awarded for his emerging achievement in cell biology research. Cited from mbl.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: David Blackwell, a statistician who earned his PhD at age 22 from the University of Illinois, became UC Berkeley's first Black tenured professor in 1954 and, in 1965, the first African American elected to the National Academy of Sciences. He co-developed the Rao-Blackwell theorem and mentored more than 60 doctoral students.

notabilityBasis.note: chaired: UC Berkeley's statistics department from 1957 to 1961 and co-developed the Rao-Blackwell theorem, a foundational result in statistical estimation theory. Cited from blackpast.org.

became: the first African American elected to the National Academy of Sciences, in 1965, and previously UC Berkeley's first Black tenured professor after joining the faculty in 1954. Cited from britannica.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Shirley Ann Jackson earned a PhD in theoretical elementary particle physics from MIT in 1973, becoming the first Black woman to earn a doctorate from that institution. After a research career at Bell Laboratories and chairing the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission, she became president of Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute in 1999.

notabilityBasis.note: co founded: MIT's Black Students' Union in 1968 as a graduate student, advocating for the recruitment and retention of Black students at the institution. Cited from britannica.com.
became: the first Black woman to earn a doctorate from MIT, completing her PhD in theoretical elementary particle physics in 1973. Cited from blackhistory.mit.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Marie Maynard Daly earned her PhD in chemistry from Columbia University on June 3, 1947, becoming the first Black woman in the United States to hold a doctorate in chemistry. Her subsequent research at Rockefeller University and Albert Einstein College of Medicine advanced understanding of protein synthesis and the links between cholesterol, hypertension, and atherosclerosis.

notabilityBasis.note: conducted: research at Rockefeller University co-authoring studies on how protein production requires RNA, and later research at Albert Einstein College of Medicine linking cholesterol and hypertension to atherosclerosis. Cited from sciencehistory.org.

became: the first African American woman in the United States to earn a PhD in chemistry, awarded by Columbia University's Graduate School of Arts and Sciences on June 3, 1947. Cited from gsas.columbia.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Ralph Bunche, a Howard University political scientist who helped draft the United Nations charter, mediated the 1949 Arab-Israeli armistice agreements after his predecessor was assassinated. In 1950 he became the first Black person and first person of color to win the Nobel Peace Prize.

notabilityBasis.note: helped: draft the United Nations charter as a State Department advisor to the 1945 San Francisco Conference, before becoming a UN under-secretary-general and briefly acting secretary-general in 1953. Cited from nps.gov.
documented by: Library of Congress holds archival materials documenting Ralph Bunche, corroborating biographical and career records cited in this catalog entry. Cited from loc.gov.

won: the 1950 Nobel Peace Prize, becoming the first Black person and first person of color to receive a Nobel Prize, for mediating the 1949 armistice agreements between Israel and its neighbors. Cited from nobelprize.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Toni Morrison, a novelist and Howard University graduate whose works include Song of Solomon and Beloved, became the first Black woman to win the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1993. She joined Princeton University's faculty in 1989 as the Robert F. Goheen Professor, teaching literature and creative writing until her 2006 retirement.

notabilityBasis.note: taught: as the Robert F. Goheen Professor at Princeton University from 1989 until her retirement in 2006, teaching literature, creative writing, and African American studies and founding the Princeton Atelier. Cited from britannica.com.
documented by: Library of Congress holds archival materials documenting Toni Morrison, corroborating biographical and career records cited in this catalog entry. Cited from loc.gov.
won: the 1993 Nobel Prize in Literature, becoming the first Black woman to receive the honor, cited for novels 'characterized by visionary force and poetic import'. Cited from nobelprize.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Rebecca Lee Crumpler, the only Black student at Boston's New England Female Medical College, graduated on March 1, 1864, becoming the first African American woman to earn a medical degree in the United States. She practiced in Boston and postwar Richmond, Virginia, focusing on care for formerly enslaved people, women, and children.

notabilityBasis.note: honored with: a granite gravestone dedicated in 2020 at Fairview Cemetery in Hyde Park, 125 years after her death, after decades in an unmarked grave, funded by donations from Massachusetts medical schools and the public. Cited from bu.edu.
became: the first African American woman to earn a medical degree in the United States, graduating from the New England Female Medical College in Boston on March 1, 1864. Cited from nps.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: James McCune Smith, denied admission to American medical schools because of his race, earned three degrees including a medical doctorate from the University of Glasgow before returning to New York City in 1837 to open a medical practice and pharmacy, becoming the first African American to hold a medical degree and to operate a pharmacy in the United States.

notabilityBasis.note: became: the first African American to hold a medical degree, earning his M.D. from the University of Glasgow in 1837 after no American university would admit him. Cited from smithsonianmag.com.

opened: a medical office and pharmacy at 93 West Broadway in Manhattan upon his return to New York in 1837, becoming the first African American to operate a pharmacy in the United States. Cited from nyhistory.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Alexa Canady completed her neurosurgical residency at the University of Minnesota in 1981, becoming the first African American woman to train and qualify as a neurosurgeon in the United States. She later served as chief of neurosurgery at the Children's Hospital of Michigan in Detroit and co-developed a widely used programmable shunt for treating hydrocephalus.

notabilityBasis.note: served: as chief of neurosurgery at the Children's Hospital of Michigan in Detroit from 1987 until her retirement in 2001, and co-developed a programmable antisiphon shunt system for treating hydrocephalus. Cited from aaregistry.org.

became: the first African American woman in the United States to train and become board-certified in neurosurgery, completing her residency at the University of Minnesota in 1981 and certifying in 1984. Cited from nlm.nih.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: As director of pediatric neurosurgery at Johns Hopkins, Ben Carson led a 70-person surgical team that on September 6, 1987 separated 7-month-old craniopagus (head-conjoined) twins Patrick and Benjamin Binder, the first such separation in which both twins survived the operation itself, after 23 previous attempts worldwide had ended in at least one death.

notabilityBasis.note: directed: pediatric neurosurgery at Johns Hopkins Hospital, where he became one of the youngest major-hospital department directors in the country before later serving as U.S. Secretary of Housing and Urban Development. Cited from foxnews.com.

led: a 22-hour, 70-person surgical team at Johns Hopkins Children's Center that separated craniopagus twins Patrick and Benjamin Binder on September 6, 1987, a first-of-its-kind operation using deep hypothermic circulatory arrest. Cited from britannica.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Alain Locke graduated with honors from Harvard in 1907 and became the first African American selected as a Rhodes Scholar, studying at Oxford's Hertford College from 1907 to 1910 despite being denied admission to several colleges because of his race. He later chaired Howard University's philosophy department for nearly 40 years, remembered as the philosophical architect of the Harlem Renaissance.

notabilityBasis.note: became: the first African American selected as a Rhodes Scholar in 1907, studying at Oxford's Hertford College from 1907 to 1910 after being refused entry to several other Oxford colleges because of his race. Cited from nmaahc.si.edu.

chaired: Howard University's philosophy department, which he helped establish as one of the first at a historically Black college, teaching there for nearly 40 years until his 1953 retirement. Cited from blackpast.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Euphemia Lofton Haynes earned her PhD in mathematics from the Catholic University of America in 1943, becoming the first African American woman to hold a doctorate in mathematics. She founded the math department at Miner Teachers College in Washington, D.C. in 1930 and later became the first woman to chair the D.C. Board of Education.

notabilityBasis.note: became: the first African American woman to earn a PhD in mathematics, awarded by the Catholic University of America in 1943 for a dissertation on symmetric correspondences. Cited from catholic.edu; britannica.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Evelyn Boyd Granville earned her PhD in mathematics from Yale University in 1949, becoming, together with Marjorie Lee Browne that same year, one of the first two African American women to earn a mathematics doctorate. She later worked at IBM and NASA contractors calculating orbital trajectories for the Mercury and Apollo space programs.

notabilityBasis.note: calculated: orbital trajectories and computer software for NASA's Mercury and Apollo programs while working for IBM and later a NASA contractor in the 1950s and 1960s. Cited from encyclopedia.com.

became: in 1949 one of the first two African American women to earn a PhD in mathematics, completing her doctorate at Yale with a dissertation on Laguerre series in the complex domain, the same year Marjorie Lee Browne earned hers at the University of Michigan. Cited from agnesscott.edu.

docs.summary: Marjorie Lee Browne completed her mathematics doctorate at the University of Michigan in 1949, becoming, together with Evelyn Boyd Granville that same year, one of the first two African American women to earn a PhD in mathematics. She spent 30 years on the faculty of North Carolina College (now North Carolina Central University), securing a rare early IBM computer for her students in 1960.

notabilityBasis.note: became: in 1949 one of the first two African American women to earn a PhD in mathematics, completing her University of Michigan dissertation on topological and matrix groups, the same year Evelyn Boyd Granville earned hers at Yale. Cited from scientificlib.com.

secured: a National Science Foundation grant in 1960 to bring one of the first computers at a historically Black college to North Carolina College, where she taught mathematics for three decades. Cited from appstate.edu.

docs.summary: Garrett Morgan patented a smoke-protective safety hood in 1912 and personally led rescuers wearing it into a gas-filled tunnel beneath Lake Erie after the July 1916 Cleveland Waterworks disaster, saving several lives. He patented a three-position traffic signal with a caution position in 1923 and sold the rights to General Electric.

notabilityBasis.note: led: a rescue party wearing his patented safety hoods into a gas-filled tunnel beneath Lake Erie after the July 25, 1916 Cleveland Waterworks explosion, helping locate survivors in a disaster that killed 20 workers. Cited from clevelandwater.com.
patented: a three-position traffic signal with a caution position in 1923, later selling the rights to General Electric for $40,000. Cited from invent.org.
documented by: National Park Service holds archival materials documenting Garrett Morgan, corroborating biographical and career records cited in this catalog entry. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Frederick McKinley Jones patented the first practical automatic refrigeration unit for long-haul trucks in 1940, work that led to the 1938 founding of Thermo King in Minneapolis, where he served as chief engineer. He held more than 60 patents and in 1944 became the first African American elected to the American Society of Refrigeration Engineers.

notabilityBasis.note: patented: the first practical automatic refrigeration system for long-haul trucks in 1940, an invention that led Minneapolis entrepreneur Joseph Numero to found the Thermo Control Company (now Thermo King) in 1938 around Jones's engineering. Cited from mnhs.org.

became: the first African American elected to the American Society of Refrigeration Engineers in 1944, and was posthumously awarded the National Medal of Technology in 1991. Cited from aaregistry.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Lewis Latimer, self-taught son of formerly enslaved parents, patented an improved method of manufacturing durable carbon filaments for incandescent light bulbs in 1882, helping make electric lighting affordable. In 1918 he became the first person of color admitted to the Edison Pioneers, the group of engineers who had worked directly with Thomas Edison.

notabilityBasis.note: patented: an improved process for manufacturing carbon filaments on January 17, 1882, which reduced breakage and made incandescent light bulbs more durable and affordable to produce at scale. Cited from uspto.gov.
documented by: National Park Service holds archival materials documenting Lewis Latimer, corroborating biographical and career records cited in this catalog entry. Cited from nps.gov.
became: the first person of color admitted to the Edison Pioneers on February 11, 1918, the honorary society of engineers who worked directly with Thomas Edison. Cited from invent.org.

docs.summary: Granville Woods, known as "Black Edison," patented the multiplex telegraph (also called the induction telegraph) in 1887 while running his own machine shop in Cincinnati, allowing moving trains to communicate by voice over telegraph wires and helping prevent collisions. He successfully defended his patent against an infringement lawsuit from Thomas Edison.

notabilityBasis.note: patented: the multiplex telegraph, or induction telegraph, in 1887, a device that let people on moving trains communicate by voice over telegraph wires, helping prevent train accidents. Cited from blackpast.org.
defeated: a patent infringement lawsuit brought by Thomas Edison over the multiplex telegraph, then declined Edison's offer to become a business partner. Cited from invent.org.

docs.summary: Elijah McCoy, a mechanical engineer trained in Scotland who could only find work as a railroad fireman in the segregated United States, patented an automatic lubricating cup for steam engines in 1872 that distributed oil continuously without stopping the machine. Railroad engineers came to insist on "the real McCoy" over imitators, and he held more than 57 patents in his lifetime.

notabilityBasis.note: patented: an automatic lubricating cup for steam engines in 1872, his 'Improvement in Lubricators for Steam-Engines,' which distributed oil evenly over moving parts while the engine remained in motion. Cited from nps.gov.
inspired: the phrase 'the real McCoy,' as railroad engineers specifically requested his superior lubricator over competing imitations. Cited from invent.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Sarah Boone, a dressmaker who had escaped slavery in North Carolina and settled in New Haven, Connecticut, patented an improved ironing board on April 26, 1892 — a narrow, curved, reversible board designed to press sleeves and fitted garment seams. She was the first Black woman in Connecticut, and only the fourth known Black woman in the U.S., to hold a patent.

notabilityBasis.note: patented: an improved ironing board on April 26, 1892, featuring a narrow, curved, reversible design for pressing sleeves and fitted seams on women's and men's garments. Cited from connecticuthistory.org.
became: the fourth known Black woman in the United States to hold a patent, after settling with her family in a New Haven neighborhood near Yale University. Cited from blackpast.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Marie Van Brittan Brown, a nurse in Jamaica, Queens who often worked irregular hours in a neighborhood with slow police response, co-invented an audio-visual home security system with her husband Albert, an electronics technician. Their 1969 patent for a peephole camera, monitor, and remote door lock is widely credited as a forerunner of modern home security and CCTV systems.

notabilityBasis.note: received: U.S. Patent No. 3,482,037 in 1969 for a 'Home Security System Utilizing Television Surveillance,' featuring a sliding camera, multiple peepholes, a monitor, and remote door-unlock capability. Cited from smithsonianmag.com.

influenced: the later development of modern closed-circuit television and home security systems, and Brown was later honored with a National Scientists Committee award for her work. Cited from lemelson.mit.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: George Washington Carver, born into slavery near Diamond, Missouri, became Tuskegee Institute's leading agricultural scientist, developing more than 300 uses for the peanut and 100 for the sweet potato to help Southern farmers diversify away from cotton monoculture. The 1943 George Washington Carver National Monument was the first U.S. national memorial to an African American.

notabilityBasis.note: developed: more than 300 documented uses for the peanut and 100 for the sweet potato while researching crop rotation and soil-depleting cotton alternatives for Southern farmers at Tuskegee Institute after 1896. Cited from nps.gov.
documented by: Library of Congress holds archival materials documenting George Washington Carver, corroborating biographical and career records cited in this catalog entry. Cited from loc.gov.

honored by: the George Washington Carver National Monument, established July 14, 1943 near his birthplace on legislation sponsored by Senator Harry S. Truman, the first U.S. national monument dedicated to an African American. Cited from shsmo.org.

docs.summary: Otis Boykin, a Chicago engineer who patented as many as 26 devices, developed a low-cost, high-precision wire resistor in 1959 that could withstand extreme temperature and pressure changes. Versions of his resistor technology were used in guided missiles, televisions, and a control unit for the artificial cardiac pacemaker that helped regulate heartbeats.

notabilityBasis.note: patented: a wire precision resistor in 1959 and a more advanced, temperature- and pressure-resistant version soon after, technology later used in radios, televisions, guided missiles, and pacemaker control units. Cited from britannica.com.
inducted into: the National Inventors Hall of Fame in 2014, posthumously recognizing his contributions to resistor technology and pacemaker components. Cited from invent.org.

docs.summary: Valerie Thomas, a NASA physicist who managed image-processing systems for the pioneering Landsat Earth-observation satellites, patented an illusion transmitter in 1980 — a device using concave mirrors to produce three-dimensional optical illusions — technology NASA later adapted and which influenced surgical imaging and video display research.

notabilityBasis.note: patented: an illusion transmitter in 1980, a device using two concave mirrors to project three-dimensional optical illusion images, technology later adapted by NASA and studied for use in surgery and video display. Cited from britannica.com.

managed: development of the image-processing systems for Landsat, the first Earth-observation satellite program to transmit images from space, during her NASA career at Goddard Space Flight Center. Cited from nasa.gov.

docs.summary: Mark Dean co-invented the IBM Personal Computer in 1981 while working at IBM's Boca Raton, Florida development lab, and holds three of IBM's original nine PC patents, including the ISA bus architecture that let printers, monitors, and other peripherals connect to computers. In 1995 he became the first African American named an IBM Fellow, the company's highest technical honor.

notabilityBasis.note: became: the first African American named an IBM Fellow, the company's highest technical distinction, in 1995. Cited from floridainvents.org.

co invented: the Industry Standard Architecture (ISA) bus with Dennis Moeller, patented in 1985, which allowed peripheral devices to connect to the IBM Personal Computer's motherboard and became a National Inventors Hall of Fame-recognized invention. Cited from ibm.com.

The entity received a major, named national or field-defining honor or hall-of-fame induction (e.g. Congressional Gold Medal, a national Hall of Fame, a Pulitzer, a National Medal). Local or purely commercial awards do not qualify alone.

docs.summary: Marian Croak spent three decades at Bell Labs and AT&T developing Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) technology, filing more than 200 patents including foundational methods for monitoring call quality over internet networks. In 2022 she became one of the first two Black women inducted into the National Inventors Hall of Fame.

notabilityBasis.note: patented: foundational Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) technology, including U.S. Patent No. 7,599,359 for monitoring end-to-end network performance, among more than 200 patents filed during her career at Bell Labs and AT&T. Cited from invent.org.
became: one of the first two Black women inducted into the National Inventors Hall of Fame in 2022. Cited from aaregistry.org.

docs.summary: Jane Cooke Wright pioneered the use of human tissue culture, rather than laboratory mice, to test cancer drugs and helped introduce chemotherapy combinations including nitrogen mustard and methotrexate into standard cancer treatment. In 1967 she became an associate dean of New York Medical College, the highest-ranking Black woman at a U.S. medical institution at the time.

notabilityBasis.note: pioneered: the technique of using human tissue culture, instead of laboratory mice, to test the effects of potential chemotherapy drugs on cancer cells, advancing multi-drug treatment regimens over her 40-year career. Cited from nlm.nih.gov.

became: an associate dean of New York Medical College in 1967, the highest-ranking African American woman at a U.S. medical institution at that time, and co-founded the American Society of Clinical Oncology in 1964. Cited from womenshistory.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Norbert Rillieux, a free man of color trained in engineering in Paris, returned to New Orleans in 1840 and patented the multiple-effect vacuum evaporator in 1843 and 1846, a system that reused heat across stages to refine sugar faster and cheaper with far less fuel. The process became the foundation of modern industrial evaporation.

notabilityBasis.note: patented: the multiple-effect vacuum evaporator in 1843 and 1846 while chief engineer at a New Orleans sugar refinery, a system that reused latent heat across evaporation stages to refine sugar more cheaply and efficiently. Cited from acs.org.

endured: discriminatory treatment despite his achievements, including a patent application authorities initially challenged over his race, and exclusion from planters' homes even as he worked at their refineries. Cited from hnoc.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Thomas L. Jennings, a free-born New York City tailor, patented a clothes-cleaning process he called "dry scouring" on March 3, 1821, becoming the first African American to hold a U.S. patent. He used his resulting fortune to fund the abolitionist movement and later organized the legal fight after his daughter Elizabeth was forcibly removed from a New York streetcar in 1854.

notabilityBasis.note: patented: a garment-cleaning process called 'dry scouring' on March 3, 1821, becoming the first African American to be granted a U.S. patent, signed by then-Secretary of State John Quincy Adams. Cited from smithsonianmag.com.

organized: the legal defense after his daughter Elizabeth Jennings was forcibly removed from a whites-only New York City streetcar in 1854, a case argued in part by future president Chester Arthur that helped desegregate the city's streetcars by 1859. Cited from blackpast.org.

docs.summary: Sarah E. Goode, born into slavery and later a Chicago furniture store owner, received a U.S. patent on July 14, 1885 for a folding cabinet bed that converted into a desk, designed for customers in small apartments. She is widely credited as the first African American woman to receive a U.S. patent.

notabilityBasis.note: patented: a folding cabinet bed on July 14, 1885, which converted into a roll-top desk with storage compartments, designed for customers in small working-class apartments. Cited from blackpast.org.

credited as: the first African American woman to receive a U.S. patent, according to the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office's historical record, preceding the better-known Murphy bed by roughly 25 years. Cited from uchicago.edu.
