The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: James West and fellow Bell Labs scientist Gerhard Sessler patented the foil electret microphone in 1962, a design so efficient and inexpensive that it is used in roughly 90 percent of the two billion microphones manufactured each year, from telephones to hearing aids. West later became a research professor at Johns Hopkins University after more than four decades at Bell Labs.

notabilityBasis.note: co patented: the foil electret microphone with Gerhard Sessler in 1962 at Bell Labs, a design used in an estimated 90 percent of the two billion microphones produced annually today. Cited from invent.org.
became: a research professor at Johns Hopkins University's Whiting School of Engineering after retiring from Bell Labs in 2001, following more than four decades at the company. Cited from jhu.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: George Robert Carruthers, a physicist at the U.S. Naval Research Laboratory, designed the Far Ultraviolet Camera/Spectrograph that Apollo 16 astronauts set up on the Moon in April 1972 — the first telescope operated from the surface of another world. The instrument, based on Carruthers's patented image converter, captured the first global images of Earth's outer atmosphere from space.

notabilityBasis.note: designed: the Far Ultraviolet Camera/Spectrograph deployed by Apollo 16 astronauts on the Moon on April 20, 1972, the first telescope operated on the surface of another celestial body, which captured over 200 images and spectra of Earth's atmosphere and deep space. Cited from nasa.gov.

patented: an image converter for detecting electromagnetic radiation in short wavelengths in 1969, the technology underlying his ultraviolet cameras, and was inducted into the National Inventors Hall of Fame in 2003. Cited from invent.org.

The entity received a major, named national or field-defining honor or hall-of-fame induction (e.g. Congressional Gold Medal, a national Hall of Fame, a Pulitzer, a National Medal). Local or purely commercial awards do not qualify alone.

docs.summary: Emmett Chappelle, a NASA exobiologist who worked on instruments for the Viking Mars landers, discovered how to harness the firefly's luciferin-luciferase bioluminescence reaction as a rapid method of detecting bacteria in blood, water, and food. He held more than 14 U.S. patents and was inducted into the National Inventors Hall of Fame in 2007.

notabilityBasis.note: developed: a rapid bacteria-detection method using the firefly's luciferin-luciferase bioluminescence reaction with adenosine triphosphate (ATP), applied to testing blood, spinal fluid, drinking water, and food for contamination. Cited from invent.org.

worked: as a NASA exobiologist and astrochemist starting in 1966, helping develop instruments for the Viking Mars lander program to collect and analyze Martian soil samples. Cited from afroscience.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Solomon Carter Fuller became the first Black psychiatrist in the United States in 1897 after earning his medical degree from Boston University. Selected as one of five research assistants under Dr. Alois Alzheimer in Munich, Fuller published the first comprehensive English-language review of Alzheimer's disease in 1912 and became a Boston University associate professor of pathology and neurology.

notabilityBasis.note: became: the first Black psychiatrist in the United States in 1897, after earning his medical degree from Boston University School of Medicine. Cited from mayoclinic.org.

published: the first comprehensive English-language review of Alzheimer's disease in 1912, based on his work as one of five researchers Alois Alzheimer selected for his Munich laboratory, documenting one of the earliest known cases of the disease. Cited from blackpast.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: William Augustus Hinton developed the Hinton test, a highly accurate blood-serum diagnostic for syphilis adopted by the U.S. Public Health Service, and in 1936 became the first Black person to publish a medical textbook, "Syphilis and Its Treatment." In 1949 he became Harvard Medical School's first Black tenured professor.

notabilityBasis.note: developed: the Hinton test, a highly accurate blood-serum diagnostic test for syphilis that the U.S. Public Health Service adopted for its accuracy. Cited from blackpast.org.

became: Harvard Medical School's first Black tenured professor in 1949, after three decades teaching bacteriology and immunology there while directing Massachusetts's Wassermann Laboratory. Cited from thecrimson.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Charles Henry Turner, the first African American to earn a PhD from the University of Chicago (1907), conducted pioneering experiments proving insects can hear, learn, and modify behavior. Barred by racism from university faculty positions despite authoring more than 70 papers, he taught for 14 years at Sumner High School in St. Louis, the first Black public high school west of the Mississippi.

notabilityBasis.note: became: the first African American to earn a PhD from the University of Chicago, in zoology, in 1907, after experiments demonstrating that insects can hear and distinguish airborne sounds. Cited from britannica.com.

taught: science for 14 years at Sumner High School in St. Louis, from 1908 until his 1922 retirement, while continuing research that made him the first African American published in the journal Science. Cited from blackpast.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: David E. Harris, an Ohio State University graduate and Air Force B-47 and B-52 pilot, was hired by American Airlines on December 3, 1964, becoming the first African American pilot for a major U.S. commercial airline. He flew for American for 30 years, retiring in 1994 as a captain on the Boeing 747.

notabilityBasis.note: flew: for American Airlines for 30 years, becoming a captain within three years of hire and retiring in 1994 after piloting aircraft including the Boeing 747. Cited from npr.org.

became: the first African American hired as a pilot by a major U.S. commercial airline when American Airlines hired him on December 3, 1964, two days after he left the U.S. Air Force. Cited from si.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Jill Elaine Brown, a Baltimore-born pilot who built flight hours flying for a small Black-owned commuter carrier, was hired by Texas International Airlines in 1978, becoming the first African American woman pilot for a major U.S. airline.

notabilityBasis.note: built: her flight hours working for Wheeler Airlines, a small Black-owned commuter carrier, after leaving the U.S. Navy's flight training program, before qualifying for a major-airline pilot position. Cited from blackhistory.com.

became: the first African American woman pilot for a major U.S. airline when Texas International Airlines hired her in 1978, one of six women in a training class of 38 pilots. Cited from blackpast.org.

docs.summary: Founded in 1794 by Richard Allen on land he purchased in 1791 — the oldest parcel in the United States continuously owned by Black Americans. The African Methodist Episcopal denomination was organized here in 1816, and the present 1890 sanctuary still seats an active congregation.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1794. Cited from Mother Bethel AME Church (official site).

documented by: National Park Service documents Mother Bethel AME Church in federal historic-place and archival records, providing independent authority corroboration for this settlement's documented history. Cited from nps.gov.

denomination organized: 1816 — the African Methodist Episcopal Church, the first independent Black denomination in the U.S., was organized at Mother Bethel. Cited from Encyclopedia of Greater Philadelphia.

docs.displayName: Mother African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church
docs.nameLower: mother african methodist episcopal zion church
docs.summary: Organized in 1796 by Black Methodists who left the segregated John Street Church, Mother Zion is New York City's oldest Black congregation and the mother church of the AME Zion denomination. Called the 'Freedom Church' for its Underground Railroad work, it built its present West 137th Street sanctuary in 1923-25 to a design by Black architect George W. Foster Jr.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1796. Cited from Mother AME Zion Church (official site).
underground railroad role: Known as the 'Freedom Church' for its central role in the Underground Railroad. Cited from BlackPast.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Organized in 1774 from a body of Black Baptists worshipping in Prince George County since the 1750s, First Baptist is among the oldest Black congregations in North America. Settled on Harrison Street in Petersburg by 1820, it ran one of the nation's earliest schools for Black children and remained an organizing base into the civil rights era.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1774. Cited from BlackPast.org.
education role: Established one of the first local schools for Black children in the United States. Cited from WTVR CBS 6 Richmond.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Tracing its organization to 1773 under Rev. George Leile at Brampton Plantation, First African Baptist is among the oldest Black congregations in North America. Its Franklin Square sanctuary, completed in 1859, was built of Savannah Grey brick by congregants both free and enslaved.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1773. Cited from First African Baptist Church (official site).
building completed: 1859. Cited from Georgia Historical Society.

documented by: National Park Service documents First African Baptist Church in federal historic-place and archival records, providing independent authority corroboration for this settlement's documented history. Cited from nps.gov; loc.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Built in 1806 by Boston's free Black community on the north slope of Beacon Hill, the African Meeting House is the oldest surviving Black church building in the United States. It housed a church and school and hosted the founding of the New England Anti-Slavery Society in 1832; Frederick Douglass and Maria Stewart spoke from its pulpit.

notabilityBasis.note: built year: 1806. Cited from National Park Service.

docs.displayName: Emanuel African Methodist Episcopal Church (Mother Emanuel)
docs.nameLower: emanuel african methodist episcopal church (mother emanuel)
docs.summary: Founded in 1816 by free and enslaved Black Charlestonians led by Morris Brown, Emanuel is the oldest AME congregation in the South. Forced underground when Black churches were outlawed in 1834, it reorganized openly in 1865 and raised its Gothic Revival sanctuary on Calhoun Street in 1891, anchoring over two centuries of worship and civic leadership.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1816. Cited from National Park Service.

attack 2015: On June 17, 2015, nine members, including pastor Rev. Clementa Pinckney, were killed in a racist attack during Bible study; the congregation continues to worship and lead. Cited from Emanuel Nine Memorial.

docs.summary: Organized in 1873 as the First Colored Baptist Church of Birmingham, the congregation built its landmark sanctuary in 1909-11 to a design by Black architect Wallace A. Rayfield. notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1873. Cited from National Park Service.

attack 1963: On September 15, 1963, a Ku Klux Klan bombing at the church killed Addie Mae Collins, Denise McNair, Carole Robertson, and Cynthia Wesley; the congregation rebuilt and remains active. Cited from National Trust for Historic Preservation.

docs.summary: Founded in 1886 by Rev. John A. Parker with eight members, Ebenezer built its Auburn Avenue heritage sanctuary in 1922. docs.displayName: Dexter Avenue King Memorial Baptist Church
docs.nameLower: dexter avenue king memorial baptist church
docs.summary: Organized in 1877 by formerly enslaved Montgomerians, the congregation completed its brick church a block from the Alabama Capitol in 1889. Martin Luther King Jr. served as pastor from 1954 to 1960, and the Montgomery bus boycott was coordinated from its basement beginning in December 1955; it was named a National Historic Landmark in 1974.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1877. Cited from National Park Service.
civil rights role: Organizing center of the 1955-56 Montgomery bus boycott under its pastor, Martin Luther King Jr.. Cited from Library of Congress (HABS).

docs.summary: Founded in 1866, Brown Chapel raised its twin-towered Romanesque Revival sanctuary in 1908 under Black builder A. J. Farley. civil rights role: Starting point and headquarters of the 1965 Selma-to-Montgomery voting rights marches. Cited from National Park Service — Selma to Montgomery NHT.

docs.summary: Chicago's first Black congregation began as an 1844 prayer group and organized in 1847 as Quinn Chapel AME. It sheltered freedom seekers on the Underground Railroad before the Civil War, lost its first building to the 1871 fire, and completed its present Wabash Avenue sanctuary in 1892; it was listed on the National Register in 1979.

docs.summary: Completed in the early 1890s for Second Presbyterian Church and purchased by an AME congregation in 1949, Clayborn Temple anchored the 1968 Memphis sanitation workers' strike: the 'I AM A MAN' placards were printed there and daily marches to City Hall left from its doors. docs.summary: Opened in 1853 when Savannah reserved land at Laurel Grove for African American burials, this 90-acre municipal cemetery replaced older Black graveyards displaced by the city's expanding square plan. More free African Americans were interred here during slavery than at any other cemetery in the Southeast, and its stones record pastors, educators, and civil-rights figures across two centuries.

notabilityBasis.note: established: In 1853 the city reserved four acres in the new Laurel Grove Cemetery for Savannah's African American community, replacing an earlier Black burial ground near Whitefield Square; the section grew to roughly 90 acres and remains in active use. listed on national register: Laurel Grove South is documented in the National Register of Historic Places as a significant record of Black social history in Savannah, preserving tombstone evidence for many of the city's most important African American leaders. docs.summary: Founded in 1891 by Richmond's Evergreen Cemetery Association, this 59-acre African American burial ground was designed as a Jim Crow-era counterpart to white-only Hollywood Cemetery. notabilityBasis.note: founded: The Evergreen Cemetery Association opened the 59.2-acre burial ground in 1891–1892 as a premier African American cemetery during segregation; the firm James T. docs.summary: Organized in 1897 by Richmond's East End Memorial Burial Association, this 16-acre African American cemetery straddles the city–Henrico County line beside Evergreen Cemetery. notabilityBasis.note: founded: East End Cemetery was founded in 1897 by the East End Memorial Burial Association after white cemeteries in Virginia remained segregated; the 16-acre grounds straddle Henrico County and the City of Richmond and remained active into the early 2000s. documented burial count: The cemetery spans more than 20 acres and serves as the final resting place of more than 17,500 African Americans, including Richmond business owners, doctors, lawyers, educators, and community leaders. Cited from City of Richmond.

docs.summary: Tracing its roots to the 1790s and housed since 1856 in an Italianate brick sanctuary on East Short Street, First African Baptist is the oldest Black Baptist church in Kentucky and among the oldest in the United States west of the Allegheanies. notabilityBasis.note: constructed: The Italianate church on East Short Street was built in 1856; by 1861 the congregation numbered more than 2,200 members, making it the largest church in Kentucky at that time. Cited from National Park Service — NPGallery.

docs.summary: Over Easter weekend, April 1960, Ella Baker convened 126 student sit-in leaders from across the South at Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina, where they formed SNCC as an independent organization rather than a youth wing of an existing group.

notabilityBasis.note: founded on: April 1960, at a conference of 126 student delegates from 58 sit-in centers held at Shaw University over Easter weekend. Cited from snccdigital.org.

docs.summary: Huey P. Newton and Bobby Seale, who had met as students at Merritt College, founded the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense in Oakland in October 1966, drafting a Ten-Point Program demanding land, jobs, housing, education, and an end to police brutality.

notabilityBasis.note: founded on: October 1966 in Oakland, California, by Huey P. Newton and Bobby Seale. Cited from archives.gov.

docs.displayName: Martin Luther King Jr. Birth Home

notabilityBasis.note: birthplace of: Martin Luther King Jr., born January 15, 1929, at 501 Auburn Avenue in his maternal grandparents' home. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: This Montgomery house served as the King family's parsonage from 1954 to 1960 while King pastored Dexter Avenue Baptist Church. A bomb exploded on its front porch on January 30, 1956, during the Montgomery Bus Boycott, while Coretta Scott King and their infant daughter were inside.

notabilityBasis.note: served as: the parsonage of Dexter Avenue Baptist Church, home to Martin Luther King Jr.'s family from 1954 until 1960. Cited from aaacrhsc.org.

docs.summary: Dedicated October 16, 2011, on the National Mall's Tidal Basin, this memorial depicts King emerging from a 'Stone of Hope' carved from a 'Mountain of Despair,' a reference to his 'I Have a Dream' speech. It is the first National Mall memorial honoring an African American and a non-president.

notabilityBasis.note: dedicated on: October 16, 2011, after the original August 28, 2011 dedication date (the 48th anniversary of the March on Washington) was postponed by a hurricane. Cited from nps.gov.
significant as: the first memorial on the National Mall honoring an African American individual and the first honoring a citizen activist rather than a president or war hero. Cited from nps.gov.

notabilityBasis.note: birthplace of: Malcolm Little (Malcolm X), born May 19, 1925, whose family lived at 3448 Pinkney Street before Klan threats forced them to leave Omaha in 1926. Cited from nps.gov.

notabilityBasis.note: housed: Malcolm X during his later teenage years after his half-sister Ella Little-Collins, who purchased the house in 1941, took him in following his mother's institutionalization. Cited from boston.gov.

docs.summary: Originally Crane Junior College and later Theodor Herzl Junior College, this City Colleges of Chicago campus was renamed Malcolm X College in 1969 at the request of the local West Side community, becoming one of the first American institutions of higher learning named for the civil rights leader.

notabilityBasis.note: renamed in: 1969, when the City Colleges of Chicago renamed the school for Malcolm X at the request of the surrounding community, on its move to a new Near West Side campus. Cited from ccc.edu.

docs.summary: The Little family's home on Lansing's northwest side was burned down by the white-supremacist Black Legion on November 7, 1929, an attack the four-year-old Malcolm X later described vividly in his autobiography. A Michigan historical marker now stands near the site.

notabilityBasis.note: destroyed on: November 7, 1929, when the Little family home was burned to the ground, an arson attack local historians attribute to the white-supremacist Black Legion. Cited from lansingcitypulse.com.
commemorated by: a Michigan historical marker near the original home site, first erected in 1975 and replaced after vandalism, most recently in 2022. Cited from lansingcitypulse.com.

docs.summary: Trained in nonviolent resistance by Rev. James Lawson at workshops beginning in 1959, Nashville college students led sit-ins at downtown lunch counters from February to May 1960 under Diane Nash's leadership, achieving one of the movement's earliest lunch-counter desegregation victories.

notabilityBasis.note: conducted sit ins: from February 13 to May 10, 1960, targeting segregated lunch counters at downtown Nashville stores. Cited from blackpast.org.

docs.summary: Formed in November 1961 by local Black leaders, NAACP members, and SNCC organizers Charles Sherrod and Cordell Reagon, the Albany Movement was the first mass movement of the modern civil rights era aimed at desegregating an entire community, jailing more than 1,000 protesters by 1962.

notabilityBasis.note: formed in: November 1961 in Albany, Georgia, by local Black leaders and ministers together with SNCC and NAACP members, aiming to desegregate the entire city. Cited from blackpast.org.

resulted in: the jailing of more than 1,000 African Americans in Albany and surrounding counties by summer 1962, though the city removed its segregation ordinances only in March 1963. Cited from kinginstitute.stanford.edu.

docs.summary: In spring 1964, as St. Augustine, Florida prepared to mark its 400th anniversary, SCLC joined a local movement led by Dr. Robert Hayling demanding desegregation. King's June 11 arrest at the Monson Motor Lodge and the photographed acid-pool attack on swimmers helped push the Civil Rights Act through Congress.

notabilityBasis.note: arrested at: the Monson Motor Lodge on June 11, 1964, when Martin Luther King Jr. was arrested attempting to be served at the restaurant — his only arrest in Florida. Cited from kinginstitute.stanford.edu.

captured in photos: images of the Monson's owner pouring acid into the whites-only pool on June 18, 1964, after Black and white activists jumped in together in protest, broadcast worldwide. Cited from civilrightstrail.com.

docs.summary: Led by Gloria Richardson and the Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee from 1961 to 1964, this Eastern Shore Maryland movement combined sit-ins and voter registration with armed community self-defense, prompting the state's longest period of martial law since 1877 before the 1963 Treaty of Cambridge.

notabilityBasis.note: led by: Gloria Richardson, who organized and led the Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee's sit-ins and voter-registration drives beginning in 1962. Cited from archives.gov.

resolved by: the July 1963 'Treaty of Cambridge,' negotiated after Governor Millard Tawes declared martial law and sent in the National Guard, proposing to desegregate public facilities and create a human rights committee. Cited from loc.gov.

docs.summary: Organized in spring 1963 by local ministers as the Danville Christian Progressive Association, this Virginia campaign against segregation culminated on June 10, 1963 — 'Bloody Monday' — when police and deputized city workers beat and fire-hosed demonstrators outside the jail, injuring dozens.

notabilityBasis.note: occurred on: June 10, 1963, when police and deputized city workers used fire hoses and nightsticks on a prayer vigil outside the Danville jail, injuring about 47 demonstrators and arresting 60. Cited from cardinalnews.org.

organized by: the Danville Christian Progressive Association, led by ministers Lawrence Campbell and Alexander Dunlap, following a 1960 sit-in at the Danville Public Library. Cited from uncommonwealth.lva.virginia.gov.

docs.summary: Led by Martin Luther King Jr., James Bevel, and Al Raby from 1965 to 1966, this open-housing campaign brought 35,000 people to a Soldier Field rally and, after a mob of thousands attacked marchers at Marquette Park on August 5, 1966, produced a Summit Agreement that helped inspire the 1968 Fair Housing Act.

notabilityBasis.note: attacked at: Marquette Park on August 5, 1966, when a white mob of over 10,000 met open-housing marchers with rocks and violence, striking King himself. Cited from npr.org.

docs.summary: James Meredith set out to walk alone from Memphis to Jackson, Mississippi on June 5, 1966, to challenge fear of Black voter registration; shot by a sniper the next day, he was succeeded by SNCC, SCLC, and CORE marchers, and in Greenwood on June 16 Stokely Carmichael delivered his galvanizing 'Black Power' speech.

notabilityBasis.note: began on: June 5, 1966, when James Meredith set out to walk from Memphis, Tennessee to Jackson, Mississippi to encourage Black voter registration, before being shot and wounded by a sniper on June 6. Cited from blackpast.org.

delivered at: Stokely Carmichael's 'Black Power' speech to a rally of about 1,500 people in Greenwood, Mississippi on June 16, 1966, widely seen as launching the Black Power era. Cited from archives.gov.

docs.displayName: Poor People's Campaign and Resurrection City
docs.nameLower: poor people's campaign and resurrection city
docs.summary: Organized by SCLC under Ralph Abernathy after Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination, this 1968 campaign for economic justice built a 3,000-person encampment called Resurrection City on the National Mall, occupying 16 acres between the Lincoln Memorial and Washington Monument for 42 days.

notabilityBasis.note: occupied: 16 acres of the National Mall with a 3,000-resident encampment called Resurrection City, complete with its own zip code, dining tent, and city hall. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: On February 8, 1968, highway patrolmen opened fire on Black students protesting segregation at a local bowling alley on the campus of South Carolina State College, killing Samuel Hammond, Henry Smith, and Delano Middleton and wounding twenty-eight others — the first fatal police shooting of student protesters on a U.S. campus.

notabilityBasis.note: occurred on: February 8, 1968, when nine state troopers and one city officer fired on student protesters at South Carolina State College, killing three and wounding twenty-eight. Cited from blackpast.org.

killed: Samuel Hammond (18), Henry Smith (18), and Delano Middleton (17), a local high school student, while federal charges against the officers later ended in acquittal. Cited from smithsonianmag.com.

docs.summary: Formed in 1965 by local Black residents under John Hulett and SNCC organizer Stokely Carmichael, the Lowndes County Freedom Organization ran independent Black candidates under a black panther emblem in a county nicknamed 'Bloody Lowndes' for its history of racial terror, directly inspiring the Black Panther Party's name.

notabilityBasis.note: founded in: 1965 in Lowndes County, Alabama, by local Black residents led by John Hulett together with SNCC staff under Stokely Carmichael, after the Selma to Montgomery march passed through the county. Cited from encyclopediaofalabama.org.

adopted symbol: a black panther as its ballot emblem, in contrast to the Alabama Democratic Party's white rooster, a symbol that directly inspired Huey Newton and Bobby Seale's naming of the Black Panther Party. Cited from blackpast.org.

docs.displayName: Freedom Summer and the Burning of Mount Zion Church
docs.nameLower: freedom summer and the burning of mount zion church
docs.summary: Mount Zion United Methodist Church in rural Longdale, Mississippi, agreed to host a Freedom Summer Freedom School in 1964; Klansmen beat congregants and burned the church on June 16, and when civil rights workers Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner came to investigate on June 21, they were murdered nearby.

notabilityBasis.note: burned on: June 16, 1964, when Klansmen assaulted Mt. Zion congregants leaving a meeting and later burned the church, which had agreed to host a Freedom Summer Freedom School. Cited from sah-archipedia.org.

linked to: the June 21, 1964 murders of civil rights workers James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner, who were killed after visiting the burned church site to investigate. Cited from mississippiencyclopedia.org.

docs.displayName: Integration of the University of Mississippi
docs.nameLower: integration of the university of mississippi
docs.summary: James Meredith's court-ordered enrollment triggered the Ole Miss riot of September 30-October 1, 1962, when a mob of roughly 2,500 segregationists battled federal marshals near the Lyceum, killing two civilians before 30,000 federal troops restored order and Meredith registered as the university's first Black student.

notabilityBasis.note: occurred on: the night of September 30-October 1, 1962, when a mob of about 2,500 attacked federal marshals guarding James Meredith near the Lyceum, killing French journalist Paul Guihard and local resident Ray Gunter. Cited from history.com.

resulted in: James Meredith's registration on October 1, 1962, as the first African American student at the University of Mississippi, after President Kennedy mobilized more than 30,000 troops. Cited from usmarshals.gov.

docs.summary: Autherine Lucy became the University of Alabama's first Black student on February 3, 1956, but after mobs rioted against her for three days, the university suspended and then expelled her, a wrong it did not formally reverse until 1988, allowing her to complete her degree in 1992.

notabilityBasis.note: readmitted in: 1988, when the university's board voted to lift her expulsion; she returned to complete her degree, graduating in 1992 alongside her daughter. Cited from news.ua.edu.

docs.summary: Federal judge W.A. Bootle ordered the University of Georgia to admit Charlayne Hunter and Hamilton Holmes on January 6, 1961; after they registered January 9, a rioting mob smashed windows at Hunter's dormitory on January 11, but both students returned days later under a new court order and graduated.

docs.summary: Built in 1954 by Black businessman A.G. Gaston, this Birmingham motel served as campaign headquarters for the 1963 Birmingham movement, with King, Abernathy, and Shuttlesworth strategizing from Room 30's 'War Room' until a Klan bombing struck the motel the night after the campaign's truce was announced.

notabilityBasis.note: served as: the Birmingham campaign's headquarters, where King, Ralph Abernathy, and Fred Shuttlesworth planned strategy from Room 30, known as the 'War Room,' from April through May 1963. Cited from nps.gov.

bombed on: May 11, 1963, the day after the truce ending the Birmingham campaign was announced at the motel, in a bombing believed carried out by Klan members cooperating with Birmingham police. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Formed the evening of December 5, 1955 at a mass meeting of thousands at Holt Street Baptist Church, the Montgomery Improvement Association elected 26-year-old Martin Luther King Jr. as its president and organized the carpool system that sustained the 381-day Montgomery Bus Boycott.

founded: the Organization of Afro-American Unity in June 1964, after leaving the Nation of Islam and completing his hajj to Mecca as El-Hajj Malik El-Shabazz, giving the OAAU's first public address at the Audubon Ballroom on June 28, 1964. Cited from blackpast.org.

docs.summary: A behind-the-scenes strategist for more than five decades, Ella Baker directed NAACP branches nationally, served as SCLC's first executive director, and convened the 1960 Shaw University conference that founded SNCC, all while championing grassroots, group-centered leadership over charismatic figureheads.

notabilityBasis.note: directed: NAACP branch operations nationally as director of branches starting in 1943, becoming the organization's highest-ranking woman. Cited from britannica.com.

docs.summary: An openly gay pacifist and organizing strategist, Bayard Rustin introduced Martin Luther King Jr. to Gandhian nonviolence, helped plan the 1947 Journey of Reconciliation and 1961 Freedom Rides, and served as chief organizer of the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom.

docs.summary: A Mississippi sharecropper turned SNCC organizer, Fannie Lou Hamer survived a brutal jailhouse beating in Winona in 1963, co-founded the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, and delivered nationally televised 1964 Democratic National Convention testimony that exposed the violence behind Black disenfranchisement.

docs.summary: As the 22-year-old chairman of the Nashville Student Movement, Diane Nash led the 1960 sit-ins that made Nashville the first Southern city to desegregate lunch counters, then insisted the Freedom Rides continue after 1961 mob violence, and later co-founded SNCC and helped drive the Selma voting-rights campaign.

notabilityBasis.note: insisted on: continuing the Freedom Rides after the original riders were attacked in Alabama in May 1961, recruiting Fisk and other students to keep the rides going. Cited from britannica.com.
led: the Nashville sit-in campaign as its chairman in 1960, which made Nashville the first major Southern city to desegregate its lunch counters. Cited from blackpast.org.

docs.summary: A founding member and later chairman of SNCC, John Lewis was one of the original 1961 Freedom Riders and, on March 7, 1965, led marchers across the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma, suffering a fractured skull on 'Bloody Sunday' before serving Georgia in Congress for over three decades.

notabilityBasis.note: led: over 600 marchers across the Edmund Pettus Bridge on March 7, 1965, 'Bloody Sunday,' where he suffered a concussion and fractured skull from state troopers' attack. Cited from archives.gov.

docs.summary: Born in Trinidad and raised in the Bronx, Stokely Carmichael organized Mississippi voter registration and the Lowndes County Freedom Organization before becoming SNCC's chairman in 1966, popularizing 'Black Power' during the Meredith March Against Fear and later adopting the name Kwame Ture as a Pan-Africanist organizer in Guinea.

notabilityBasis.note: became chairman of: SNCC in May 1966, replacing John Lewis and signaling the organization's shift away from an emphasis on nonviolence and integration. Cited from britannica.com.

docs.summary: Three marches in March 1965 pressed for Black voting rights in Alabama. docs.displayName: Medgar and Myrlie Evers Home National Monument
docs.nameLower: medgar and myrlie evers home national monument
docs.summary: The ranch house at 2332 Margaret Walker Alexander Drive was home to Mississippi NAACP field secretary Medgar Evers, who organized voter registration and boycotts statewide until his assassination in the driveway on June 12, 1963. The home was designated a National Historic Landmark in 2017 and a national monument in 2020.

notabilityBasis.note: home of: Medgar Evers, Mississippi's first NAACP field secretary, from 1956 until his assassination there on June 12, 1963. Cited from nps.gov.
designated: a National Historic Landmark in 2017 and established as a National Park Service national monument in December 2020. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: At Montgomery's former Greyhound station on South Court Street, Freedom Riders testing interstate bus desegregation were attacked by a mob on May 20, 1961. The riders pressed on, and by November 1961 the Interstate Commerce Commission's desegregation order took effect. The Alabama Historical Commission opened the Freedom Rides Museum in the station in 2011.

notabilityBasis.note: site of: the May 20, 1961 mob attack on Freedom Riders arriving from Birmingham, which prompted federal marshals to be sent to Montgomery. Cited from encyclopediaofalabama.org.
commemorates: the 1961 Freedom Rides, whose continuation led the ICC to enforce desegregated interstate travel effective November 1, 1961. Cited from crmvet.org.

docs.displayName: Robert Russa Moton High School (Moton Museum)
docs.nameLower: robert russa moton high school (moton museum)
docs.summary: On April 23, 1951, sixteen-year-old Barbara Johns led some 450 classmates out of overcrowded Robert Russa Moton High School to demand a school equal to Farmville's white high school. Their strike produced Davis v. County School Board, one of the five cases decided in Brown v. Board of Education. The building is now the Moton Museum.

notabilityBasis.note: site of: the April 23, 1951 student strike led by Barbara Johns against unequal school facilities in Prince Edward County. Cited from motonmuseum.org.
led to: Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County, consolidated into Brown v. Board of Education (1954). Cited from encyclopediavirginia.org.

docs.summary: From July 19 to August 11, 1958, Wichita NAACP Youth Council members led by Ron Walters and Carol Parks sat in daily at the downtown Dockum Drug Store lunch counter at Douglas and Broadway. The chain agreed to serve all customers statewide — a successful student-led sit-in a year and a half before Greensboro.

notabilityBasis.note: began on: July 19, 1958, organized by NAACP Youth Council president Ron Walters and treasurer Carol Parks with attorney Chester I. Lewis. Cited from wichita.edu.
resulted in: the Dockum/Rexall chain agreeing in August 1958 to serve all customers regardless of race across its Kansas stores. Cited from visitwichita.com.

docs.summary: On August 27, 1956, twelve Black students walked down Foley Hill into Clinton High School, making it the first state-supported public high school in the former Confederacy to desegregate. They kept attending through mob harassment and a 1958 bombing of the school; Bobby Cain graduated in May 1957. The Green McAdoo Cultural Center tells their story.

notabilityBasis.note: began on: August 27, 1956, when twelve Black students entered Clinton High School under a federal court desegregation order. Cited from greenmcadooculturalcenter.org.
commemorated by: the Green McAdoo Cultural Center, opened in 2006 in the former segregated Green McAdoo Grammar School with bronze statues of the twelve students. Cited from tnmuseum.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Founded by the American Missionary Association after the Civil War to educate freedpeople, Dorchester Academy's surviving 1934 boys' dormitory housed the SCLC's Citizenship Education Program from 1961 to 1970. There Septima Clark and Dorothy Cotton trained community teachers who prepared thousands of Black Southerners to register to vote.

notabilityBasis.note: headquarters of: the SCLC Citizenship Education Program, 1961-1970, which trained close to 2,000 teachers who in turn taught more than 10,000 others. Cited from nps.gov.
designated: a National Historic Landmark in 2006; the dormitory is the last surviving building of the academy campus. Cited from civilrightstrail.com.

docs.summary: Highlander Folk School near Monteagle trained labor and civil rights organizers across lines of race from 1932 until Tennessee revoked its charter in 1961. Rosa Parks attended a two-week desegregation workshop led by Septima Clark in August 1955, four months before her Montgomery arrest; the citizenship school model begun here spread across the South.

notabilityBasis.note: trained: Rosa Parks at an August 1955 desegregation workshop led by Septima Clark, months before the Montgomery Bus Boycott. Cited from loc.gov.
closed in: 1961, when Tennessee revoked its charter and confiscated the property; the school rechartered as the Highlander Research and Education Center. Cited from kinginstitute.stanford.edu.

docs.displayName: Mason Temple, Church of God in Christ
docs.nameLower: mason temple, church of god in christ
docs.summary: Mason Temple, world headquarters of the Church of God in Christ and one of the largest Black-owned assembly halls in the country when completed in 1945, hosted mass meetings of the 1968 Memphis sanitation workers' strike. Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his final speech, 'I've Been to the Mountaintop,' here on April 3, 1968.

notabilityBasis.note: site of: Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'I've Been to the Mountaintop' speech on April 3, 1968, the night before his assassination. Cited from nps.gov.
built: between 1940 and 1945 as the administrative and spiritual center of the Church of God in Christ. Cited from memphisheritage.org.

docs.summary: Bethel Baptist Church in Collegeville served as headquarters of the Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights from 1956 to 1961 under pastor Fred Shuttlesworth, sustaining Birmingham's desegregation campaign after Alabama outlawed the NAACP. Bombed three times — 1956, 1958, and 1962 — the congregation rebuilt each time. It became a National Historic Landmark in 2005.

notabilityBasis.note: headquarters of: the Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights, 1956-1961, led by pastor Fred Shuttlesworth. Cited from nps.gov.
survived: bombings on December 25, 1956, June 29, 1958, and December 14, 1962, continuing weekly mass meetings throughout. Cited from thehistoricbethel.org.

docs.summary: Ellen F. Eglin (died c. 1915), a Washington, D.C. housekeeper and later Census Office clerk, invented a clothes wringer in the 1880s. In 1888 she sold the rights for $18 rather than patent in her own name, telling The Woman Inventor she feared white customers would refuse a wringer known to come from a Black woman.

notabilityBasis.note: invented: a clothes wringer whose commercial rights she sold to an agent in 1888 for $18, explaining to Charlotte Smith of The Woman Inventor that she feared market rejection if buyers knew a Black woman had patented it. Cited from blackpast.org.

employed as: a charwoman in the U.S. Census Office under the Department of the Interior by 1890, while living with her brother Charles at 1929 11th Street NW in Washington, D.C.. Cited from wikipedia.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Marjorie Stewart Joyner (1896–1994) died in Chicago's Bronzeville after a career as national adviser to Madam C.J. Walker's beauty schools. She patented a permanent-wave machine in 1928 and later helped write Illinois cosmetology law and co-founded national Black beautician associations with Mary McLeod Bethune.

notabilityBasis.note: patented: U.S. Patent 1,693,515 (November 27, 1928) for a permanent-wave machine that used rods on a table apparatus with a scalp protector so curls or straight styles lasted several days. Cited from lemelson.mit.edu.

advised: Madam C.J. Walker's company as national adviser overseeing roughly 200 beauty schools and training thousands of stylists before and alongside her patented wave-machine work. Cited from wikipedia.org.

docs.summary: Christine Darden (b. 1942) joined NASA Langley in 1967 as a human computer, earned a doctorate in engineering, and led sonic-boom research for high-speed flight. She became the first African American woman at Langley promoted into the Senior Executive Service and received the Congressional Gold Medal in 2019.

notabilityBasis.note: led: NASA Langley's sonic boom group within the High Speed Research program, designing wing and nose configurations to reduce sonic-boom noise for proposed high-speed civil transport. Cited from nasa.gov.

promoted to: the Senior Executive Service as the first African American woman at NASA Langley to reach that federal civil-service rank, later directing strategic communication and education before retiring in 2007. Cited from nasa.gov.

docs.summary: Carter G. Woodson (1875–1950) died in 1950 after founding the Association for the Study of Negro Life and History and launching Negro History Week in 1926 — the seed of Black History Month. From his Washington office-home he edited the Journal of Negro History under Jim Crow.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: the Association for the Study of Negro Life and History (now ASALH) and the Journal of Negro History, anchoring professional Black historical scholarship during the Jim Crow era. Cited from nps.gov.
established: Negro History Week in February 1926, later expanded into Black History Month, choosing February to honor the birth months of Abraham Lincoln and Frederick Douglass. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.displayName: Carter G. Woodson Home National Historic Site
docs.nameLower: carter g. woodson home national historic site
docs.summary: The Carter G. Woodson Home National Historic Site protects the Washington, D.C. row house where Dr. Woodson lived and worked while building ASALH and Negro History Week. The National Park Service interprets the office-home as the institutional birthplace of organized African American historical study.

notabilityBasis.note: designated as: Carter G. Woodson Home National Historic Site, a National Park Service unit interpreting Woodson's residence and the early Black history movement. Cited from nps.gov.
located at: 1538 9th Street NW in Washington, D.C., the office-home from which Woodson edited scholarship and organized ASALH programming. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.displayName: Boston African American National Historic Site
docs.nameLower: boston african american national historic site
docs.summary: Boston African American National Historic Site preserves and interprets Beacon Hill's 19th-century free Black community, the Black Heritage Trail, Underground Railroad networks, and the Massachusetts 54th Infantry Regiment story. Ranger-led and self-guided tours connect meeting houses, residences, and schools that anchored abolitionist Boston.

notabilityBasis.note: interprets: the Black Heritage Trail® and Beacon Hill sites documenting Boston's free Black community, Underground Railroad activity, and Civil War-era service including the Massachusetts 54th Infantry Regiment. Cited from nps.gov.
headquartered near: the Museum of African American History campus on Joy Street in Beacon Hill, the primary visitor orientation point for the national historic site partnership. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Designed by J. Edward Storck and opened in 1910 at 620 T Street NW, the Howard Theatre served Black audiences and performers for more than five decades as one of the nation's oldest theaters built expressly for African American entertainment — a peer of the Apollo, the Royal in Baltimore, and Philadelphia's Pearl.

notabilityBasis.note: opened: Architect J. Edward Storck designed and built the Howard Theatre in 1910 at 620 T Street NW, south of Howard University near LeDroit Park, as a theater serving Black audiences and providing a base for Black performers. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

listed on national register: The Howard Theatre was listed in the National Register of Historic Places on February 15, 1974 for its significance in performing arts and Black entertainment history. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

docs.summary: Built in 1912 as the Star Theater at 1615 East 18th Street and renamed the Gem after a 1924 terra cotta renovation, this Kansas City movie and vaudeville house anchored the 18th and Vine district as one of the best-known theaters serving the city's Black community through the jazz age.

notabilityBasis.note: opened as: The Star Theater opened in 1912 at 1615–1617 East 18th Street as a vaudeville and motion picture theater for Black audiences; a 1924 facade remodeling renamed it the Gem Theater. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

hosted performances: During the late 1920s the Gem presented motion pictures, vaudeville, and local jazz orchestras as part of the 18th and Vine district's Black entertainment economy. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

docs.summary: Charles Henry Douglass, Macon's leading Black entrepreneur, opened the present Douglass Theatre in 1921 at 355 Martin Luther King Jr. Boulevard after earlier venues dating to 1912. Affiliated with the Theatre Owners Booking Association, it hosted Bessie Smith, Ma Rainey, Duke Ellington, and Otis Redding before closing in 1973 and reopening after restoration in 1997.

notabilityBasis.note: opened: The New Douglass Theatre opened in 1921 at 355–359 Broadway (now Martin Luther King Jr. Boulevard) with 750–800 seats in a facility designed primarily for motion pictures while continuing live vauvelville and blues performances. Cited from Digital Library of Georgia.

affiliated with: Douglass and later operator Ben Stein were affiliated with the Theatre Owners Booking Association (T.O.B.A.), bringing prominent blues and vaudeville acts to Macon's African American audiences. Cited from Digital Library of Georgia.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Built in 1924 and opened in 1925 at 821 Eighth Avenue, the Liberty Theatre was Columbus, Georgia's first theater built solely for Black patrons and one of the largest movie houses in the city. Columbus native Ma Rainey and other blues and jazz artists performed on its stage until it closed in 1974; it reopened in 1996 as a performing arts cultural center.

notabilityBasis.note: hosted performances: At the height of jazz and blues popularity the Liberty was a major venue for Black audiences in Columbus, with frequent performances by Ma Rainey and other touring artists. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

opened: The Liberty Theatre opened in late April 1925 at 821 Eighth Avenue as Columbus's first black theater, built by the Martin Theater chain with seating for about 600 and a balcony, stage, and projection room. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

docs.summary: Opened in 1935 at 1631 Fourth Avenue North in Birmingham's Fourth Avenue Historic District, the Carver Theatre was a segregated-era movie palace where African Americans could see first-run films. Restored by the city as the Carver Performing Arts Center, it now houses the Alabama Jazz Hall of Fame.

notabilityBasis.note: contributed to district: The Carver Cinema (c. 1941), an Art Moderne motion picture theater built for Black patrons, is a contributing structure in the Fourth Avenue Historic District listed in the National Register on February 11, 1982. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

houses: After the City of Birmingham purchased and restored the theater for live performance use in the 1990s, the Carver became home to the Alabama Jazz Hall of Fame and hosts jazz festivals and touring productions. Cited from Bhamwiki.

The entity received a major, named national or field-defining honor or hall-of-fame induction (e.g. Congressional Gold Medal, a national Hall of Fame, a Pulitzer, a National Medal). Local or purely commercial awards do not qualify alone.

docs.summary: The second-floor Dreamland Ballroom in James Jewell Sr.'s 1923 Jewell Building at 2221 North 24th Street gave Omaha's Near North Side its own jazz venue from the 1920s through the 1960s, hosting Duke Ellington, Count Basie, Louis Armstrong, Nat King Cole, and Dinah Washington when Black audiences were excluded from comparable halls elsewhere in the city.

notabilityBasis.note: opened in: The Jewell Building was erected in 1923 with storefronts on the first floor and the Dreamland Ballroom on the second, providing Omaha's Black community its first dedicated entertainment facility after earlier reliance on rented halls at Krug Park or the Brandeis Theater. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

listed on national register: The Jewell Building (Dreamland Ballroom) was listed in the National Register of Historic Places on July 21, 1983 for its role in Omaha's Black musical and social heritage. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

docs.summary: Opening in 1921 as the Douglass Theatre and reopening by 1925 as the Royal, the Pennsylvania Avenue movie and vaudeville palace ranked with the Apollo, Howard, Regal, and Earl theaters as a premier stage for Black entertainment. Nat King Cole, Billie Holiday, Duke Ellington, and later Motown acts played the Royal until it closed in 1971 and was demolished.

notabilityBasis.note: opened as: The Douglass Theatre opened in 1921 as an African American-owned theater on Pennsylvania Avenue; within four years it reopened under white ownership as the Royal Theatre, the largest and most famous theater along Baltimore's Avenue. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

hosted performances: Top national music entertainers performed at the Royal, including Nat King Cole, Duke Ellington, Louis Armstrong, Billie Holiday, Count Basie, and later the Orioles, Platters, Temptations, and Supremes. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

docs.summary: Jefferson Powell's 1929 Art Deco Ritz Theatre at 829 North Davis Street anchored LaVilla — Jacksonville's Harlem of the South — as a 970-seat movie house for Black audiences through 1972. A 1999 rebuilding preserved the northwest corner marquee and opened the Ritz Theatre and Museum on the historic site.

notabilityBasis.note: opened: Architect Jefferson Powell designed the Art Deco Ritz Theatre, completed in September 1929 at 829 North Davis Street as one of LaVilla's primary motion picture venues for African American audiences. Cited from The Jaxson.

reopened as: After preservation efforts secured River City Renaissance funding, the Ritz Theatre and Museum opened on September 30, 1999, incorporating the original building's northwest corner marquee with a new theater and 11,000-square-foot museum. Cited from Ritz Theatre and Museum.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Opened February 4, 1928 at 4719 South Parkway (now Martin Luther King Jr. Drive) in Bronzeville, the Regal Theater was among the first U.S. entertainment complexes built expressly for Black audiences, seating about 3,000 and employing Black management and staff while hosting Duke Ellington, Ella Fitzgerald, Nat King Cole, and the Jackson Five until closing about 1968 and demolition in 1973.

notabilityBasis.note: hosted performances: The Regal presented films, dance, comedy, and jazz, booking Ray Charles, Ella Fitzgerald, Miles Davis, Nat King Cole, Duke Ellington, and B.B. King for Bronzeville audiences barred from most Loop palaces. Cited from Cinema Treasures.

opened: The National Theater Corporation built the Regal Theater at 4719–27 South Parkway for $1.5 million; it opened February 4, 1928 as one of the first theaters in the country built expressly to cater to the Black community. Cited from City of Chicago — Landmark Designation Report.

docs.summary: Designed by A. Hays Town and opened August 25, 1950 at 1305 Myrtle Walk, the Lincoln Theatre was Baton Rouge's primary movie and entertainment venue for African Americans during segregation, seating about 600 until closing in 1986. Listed in the National Register in 2010, the building is undergoing restoration as a cultural center.

notabilityBasis.note: opened: The Lincoln Theater opened on August 25, 1950 at the corner of Myrtle Walk and Eddie Robinson Street as an important entertainment venue for Baton Rouge's large African American population during the segregation era. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

listed on national register: The Lincoln Theater was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 2010 under Criterion A for its role in Black entertainment and recreation in Baton Rouge from 1950 through 1974. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

docs.summary: Built in 1923 at 1103 Texas Avenue by the Grand Court Order of Calanthe under Cora M. Allen, this four-story Classical Revival temple housed Black professional offices and a rooftop ballroom where Louis Armstrong, Duke Ellington, Count Basie, and Cab Calloway performed for Shreveport audiences barred from downtown white clubs.

notabilityBasis.note: built by: The four-story Classical Revival Calanthean Temple was built in 1923, housing Black professional offices, the Calanthean women's organization, and a rooftop ballroom that became a major social venue. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

hosted performances: The rooftop ballroom hosted packed audiences for Louis Armstrong, Cab Calloway, Duke Ellington, Count Basie, and other jazz greats traveling between northern tours and New Orleans. Cited from Downtown Development Authority — Shreveport.

docs.summary: Remodeled from a 1909 automobile garage in 1921, the Sunset Cafe at 315 East 35th Street became one of Chicago's earliest integrated Black-and-tan jazz clubs. Louis Armstrong, Earl Hines, Johnny Dodds, and Benny Goodman played its house orchestra before a 1937 remodeling renamed it the Grand Terrace Cafe, which remained popular until 1950.

notabilityBasis.note: renamed: After a 1937 remodeling the venue became the Grand Terrace Cafe and remained a popular nightclub until 1950; original Jazz Age murals survive on interior walls. Cited from Commission on Chicago Landmarks — historical marker.

designated landmark: Following a 1921 remodeling, the Sunset Cafe became a legendary jazz venue; the building was designated a Chicago Landmark on September 9, 1998 as part of the Black Metropolis-Bronzeville Historic District. Cited from City of Chicago — Landmarks.

docs.summary: From Christmas Eve 1941 through 1951, Orchestra Hall at 3711 Woodward Avenue operated as the Paradise Theater, Detroit's premier jazz venue in the Paradise Valley neighborhood adjacent to Black Bottom. Ella Fitzgerald, Billie Holiday, Duke Ellington, Louis Armstrong, and Cab Calloway played the hall before it returned to symphonic use as Orchestra Hall.

notabilityBasis.note: operated as: Orchestra Hall presented jazz artists under the name Paradise Theater from December 24, 1941 until 1951, hosting Ella Fitzgerald, Billie Holiday, Count Basie, Duke Ellington, and Cab Calloway for Black Bottom and Paradise Valley audiences. Cited from Detroit Historical Society.

listed on national register: The building, designed by C. Howard Crane and opened in 1919 as Orchestra Hall, was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1971; the Library of Congress documents its later use as the Paradise Theatre. Cited from Library of Congress — Historic American Buildings Survey.

docs.summary: Opening Thanksgiving Eve 1927 at 4733 South Parkway in Bronzeville, the Savoy Ballroom seated roughly 3,800 dancers in a refined setting that showcased Louis Armstrong, Count Basie, and other national bands seven nights a week until closing in 1948. Built alongside the Regal Theater, it was demolished in the 1970s; the Harold Washington Cultural Center now occupies the site.

notabilityBasis.note: opened: The Savoy Ballroom opened on Thanksgiving Eve, November 23, 1927, at 4733 South Parkway as part of a $1 million commercial development that also included the Regal Theater and South Center department store. Cited from Jazz Age Chicago.

hosted performances: Two major jazz bands alternated nightly at the Savoy, which accommodated approximately 3,800 dancers and featured Louis Armstrong, Count Basie, and other leading bands until it closed in 1948. Cited from National Trust for Historic Preservation.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Completed in 1921 at 1901–1903 Seventh Street NW, the Southern Aid Society–Dunbar Theater Building combined the Washington headquarters of the nation's oldest Black life insurance company with the Dunbar Theatre movie house designed by Reginald W. Geare on the first floor — a mixed-use anchor of the Seventh and U Street Black commercial corridor.

notabilityBasis.note: opened: Black architect Isaiah T. Hatton designed the four-story building completed in 1921; Reginald W. Geare designed the Dunbar Theatre on the first floor for the Murray Brothers, with offices and apartments above for Black tenants. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

listed on national register: The Southern Aid Society–Dunbar Theater Building was listed in the National Register of Historic Places on November 6, 1986 and is a contributing property in the Greater U Street Historic District. Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

docs.summary: By 1921 Greenwood Avenue anchored roughly 35 square blocks of Black-owned groceries, hotels, theaters, newspapers, and law and medical offices — the 'Black Wall Street' of the nation. Residents rebuilt after the district's 1921 destruction, and by the 1940s Greenwood again supported more than 200 Black-owned businesses.

notabilityBasis.note: known as: Black Wall Street — a nationally renowned, self-contained Black entrepreneurial district along Greenwood Avenue with nightclubs, hotels, cafes, newspapers, clothiers, theaters, and professional offices. Cited from okhistory.org.
destroyed in event: Tulsa Race Massacre of May 31 – June 1, 1921, which left the Greenwood area in ruins; residents subsequently rebuilt the district. Cited from nps.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: By the 1920s Auburn Avenue held Atlanta Life Insurance (founded 1905 by Alonzo Herndon), the 1912 Odd Fellows Building, Big Bethel AME, and the Atlanta Daily World (1928). Civic leader John Wesley Dobbs coined the name 'Sweet Auburn' for its prosperity; the district was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1976.

notabilityBasis.note: designated national historic landmark: Sweet Auburn Historic District, designated by the Secretary of the Interior in 1976 for its late-19th to early-20th century Black commercial, residential, and religious buildings. Cited from nps.gov.

center of: Atlanta's African American commercial, social, religious, and political life in the early twentieth century, and the neighborhood where Martin Luther King Jr. was born in 1929. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Chicago's 'Black Metropolis' grew with the Great Migration after 1916, centered on State Street and South Parkway: the Chicago Defender, Overton Hygienic Building (1922-23), Chicago Bee Building, Supreme Life Insurance, and the 1913 Wabash Avenue YMCA. Chicago landmarked the Black Metropolis-Bronzeville District in 1998.

notabilityBasis.note: designated national heritage area: Bronzeville-Black Metropolis National Heritage Area, designated by Congress in 2023, covering more than 200 sites between 18th and 71st Streets. Cited from nps.gov.

designated city landmark district: Black Metropolis-Bronzeville District, designated a Chicago Landmark district in 1998, comprising eight historic buildings and the Victory Monument. Cited from chicago.gov.

docs.summary: After Philip A. Payton Jr.'s Afro-American Realty Company began opening Harlem housing to Black tenants in 1904, the neighborhood went from roughly 10 percent African American in 1910 to about 70 percent by 1930, becoming the capital of the Harlem Renaissance and home to institutions like the Apollo Theater and the Schomburg collection.

notabilityBasis.note: population transformation: Approximately 70 percent African American by 1930, up from approximately 10 percent in 1910, as the neighborhood became the center of the Harlem Renaissance. Cited from nps.gov.
home of: The Apollo Theater, listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1983 as the nation's premier performance hall for Black American performers. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: From the 1890s Beale Street was the main street of Black Memphis: Robert Church Sr.'s Church Park and Auditorium opened in 1899, Ida B. Wells edited the Free Speech nearby, and W. C. Handy published 'The Memphis Blues' in 1912. The street's blues legacy earned it National Historic Landmark designation in 1966.

notabilityBasis.note: designated national historic landmark: Beale Street Historic District, listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1966 (reference 66000731) for its significance in the history of the blues. Cited from npgallery.nps.gov.
commemorated by: Beale Street Historic District marker noting the street's designation as a Registered National Historic Landmark. Cited from hmdb.org.

docs.summary: Between the 1890s and 1920s, U Street NW became Washington's 'Black Broadway' — the True Reformer Building (1903), Howard Theatre (1910), the nation's first full-service Black YMCA, and the Whitelaw Hotel among hundreds of Black-owned businesses. The historic district, designated in 1998, has a period of significance of 1862-1948.

notabilityBasis.note: designated historic district: Greater U Street Historic District, designated in 1998 with a period of significance of 1862-1948. Cited from planning.dc.gov.

known as: Washington's 'Black Broadway' during segregation — the heart of African American business and culture, with hundreds of Black-owned businesses, banks, churches, and theaters by the 1920s. Cited from culturaltourismdc.org.

docs.summary: Founded by freedpeople after the Civil War south of Durham's rail line, Hayti grew commercial spines along Fayetteville, Pettigrew, and Pine Streets, anchored by St. Joseph's AME Church (1891) and Lincoln Hospital (1901). Booker T. Washington praised the community, whose builders also created N.C. Mutual, the nation's largest Black-owned insurance company.

notabilityBasis.note: anchored by: St. Joseph's AME Church, built 1891, the last remaining original structure of Durham's historic Black district, preserved since 1975 as the Hayti Heritage Center. Cited from hayti.org.

recognized as: A self-sufficient African American community founded by freedpeople, later disrupted when 1960s urban renewal and the Durham Freeway displaced thousands of homes and hundreds of Black-owned businesses. Cited from civilrightstrail.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: By 1900 Richmond's Jackson Ward was the 'Cradle of Black Capitalism': the True Reformers' bank (chartered 1888) and Maggie L. Walker's St. Luke Penny Savings Bank (1903) — she was the first American woman to charter a bank — grew among fraternal orders and insurance firms. The 42-block district became a National Historic Landmark in 1978.

notabilityBasis.note: center of: One of the nation's most thriving Black business communities at the turn of the 20th century, with Black-founded banks, insurance companies, and fraternal organizations, including Maggie L. Walker's St. Luke Penny Savings Bank. Cited from nps.gov.

designated national historic landmark: Jackson Ward Historic District, listed in the National Register in 1976 and designated a National Historic Landmark in 1978, one of the largest landmark districts associated with African American history. Cited from dhr.virginia.gov.

docs.summary: Subdivided in the 1790s from Claude Tremé's plantation just outside the French Quarter, Faubourg Tremé is among the oldest Black neighborhoods in the United States: free people of color owned property here before the Civil War, St. Augustine Catholic Church opened in 1841, and the gatherings at nearby Congo Square fed directly into the birth of jazz.

notabilityBasis.note: designated historic district: Tremé local historic district regulated by the New Orleans Historic District Landmarks Commission, encompassing the early-19th-century faubourg where free people of color owned and built property. Cited from nola.gov.
birthplace of: Early jazz culture — brass band, church, and benevolent-society traditions in the neighborhoods around Congo Square shaped the emergence of jazz. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: From the 1890s through the 1960s the 125-acre Farish Street district was the largest economically independent Black community in Mississippi — Black-owned banks, drugstores, funeral homes, churches, the Alamo Theatre, and record labels that cut Delta blues sides. The district was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1980.

notabilityBasis.note: recognized as: The largest economically independent African American community in Mississippi through the 1960s, a business, social, cultural, and residential center from the 1880s onward. Cited from visitjackson.com.
listed national register: Farish Street Neighborhood Historic District, a 125-acre district listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1980. Cited from mdah.ms.gov.

docs.summary: After 1900, Jim Crow ordinances pushed Birmingham's growing Black business community into the blocks along Third, Fourth, and Fifth Avenues North between 15th and 18th Streets. At its peak the district held more than 300 Black-owned businesses around the Colored Masonic Temple; it was listed in the National Register in 1982 and remains majority Black-owned.

notabilityBasis.note: peak scale: More than 300 Black-owned businesses at the district's height, anchored by the Masonic Temple with its library, soda fountain, and ballroom that hosted Duke Ellington and Count Basie. Cited from birminghamcivilrights.com.
listed national register: Fourth Avenue Historic District, listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1982. Cited from hmdb.org.

docs.summary: By the 1920s the 300 block of Northeast Second Street — 'Deep Deuce' — was Oklahoma City's Black main street: barbershops, doctors' and lawyers' offices, a movie theater, and Roscoe Dunjee's Black Dispatch newspaper at 324 NE Second, with dance halls where Jimmy Rushing and Charlie Christian began national careers.

notabilityBasis.note: functioned as: Oklahoma City's African American business district by day and cultural center by night from the early 1900s, known by the 1920s as Deep Deuce, Deep Two, and Deep Second. Cited from okhistory.org.
home of: Roscoe Dunjee, editor of the Black Dispatch, who used the press, courts, and legislature to fight segregation in housing, education, and public facilities. Cited from hmdb.org.

docs.summary: Incorporated on August 15, 1887, by 27 registered Black voters, Eatonville is the oldest incorporated African American municipality in the United States. The Hungerford Normal and Industrial School opened in 1897, and the town shaped the work of Zora Neale Hurston, who grew up here and set much of her writing in Eatonville.

notabilityBasis.note: incorporated year: 1887 — the first incorporated African American community in America, with a preservation trail of ten historic sites. Cited from hmdb.org.
known as: 'The town that freedom built' — the oldest incorporated African American municipality in America, as proclaimed on the town's gateway arch. Cited from hmdb.org.

docs.summary: Founded July 12, 1887, in the Yazoo Delta by Isaiah T. Montgomery and Benjamin T. Green, formerly enslaved at Davis Bend, and incorporated in 1898, Mound Bayou became the nation's leading self-governing all-Black town, with private businesses, a high school, and two hospitals serving Black Mississippians across the Delta.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1887, by cousins Isaiah T. Montgomery and Benjamin T. Green, formerly enslaved at the Davis Bend plantation, on land purchased from the Louisville, New Orleans and Texas Railroad. Cited from cityofmoundbayou.com.

commemorated by: Mississippi Department of Archives and History marker (2017) recording the town's 1887 founding, 1898 incorporation, and its schools and two hospitals serving the Delta. Cited from hmdb.org.

docs.summary: Founded in 1877 by Black settlers from Kentucky on the high plains of Graham County, Nicodemus is the oldest surviving Black homesteader town west of the Mississippi. By 1880 it had a bank, two hotels, three churches, and general stores; five surviving buildings are preserved as Nicodemus National Historic Site, established in 1996.

notabilityBasis.note: designated national historic site: Nicodemus National Historic Site, a unit of the National Park System preserving the town's five surviving historic buildings. Cited from nps.gov.
founded year: 1877, by the Nicodemus Town Company organized April 18, 1877, with W. H. Smith as president; roughly 350 settlers from Kentucky arrived in September 1877. Cited from kansashistory.gov.

docs.summary: Founded after 1865 at Magazine Point, north of Mobile, by West Africans trafficked aboard the schooner Clotilda in 1860, Africatown's founders bought land collectively, elected their own leaders, and established Union Missionary Baptist Church and a school while keeping African language and custom. The historic district was listed in the National Register in 2012.

notabilityBasis.note: founded by: Freed survivors of the schooner Clotilda, the last documented U.S. slave ship (1860), who settled Magazine Point after the Civil War, elected their own officials, purchased land collectively, and established their own church. Cited from hmdb.org.
memorialized by: Africatown Heritage House, the Mobile County museum housing 'Clotilda: The Exhibition' on the ship's 2019 rediscovery and the community's history. Cited from mobilecountyal.gov.

docs.summary: Begun in 1838 when James Weeks, a free Black stevedore, purchased land in central Brooklyn, Weeksville grew into one of antebellum America's largest free Black communities, with churches, schools, an orphan asylum, and its own newspapers. The surviving Hunterfly Road Houses (c. 1840-1880) were designated New York City landmarks in 1970.

notabilityBasis.note: designated city landmark: Houses on Hunterfly Road, 1698-1708 Bergen Street, designated New York City Landmarks in 1970 as surviving structures of the Weeksville community. Cited from nyc.gov.

recognized as: One of the nation's oldest and largest free Black communities before the Civil War, whose four surviving wood-frame houses were restored by the City of New York in a project completed in 2026. Cited from nyc.gov.

docs.summary: Founded in 1908 in Tulare County by Colonel Allen Allensworth — born enslaved, retired as the Army's highest-ranking Black chaplain — with William Payne and three partners, Allensworth was the only California town founded, financed, and governed by African Americans, with a school, library, church, hotel, and businesses on an 800-acre site.

notabilityBasis.note: preserved as: Colonel Allensworth State Historic Park, established in 1976, with restored buildings including the schoolhouse, hotel, general store, and library. Cited from friendsofallensworth.org.

founded year: 1908, by Lt. Colonel Allen Allensworth, Professor William Payne, and three partners — the only California town founded, financed, and governed by African Americans. Cited from parks.ca.gov.

docs.displayName: Cedar Hill (Frederick Douglass National Historic Site)
docs.nameLower: cedar hill (frederick douglass national historic site)
docs.summary: In 1877 Frederick Douglass purchased Cedar Hill, an 8.5-acre Anacostia estate, defying a racially restrictive covenant to become the neighborhood's first Black homeowner. He lived and wrote there until his death in 1895; the National Park Service has preserved the house and grounds since 1988.

notabilityBasis.note: home of: Cedar Hill, the 8.5-acre estate where Frederick Douglass lived from 1877 until his death in 1895, is preserved as Frederick Douglass National Historic Site. Cited from nps.gov.

purchased in defiance of: Douglass bought Cedar Hill in 1877 despite a racially restrictive covenant barring sale to Black buyers, becoming the first Black resident of the Uniontown section of Anacostia. Cited from nps.gov.
