docs.displayName: Harriet Tubman Home for the Aged (Auburn, New York)
docs.nameLower: harriet tubman home for the aged (auburn, new york)
docs.summary: In 1859 Harriet Tubman bought land on South Street in Auburn, New York, from Secretary of State William H. Seward, relocating her aging parents there while she continued Underground Railroad missions. In 1908 she opened the Harriet Tubman Home for the Aged on the same ground; she died there in 1913.

notabilityBasis.note: purchased land from: Tubman purchased the South Street property from Secretary of State William H. Seward in 1859 and relocated her parents and family there while continuing abolitionist and humanitarian work. Cited from nps.gov.

founded: In 1908 Tubman opened the Harriet Tubman Home for the Aged on the property, fulfilling her long-held goal of caring for elderly and indigent Black residents; she lived there until her death in 1913. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.displayName: Harriet Tubman Underground Railroad National Historical Park
docs.nameLower: harriet tubman underground railroad national historical park
docs.summary: Harriet Tubman was born into slavery around 1822 in Dorchester County, Maryland, and escaped in 1849 before returning at least 13 times to guide roughly 70 enslaved people north. The 480-acre national historical park preserves this Eastern Shore landscape; its joint state-federal visitor center opened in March 2017.

notabilityBasis.note: preserves: 480 acres of Dorchester County landscape where Harriet Tubman was born into slavery around 1822 and lived until her escape in 1849, before returning roughly 13 times to guide about 70 enslaved people to freedom. Cited from nps.gov.

designated: Established as a national monument by President Obama on March 25, 2013, and redesignated Harriet Tubman Underground Railroad National Historical Park on December 19, 2014. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: In 1738 Spanish Florida's governor chartered Gracia Real de Santa Teresa de Mose as a free settlement for enslaved people who escaped the Carolinas, making it the first legally sanctioned free Black settlement in what is now the United States. A free Black militia under Captain Francisco Menéndez defended it in 1740.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Gracia Real de Santa Teresa de Mose was chartered by Spanish Florida's governor in 1738 as a free settlement for roughly 100 formerly enslaved people fleeing the English Carolinas and Georgia, on condition they accept Catholic baptism and allegiance to Spain. Cited from floridastateparks.org.

defended by: On June 14, 1740, Spanish troops and a free Black militia commanded by Captain Francisco Menéndez, a formerly enslaved Mandinka man, recaptured Fort Mose from British occupation in the Battle of Bloody Mose. Cited from fortmose.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Under this live oak on September 17, 1861, Mary Smith Peake held the first classes for formerly enslaved people freed by the Union's 'contraband' policy at nearby Fort Monroe, defying Virginia's ban on educating Black residents. Local Black Virginians gathered here again in January 1863 for the region's first Southern reading of the Emancipation Proclamation.

notabilityBasis.note: site of: Mary Smith Peake, a free Black woman, held the first classes for formerly enslaved 'contrabands' under this oak on September 17, 1861, in defiance of an 1831 Virginia law barring the education of enslaved and free Black Virginians. Cited from hamptonu.edu; battlefields.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: John P. Parker purchased his freedom in 1845 and by 1853 owned this riverfront house in Ripley, Ohio, from which he crossed the Ohio River to guide enslaved people north for nearly two decades. He later founded the Phoenix Foundry and patented several inventions; the house became a National Historic Landmark in 1997.

notabilityBasis.note: resided at: John P. Parker, who purchased his own freedom in 1845, lived in this riverfront house from 1853 until his death in 1900 and used it as a base for Underground Railroad crossings of the Ohio River from Kentucky. Cited from battlefields.org.

designated national historic landmark: Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1980 and designated a National Historic Landmark in 1997 for Parker's documented role as an Underground Railroad conductor, entrepreneur, and inventor. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: On May 23, 1861, Frank Baker, Shepard Mallory, and James Townsend escaped slavery and reached Fort Monroe; the next day Major General Benjamin Butler refused to return them, declaring them 'contraband of war.' The ruling drew thousands of self-emancipated people to the fort and helped set the course toward the Emancipation Proclamation.

notabilityBasis.note: site of: On May 23, 1861, Frank Baker, Shepard Mallory, and James Townsend escaped enslavement and reached Fort Monroe; the next day Major General Benjamin Butler declared them 'contraband of war' and refused to return them to bondage. Cited from nps.gov.

precipitated: Butler's contraband decision drew thousands of self-emancipated people to Fort Monroe's 'Freedom's Fortress' and served as a forerunner of the Confiscation Acts, the Militia Act of 1862, and the 1863 Emancipation Proclamation. Cited from nps.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: After Buffalo hotels turned away Black guests, W.E.B. Du Bois, William Monroe Trotter, and 27 other leaders met at Fort Erie, Ontario, July 11-14, 1905, founding the Niagara Movement to demand full civil rights; its first public U.S. meeting followed in August 1906 at Storer College in Harpers Ferry, West Virginia - the direct forerunner of the NAACP.

notabilityBasis.note: founded at: From July 11-14, 1905, W.E.B. Du Bois, William Monroe Trotter, and 27 other Black leaders met at the Erie Beach Hotel in Fort Erie, Ontario — moved across the border after Buffalo, New York hotels refused Black guests — to found the Niagara Movement. Cited from nps.gov.

issued: The Movement's 1905 Declaration of Principles, drafted largely by Du Bois, demanded manhood suffrage, an end to Jim Crow segregation, and equal economic and educational opportunity. Cited from loc.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Responding to the 1908 Springfield, Illinois race riot, roughly 300 reformers including W.E.B. Du Bois, Ida B. Wells, and Mary White Ovington convened the National Negro Conference at the United Charities Building on May 31-June 1, 1909. The Committee of Forty they formed adopted the name NAACP a year later, on May 12, 1910.

notabilityBasis.note: convened at: The National Negro Conference, precursor to the NAACP, convened at Charity Organization Hall in the United Charities Building on May 31-June 1, 1909, bringing together W.E.B. Du Bois, Ida B. Wells, Mary White Ovington, William English Walling, and roughly 300 other reformers. Cited from loc.gov.

adopted name: The Committee of Forty formed at the 1909 conference adopted the formal name National Association for the Advancement of Colored People at its second annual meeting on May 12, 1910. Cited from loc.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.displayName: Founding of the Negro National League
docs.nameLower: founding of the negro national league
docs.summary: On February 13, 1920, Andrew 'Rube' Foster and seven other Black team owners met at Kansas City's Paseo YMCA and organized the Negro National League, the first sustained professional Black baseball league, electing Foster its president. The building, listed on the National Register in 1991, now houses the Negro Leagues Baseball Museum's research center.

notabilityBasis.note: founded at: On February 13, 1920, Andrew 'Rube' Foster and the owners of seven other Black baseball teams met at the Paseo YMCA and organized the Negro National League, the first sustained professional Black baseball league, electing Foster president. Cited from nps.gov.

designated: The Paseo YMCA building was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1991 and today houses the Buck O'Neil Education and Research Center of the Negro Leagues Baseball Museum. Cited from nlbm.com.

docs.displayName: Moton Field / Tuskegee Airmen National Historic Site
docs.nameLower: moton field / tuskegee airmen national historic site
docs.summary: Built in 1941 and named for Tuskegee Institute's second principal Robert Russa Moton, Moton Field was the only primary flight-training site for African American pilot candidates in the World War II Army Air Corps. Congress established Tuskegee Airmen National Historic Site here in 1998 to honor the roughly 1,000 pilots trained there.

notabilityBasis.note: established: Congress established Tuskegee Airmen National Historic Site at Moton Field on November 6, 1998, via Public Law 105-355, to commemorate the pilots trained there who became the first Black military aviators in U.S. history. Cited from nps.gov.

site of: Built in 1941 and named for former Tuskegee Institute principal Robert Russa Moton, Moton Field was the only primary flight-training facility for African American pilot candidates in the U.S. Army Air Corps during World War II. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.displayName: Robert Gould Shaw and Massachusetts 54th Regiment Memorial
docs.nameLower: robert gould shaw and massachusetts 54th regiment memorial
docs.summary: Augustus Saint-Gaudens spent 14 years sculpting this bronze relief, dedicated on Boston Common on May 31, 1897, to Colonel Robert Gould Shaw and the men of the 54th Massachusetts, one of the first Black regiments recruited in the North. Many died in the July 18, 1863 assault on Fort Wagner; the memorial anchors Boston's Black Heritage Trail.

notabilityBasis.note: commemorates: The bronze relief by Augustus Saint-Gaudens, dedicated May 31, 1897, commemorates Colonel Robert Gould Shaw and the men of the 54th Massachusetts Infantry, one of the first Black volunteer regiments recruited in the North, many of whom died in the July 18, 1863 assault on Fort Wagner, South Carolina. Cited from nps.gov.

recognized as: The memorial is considered among the first American public sculptures to depict Black soldiers as individualized figures rather than stereotypes, and it anchors the start of Boston's Black Heritage Trail. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.displayName: Buffalo Soldier Commemorative Area, Fort Leavenworth
docs.nameLower: buffalo soldier commemorative area, fort leavenworth
docs.summary: The 10th U.S. Cavalry, one of the Army's first peacetime Black regiments, organized at Fort Leavenworth on September 21, 1866, under Colonel Benjamin Grierson; its troopers earned the name Buffalo Soldiers from the Kiowa and Cheyenne. General Colin Powell, the first Black Chairman of the Joint Chiefs, dedicated the fort's monument to them in 1992.

notabilityBasis.note: established at: The 10th U.S. Cavalry Regiment, one of the Army's first peacetime Black regiments, was organized at Fort Leavenworth on September 21, 1866, under Colonel Benjamin Grierson, drawing recruits who became known as Buffalo Soldiers. Cited from nps.gov.

dedicated: The Buffalo Soldier Monument at Fort Leavenworth was dedicated on July 25, 1992, by General Colin Powell, then Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff and the first Black American to hold that post. Cited from army.mil.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.displayName: Pullman National Historical Park — A. Philip Randolph Pullman Porter Museum
docs.nameLower: pullman national historical park — a. philip randolph pullman porter museum
docs.summary: By the 1920s the Pullman Company was the largest single employer of Black Americans in the country, staffing its sleeping cars with porters based out of its Chicago works. In 1925 the porters organized the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters under A. Philip Randolph, winning the first labor contract between a Black union and a major U.S. corporation in 1937.

notabilityBasis.note: employed: By the 1920s the Pullman Company was the largest single employer of African Americans in the United States, with porters staffing its sleeping cars nationwide out of the Chicago-based operation. Cited from nps.gov.

founded: Pullman porters organized the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters in 1925 under A. Philip Randolph; after a 12-year campaign the union won its first contract with the Pullman Company in 1937, the first labor agreement between a Black union and a major U.S. corporation. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: On June 19, 1865, Union troops under General Gordon Granger marched through Galveston reading General Order No. 3, informing Texas's more than 250,000 enslaved people of their freedom; their route's final stop was the church now known as Reedy Chapel-AME. Galveston has held an annual Juneteenth celebration on Ashton Villa's lawn since 1979.

notabilityBasis.note: site of: On June 19, 1865, Union troops under Major General Gordon Granger marched through Galveston reading General Order No. 3, which informed more than 250,000 enslaved Texans of their freedom; their route's stops included the Osterman Building headquarters, the Custom House, the courthouse, and the church now known as Reedy Chapel-AME, its final stop. Cited from galvestonhistory.org.

commemorated at: Since 1979, under the leadership of former city manager Doug Matthews and Texas state representative Al Edwards, Galveston has held its annual Juneteenth Celebration on the lawn of Ashton Villa, the 1859 Broadway mansion. Cited from galvestonhistory.org.

docs.summary: In 1825 Andrew Williams, a 25-year-old Black shoeshiner, bought the first lots of what became Seneca Village, a community of roughly 225 residents — about two-thirds African American — with three churches and a school by 1855. The city cleared the settlement by eminent domain in 1857 to build Central Park, displacing its Black landowners and their voting rights.

notabilityBasis.note: displaced by: The City of New York used eminent domain to clear Seneca Village by the end of 1857 for the construction of Central Park, displacing all of its residents, most of whom were Black landowners who also lost the voting rights tied to property ownership. Cited from centralparknyc.org.

founded: In 1825 Andrew Williams, a 25-year-old African American shoeshiner, became the first landowner of Seneca Village, purchasing three lots from John and Elizabeth Whitehead; by 1855 the community held roughly 225 residents, about two-thirds African American, with three churches and a school. Cited from centralparknyc.org.

docs.summary: In February 1870, Mississippi minister and Civil War chaplain Hiram Revels became the first African American to serve in the U.S. Senate, filling the seat once held by Jefferson Davis. He resigned within a year to become the founding president of Alcorn University, the nation's first state-supported land-grant college for Black Americans.

notabilityBasis.note: sworn in on: Hiram Revels was sworn into the U.S. Senate on February 25, 1870, becoming the first African American to serve in the United States Senate, seated in the Mississippi seat once held by Jefferson Davis.. Cited from senate.gov.

founded on: Revels resigned his Senate seat in 1871 to become the first president of Alcorn University (now Alcorn State University), the first state-supported land-grant college for Black Americans in the United States.. Cited from mississippiencyclopedia.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Joseph Hayne Rainey, born enslaved in Georgetown, South Carolina, escaped to Bermuda during the Civil War and returned to become the first African American to serve in the U.S. House of Representatives, seated December 12, 1870. In 1874 he became the first Black lawmaker to preside over the House from the Speaker's chair.

notabilityBasis.note: sworn in on: Rainey took his seat in the U.S. House of Representatives on December 12, 1870, becoming the first African American to serve in the House.. Cited from history.house.gov.

site of: After the Civil War, Rainey settled in this Prince Street house, which served as his home and district headquarters throughout his congressional career; it was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1984.. Cited from theclio.com.

The entity (place, school, institution) holds a formal landmark designation — National Register of Historic Places, National Historic Landmark, or an equivalent state/local landmark register entry — with documented listing evidence.

docs.summary: Born into slavery near Farmville, Virginia, Blanche K. Bruce rose to become, in 1875, the first African American to serve a full six-year term in the U.S. Senate, representing Mississippi. He later served as Register of the U.S. Treasury and Recorder of Deeds for the District of Columbia.

notabilityBasis.note: born on: Bruce was born into slavery on March 1, 1841, near Farmville in Prince Edward County, Virginia; a Virginia Department of Historic Resources marker near Green Bay commemorates his birthplace.. Cited from hmdb.org.

elected on: The Mississippi legislature elected Bruce to the U.S. Senate in February 1874; he served from March 1875 to March 1881, the first Black senator to complete a full term.. Cited from senate.gov.

docs.summary: Pinckney Benton Stewart Pinchback, a Union Army officer and Louisiana state senator, served as acting governor of Louisiana from December 9, 1872 to January 13, 1873, becoming the first Black governor of a U.S. state. He later helped found Southern University and was buried in New Orleans.

notabilityBasis.note: buried at: Pinchback is buried with his mother in the family plot at Metairie Cemetery in New Orleans.. Cited from 64parishes.org.

sworn in on: Pinchback served as acting governor of Louisiana from December 9, 1872 to January 13, 1873 after the impeachment of Governor Henry Warmoth, becoming the first Black governor of a U.S. state.. Cited from senate.gov.

docs.summary: Elected associate justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court in February 1870, Jonathan Jasper Wright became the first Black state supreme court justice in U.S. history and the first Black man elected to any state or federal appellate judgeship. He practiced law in Charleston until his death in 1885.

notabilityBasis.note: practiced in: After resigning from the court in 1877 amid a Reconstruction-era impeachment campaign, Wright returned to private law practice in Charleston, where he died in 1885.. Cited from brennancenter.org.

elected on: The South Carolina General Assembly elected Wright as an associate justice of the state Supreme Court on February 1, 1870, making him the first Black state supreme court justice in the United States.. Cited from scencyclopedia.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Born enslaved in Beaufort, South Carolina, Robert Smalls commandeered the Confederate steamer CSS Planter in 1862 and delivered it to the Union blockade, freeing himself and his crew. He went on to serve five terms in Congress and became the first Black captain of a U.S. vessel.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: On May 13, 1862, Smalls piloted the Confederate transport CSS Planter out of Charleston harbor and surrendered it to the Union Navy, becoming the first African American captain of a vessel in U.S. service.. Cited from nps.gov.

site of: This house at 511 Prince Street, where Smalls had been enslaved as a child, was purchased by him after the war and became his home; it was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1974.. Cited from nps.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

The entity (place, school, institution) holds a formal landmark designation — National Register of Historic Places, National Historic Landmark, or an equivalent state/local landmark register entry — with documented listing evidence.

docs.summary: John Mercer Langston, the first Black lawyer admitted to the Ohio bar, became the founding dean of Howard University's law school and, in 1885, the first president of what is now Virginia State University. In 1890 he was seated as Virginia's first Black member of Congress.

notabilityBasis.note: founded on: In January 1886, Langston became the first president of the Virginia Normal and Collegiate Institute, now Virginia State University, serving until December 1887.. Cited from encyclopediavirginia.org.

sworn in on: After successfully contesting a disputed 1888 election result, Langston was sworn into the 51st Congress on September 23, 1890, becoming Virginia's first Black member of the U.S. House of Representatives.. Cited from history.house.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: On July 3, 1844, Macon Bolling Allen was admitted to the Maine bar by examination, becoming the first African American licensed to practice law in the United States. He later co-founded the first known Black law firm in the country in Charleston, South Carolina.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: Allen was licensed to practice law in Maine on July 3, 1844, after passing a qualifying examination, becoming the nation's first African American lawyer.. Cited from fd.org; blackpast.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Charlotte E. Ray graduated from Howard University's law school in 1872, applying under the initials "C.E. Ray" to disguise her gender, and became the first Black woman lawyer in the United States and the first woman admitted to practice before the D.C. Supreme Court.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: Ray graduated from Howard University School of Law in 1872 and was admitted to the District of Columbia bar, becoming the first Black woman lawyer in the United States.. Cited from history.com; blackpast.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Mifflin Wistar Gibbs, a California gold-rush merchant and abolitionist newspaper founder, moved to Little Rock, Arkansas after the Civil War and in 1873 became the first elected African American municipal judge in the United States, serving as Little Rock's police judge.

notabilityBasis.note: elected on: Gibbs was elected Little Rock police judge, serving from November 1874 to April 1875, becoming the first elected African American municipal judge in the United States.. Cited from encyclopediaofarkansas.net.
founded on: In San Francisco in 1855, Gibbs co-founded Mirror of the Times, the first Black newspaper published west of the Mississippi River.. Cited from encyclopediaofarkansas.net.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Born enslaved in Louisiana, John Roy Lynch became, at age 24, the first African American speaker of the Mississippi House of Representatives in 1872, then represented Mississippi in Congress for three terms, one of the most prominent Black Reconstruction-era officeholders.

notabilityBasis.note: elected on: In 1872, at age 24, Lynch became the first African American Speaker of the Mississippi House of Representatives, presiding in this building, then the state capitol.. Cited from mdah.ms.gov.

sworn in on: Lynch served in the U.S. House of Representatives across the 43rd, 44th, 45th, and 47th Congresses, and in 1884 became temporary chairman of the Republican National Convention, the first Black man to deliver its keynote address.. Cited from history.house.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Alexander Thomas Augusta, commissioned an Army surgeon in 1863, became the highest-ranking Black officer of the Civil War and the first African American to head a U.S. hospital and to teach at a U.S. medical college. In 1890 he became the first Black officer buried at Arlington National Cemetery.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: Commissioned a major in April 1863 as head surgeon of the 7th U.S. Colored Infantry, Augusta became the U.S. Army's first Black surgeon and, by 1865, its highest-ranking Black officer as a brevet lieutenant colonel.. Cited from blackpast.org.

buried at: Following his death in 1890, Augusta was buried with full military honors in Section 1 of Arlington National Cemetery, a section previously reserved for white officers, making him the first Black officer buried there.. Cited from freetoursbyfoot.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Abolitionist, physician, and writer Martin Delany was commissioned a major in February 1865, becoming the first Black field officer in the U.S. Army. He recruited formerly enslaved men into United States Colored Troops regiments in the South Carolina Lowcountry.

notabilityBasis.note: buried at: Delany died in Wilberforce, Ohio, on January 24, 1885, and is buried in a family plot at Massies Creek Cemetery, about three miles from his home.. Cited from hmdb.org.

first to: In February 1865, Delany was commissioned a major, becoming the first Black field officer in the United States Army, after proposing to President Lincoln a corps of Black soldiers led by Black officers.. Cited from schistory.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Sergeant William Harvey Carney of the 54th Massachusetts Volunteer Infantry retrieved and carried the regimental colors during the July 1863 assault on Fort Wagner despite severe wounds. His 1900 Medal of Honor recognized the earliest action of any Black Medal of Honor recipient.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: During the July 18, 1863 assault on Fort Wagner, Carney carried the U.S. flag forward after the color bearer fell; his Medal of Honor, awarded May 23, 1900, recognized the earliest battlefield action of any African American Medal of Honor recipient.. Cited from cmohs.org.
buried at: Carney died in 1908 and is buried in the family plot at Oak Grove Cemetery in New Bedford, Massachusetts, where his headstone bears an image of the Medal of Honor.. Cited from army.mil.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Carl B. Stokes was elected mayor of Cleveland in November 1967 and took office January 1, 1968, becoming the first African American elected mayor of a major U.S. city. His administration pursued police reform and community investment amid the era's urban unrest.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: Stokes defeated Seth Taft, grandson of former President William Howard Taft, with 50.5 percent of the vote.. Cited from case.edu.
elected on: Stokes was elected mayor of Cleveland on November 7, 1967, and took office January 1, 1968, becoming the first Black mayor of one of the ten largest U.S. cities.. Cited from nps.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Richard Gordon Hatcher won election as mayor of Gary, Indiana in November 1967, becoming, alongside Cleveland's Carl Stokes, one of the first two African Americans elected mayor of a U.S. city with more than 100,000 people. He served twenty years, from 1968 to 1988.

notabilityBasis.note: served as: Hatcher served as mayor of Gary for twenty years, from 1968 to 1988, after overcoming a primary marked by voter intimidation and vote-rigging attempts against his candidacy.. Cited from nbcnews.com.

elected on: Hatcher won Gary's mayoral election on November 7, 1967, becoming, with Carl Stokes of Cleveland, one of the first African Americans elected mayor of a U.S. city with a population over 100,000.. Cited from blackpast.org.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Coleman Alexander Young took office as Detroit's first Black mayor in January 1974 and served five terms through 1994, disbanding a controversial police unit and pursuing police integration during a pivotal era in the city's history.

notabilityBasis.note: site of: Detroit's city government headquarters at 2 Woodward Avenue was renamed the Coleman A. Young Municipal Center in his honor.. Cited from detroitmi.gov.
sworn in on: Young took office as Detroit's first African American mayor in January 1974, going on to serve five consecutive terms until 1994.. Cited from britannica.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Tom Bradley, a former Los Angeles police lieutenant and city councilman, defeated incumbent Sam Yorty on May 29, 1973, to become the first Black mayor of Los Angeles. He served an unprecedented five terms, twenty years, until 1993.

notabilityBasis.note: served as: Bradley served twenty years as mayor of Los Angeles, from 1973 to 1993, the longest tenure in the city's history at the time.. Cited from pbssocal.org.

elected on: Bradley defeated incumbent mayor Sam Yorty on May 29, 1973, becoming the first Black mayor of Los Angeles and one of the first Black mayors of a major U.S. city.. Cited from caamuseum.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Harold Washington won a multiracial coalition campaign to become Chicago's first Black mayor, inaugurated April 29, 1983, after a voter registration drive added over 127,000 African Americans to the rolls. He served until his death in office in 1987.

notabilityBasis.note: elected on: A voter registration drive organized by community groups, Black churches, and businesses added more than 127,000 African Americans to Chicago's voting rolls ahead of Washington's 1983 win.. Cited from wttw.com.
sworn in on: Washington was inaugurated at Navy Pier on April 29, 1983, becoming Chicago's first Black mayor after winning the general election with 52 percent of the vote.. Cited from chipublib.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: David N. Dinkins, a former Manhattan borough president, was inaugurated January 1, 1990 as the 106th Mayor of New York City, becoming the first African American to hold the office. He implemented the Safe Streets, Safe City initiative during his single term.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: Dinkins implemented the Safe Streets, Safe City criminal justice plan, under which the city's homicide rate declined substantially by the end of his term.. Cited from npr.org.
sworn in on: Dinkins was inaugurated January 1, 1990 as the 106th Mayor of the City of New York, the first African American to hold the office.. Cited from archives.nyc.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: W. Wilson Goode Sr., a former city administrator who grew up in poverty in rural North Carolina, was inaugurated as Philadelphia's first Black mayor in January 1984 after defeating Frank Rizzo in the Democratic primary. He served two terms, from 1984 to 1992.

notabilityBasis.note: served as: Goode served two terms as mayor, from 1984 to 1992, a tenure significantly shaped by the 1985 MOVE police confrontation and house bombing.. Cited from upi.com.
sworn in on: Goode was inaugurated as Philadelphia's first African American mayor in January 1984, after defeating former mayor Frank Rizzo in the 1983 Democratic primary.. Cited from britannica.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Willie L. Brown Jr. served as the first African American Speaker of the California State Assembly from 1980 to 1995, a record fourteen-year tenure, before winning election as San Francisco's first Black mayor, serving from 1996 to 2004.

notabilityBasis.note: elected on: Brown was elected mayor of San Francisco in 1995, taking office in 1996 as the city's first African American mayor, and served until 2004.. Cited from britannica.com.
first to: Brown served as Speaker of the California State Assembly from 1980 to 1995, the first African American to hold the post and its longest-serving speaker.. Cited from californiamuseum.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Kenneth A. Gibson, a former city engineer, defeated two-term incumbent Hugh Addonizio in 1970 to become the first African American mayor of a major northeastern U.S. city, leading Newark, New Jersey for sixteen years until 1986.

notabilityBasis.note: elected on: Gibson defeated incumbent Hugh Addonizio in a 1970 runoff, becoming the first African American to serve as mayor of a major city in the northeastern United States.. Cited from aaihs.org.
first to: In 1976, Gibson became the first African American president of the United States Conference of Mayors.. Cited from democracynow.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Maynard Holbrook Jackson Jr. was sworn in January 7, 1974, as Atlanta's first Black mayor and the first Black mayor of any major city in the American South. He served three non-consecutive terms and pioneered minority business inclusion programs.

notabilityBasis.note: served as: Jackson served as Atlanta's mayor for three terms, from 1974 to 1982 and again from 1990 to 1994, the second-longest tenure in the city's history.. Cited from britannica.com.
sworn in on: Jackson was sworn in on January 7, 1974, as Atlanta's first Black mayor and the first Black mayor of any major city in the American South.. Cited from georgiaencyclopedia.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Ernest Nathan "Dutch" Morial took office May 2, 1978 as the first African American mayor of New Orleans, capping a career that also included being the city's first Black assistant U.S. attorney and the first Black Louisiana state legislator since Reconstruction.

notabilityBasis.note: sworn in on: Morial took office as the first African American mayor of New Orleans on May 2, 1978, after winning 95 percent of the Black vote and 20 percent of the white vote.. Cited from nola.com.

first to: Before becoming mayor, Morial was the first Black graduate of LSU's law school, the first Black assistant U.S. attorney in Louisiana, and the first Black Louisiana state legislator since Reconstruction.. Cited from washingtonpost.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Sharon Pratt Dixon was sworn in January 2, 1991 as mayor of Washington, D.C., becoming the first African American woman to lead a major American city. She served a single term, from 1991 to 1995, as the district's first mayor born in the city since 1861.

notabilityBasis.note: sworn in on: Pratt was sworn in as mayor of the District of Columbia on January 2, 1991, becoming the first African American woman mayor of a large American city.. Cited from blackpast.org.
first to: Pratt was also the first mayor born in the District of Columbia to hold the office since Richard Wallach took office in 1861.. Cited from en.wikipedia.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Karen Bass, a former physician assistant and community organizer, was elected the first woman mayor of Los Angeles in 2022. She had earlier become, in 2008, the first Black woman in U.S. history to serve as speaker of a state legislative body, leading the California State Assembly.

notabilityBasis.note: elected on: Bass was elected mayor of Los Angeles in 2022, becoming the city's first woman mayor and its second Black mayor after Tom Bradley.. Cited from en.wikipedia.org.

first to: In 2008, Bass was elected the 67th Speaker of the California State Assembly, becoming the first African American woman in U.S. history to serve as speaker of any state legislative body.. Cited from thehistorymakers.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Oscar Stanton De Priest, a Chicago alderman and real estate businessman, won a special 1928 election to Congress, becoming the first African American elected to Congress outside the South and the first Black member of Congress in the twentieth century.

notabilityBasis.note: served as: De Priest served in the U.S. House from 1929 to 1935, representing Illinois's 1st congressional district based in Chicago.. Cited from avoice.cbcfinc.org.

elected on: De Priest won election to the U.S. House of Representatives from Illinois's 1st district in 1928, becoming the first Black member of Congress elected from a northern state and the first of the twentieth century.. Cited from history.house.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Shirley Anita Chisholm won election to Congress from Brooklyn's 12th district in November 1968, becoming the first Black woman elected to the U.S. Congress. In 1972 she became the first Black candidate for a major party's presidential nomination.

notabilityBasis.note: elected on: Chisholm won election in November 1968 to a newly created congressional district centered in Bedford-Stuyvesant, Brooklyn, becoming the first Black woman elected to the United States Congress.. Cited from history.house.gov.
first to: In 1972, Chisholm became the first Black candidate for a major party's presidential nomination and the first woman to seek the Democratic Party's nomination.. Cited from archives.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Edward W. Brooke III, Massachusetts's attorney general since 1963, won election to the U.S. Senate in 1966, becoming the first African American senator elected by popular vote and the first Black senator since Reconstruction. He served two terms, from 1967 to 1979.

notabilityBasis.note: elected on: Brooke defeated former Governor Endicott Peabody in 1966 and was seated in the Senate on January 3, 1967, becoming the first African American elected to the U.S. Senate by popular vote.. Cited from senate.gov.
first to: Brooke was elected Massachusetts attorney general in 1962, becoming the first African American elected attorney general of any U.S. state.. Cited from britannica.com.

docs.summary: Barbara Jordan, the first Black woman elected to the Texas Senate since Reconstruction, was elected to the U.S. House in 1972, becoming the first woman descended from enslaved Americans elected to Congress. notabilityBasis.note: first to: Flipper graduated from the United States Military Academy at West Point on June 14, 1877, fiftieth in a class of seventy-six, becoming the first African American graduate of West Point.. Cited from nps.gov; army.mil.

docs.summary: Benjamin Oliver Davis Sr. was promoted to brigadier general on October 25, 1940, becoming the U.S. Army's first African American general officer, after rising through the ranks including command of the New York National Guard's 369th Infantry Regiment.

notabilityBasis.note: buried at: Davis is buried in Section 2 of Arlington National Cemetery, near the grave of his son, Air Force General Benjamin O. Davis Jr.. Cited from findagrave.com.
first to: Davis was promoted to brigadier general on October 25, 1940, becoming the U.S. Army's first African American general officer.. Cited from armyheritage.org.

docs.summary: Benjamin O. Davis Jr., the only Black cadet at West Point during his four years there, commanded the Tuskegee Airmen's 99th Fighter Squadron and 332nd Fighter Group in World War II and became the first Black general in the U.S. Air Force; in 1998 he was advanced to four-star general in retirement.

notabilityBasis.note: buried at: On December 9, 1998, President Clinton advanced the retired Davis Jr. to four-star general; he is buried in Section 2 of Arlington National Cemetery near his father, General Benjamin O. Davis Sr.. Cited from nationalmuseum.af.mil.

first to: Davis Jr. became the first African American general officer in the U.S. Air Force, commanding the Tuskegee Airmen's 332nd Fighter Group in combat over Europe during World War II.. Cited from af.mil.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Charles Young, the third African American graduate of West Point, became the U.S. Army's first Black colonel and its highest-ranking Black officer until his death in 1922. He led a cavalry charge against Pancho Villa's forces in 1916 and taught military science at Wilberforce University.

notabilityBasis.note: site of: This house, where Young lived while a professor of military science at nearby Wilberforce University, was designated the Charles Young Buffalo Soldiers National Monument by President Obama in 2013.. Cited from nps.gov.

first to: Young was promoted to colonel in 1917, becoming the first African American to reach that rank in the U.S. Army and the highest-ranking Black officer in the Regular Army until his death in 1922.. Cited from nps.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Doris "Dorie" Miller, a mess attendant aboard the USS West Virginia, carried wounded sailors to safety and manned an anti-aircraft gun during the December 7, 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor. In May 1942 he became the first Black American awarded the Navy Cross.

notabilityBasis.note: site of: Miller's hometown of Waco, Texas, dedicated the Doris Miller Memorial in his honor, commemorating his Pearl Harbor heroism.. Cited from waco-texas.com.

first to: Miller received the Navy Cross from Admiral Chester Nimitz on May 27, 1942, for his actions during the attack on Pearl Harbor, becoming the first African American awarded the Navy Cross.. Cited from britannica.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Vernon Baker, an Army lieutenant, led an assault that eliminated multiple enemy positions near Viareggio, Italy in April 1945. Denied recognition for decades due to racial bias in the awards process, he received the Medal of Honor from President Clinton in 1997, one of seven Black World War II recipients.

notabilityBasis.note: buried at: Baker died in 2010 and was buried with full military honors in Section 59 of Arlington National Cemetery.. Cited from va.gov.

first to: In a 1997 White House ceremony, Baker was one of seven African Americans awarded the Medal of Honor for World War II service, and the only one of the seven still living to accept it in person.. Cited from nationalww2museum.org.

docs.summary: Marcelite Jordan Harris, a Spelman College graduate and Air Force maintenance officer, became the first Black woman general in the U.S. Air Force in 1991 and, in 1995, the first Black woman major general in the history of the U.S. armed forces.

notabilityBasis.note: buried at: Harris was buried with full military honors alongside her husband at Arlington National Cemetery on February 7, 2019.. Cited from nbcnews.com.

first to: In 1991, Harris became the first African American woman brigadier general in the U.S. Air Force; on May 25, 1995, she became the first Black woman major general in the history of the U.S. armed forces.. Cited from tshaonline.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Michelle Howard, a 1982 U.S. Naval Academy graduate, became the first Black woman to command a Navy combatant ship in 1999 and, in 2014, the first woman four-star admiral in U.S. history, later commanding NATO's Allied Joint Force Command Naples.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: In 1999, Howard became the first Black woman to command a U.S. Navy combatant ship, USS Rushmore.. Cited from navy.mil; blackenterprise.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Roscoe Robinson Jr., a 1951 West Point graduate decorated for combat leadership in Vietnam, became the first African American to command the 82nd Airborne Division in 1976 and, on August 30, 1982, the first Black four-star general in U.S. Army history.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: On August 30, 1982, Robinson became the first African American to attain the rank of four-star general in the United States Army.. Cited from armyhistory.org; blackpast.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Daniel "Chappie" James Jr., a Tuskegee-trained fighter pilot who flew combat missions in Korea and Vietnam, was promoted to four-star general on September 1, 1975, becoming the first African American to reach four-star rank in any branch of the U.S. armed forces.

notabilityBasis.note: buried at: James died in 1978 and was buried with full military honors in Section 2 of Arlington National Cemetery.. Cited from washingtonpost.com.

first to: James was promoted to four-star general on September 1, 1975, and assigned commander in chief of the North American Air Defense Command, becoming the first African American to reach four-star rank in the U.S. armed forces.. Cited from nationalmuseum.af.mil.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Samuel L. Gravely Jr. became, in 1961, the first Black naval officer to command a U.S. Navy ship, and in 1971 the first Black officer promoted to flag rank in the Navy, ultimately retiring as a vice admiral after commanding the Third Fleet.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: In 1961, Gravely became temporary commanding officer of the USS Theodore E. Chandler, making him the first African American naval officer to command a U.S. Navy ship.. Cited from usni.org; news.va.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Frank E. Petersen Jr., commissioned in 1952, became the first African American Marine Corps aviator, and on February 23, 1979, the first Black general in Marine Corps history, after flying 350 combat missions over two wars.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: Petersen was commissioned in 1952 and became the Marine Corps' first Black aviator; on February 23, 1979, he was promoted to brigadier general, becoming the first African American general in Marine Corps history.. Cited from airandspace.si.edu; marines.mil.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Hazel Winifred Johnson-Brown, who had been denied nursing school admission in her native Pennsylvania because she was Black, became in 1979 the first Black woman general in the U.S. Army and the first Black chief of the Army Nurse Corps, commanding more than 7,000 nurses.

notabilityBasis.note: buried at: Johnson-Brown died in 2011 and is buried in Section 60 of Arlington National Cemetery.. Cited from findagrave.com.

first to: In 1979, Johnson-Brown was promoted to brigadier general and named chief of the U.S. Army Nurse Corps, becoming the first Black woman general in the United States Army and commanding more than 7,000 nurses.. Cited from thenmusa.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Charity Adams Earley became the first African American woman commissioned as an officer in the Women's Army Auxiliary Corps in 1942, and in 1945 commanded the 6888th Central Postal Directory Battalion, the only Black WAC unit deployed overseas in World War II. Fort Lee, Virginia was renamed Fort Gregg-Adams in her honor in 2023.

notabilityBasis.note: site of: On April 27, 2023, Fort Lee, Virginia was renamed Fort Gregg-Adams in honor of Lieutenant General Arthur J. Gregg and Lieutenant Colonel Charity Adams Earley.. Cited from encyclopediavirginia.org.

first to: Adams was commissioned a second lieutenant on August 29, 1942, becoming the first African American woman officer in the Women's Army Auxiliary Corps, later the WACs.. Cited from nationalww2museum.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Nancy Leftenant-Colon, repeatedly denied entry to the military as a Black nurse, was accepted as an Army Nurse Corps reservist in 1945 and, after President Truman's 1948 desegregation order, became the first Black woman integrated into the Regular Army Nurse Corps.

notabilityBasis.note: served as: After graduating from nursing school in 1941 and being repeatedly rejected because of her race, Leftenant-Colon was accepted as an Army Nurse Corps reservist in January 1945.. Cited from washingtonpost.com.

first to: Following President Truman's Executive Order 9981 desegregating the military, Leftenant-Colon applied for and received regular Army Nurse Corps status in 1948, becoming the first Black woman integrated into the Regular Army Nurse Corps.. Cited from armytimes.com.

docs.displayName: Frederick Douglass (U.S. Marshal and 1872 Vice-Presidential Nominee)
docs.nameLower: frederick douglass (u.s. marshal and 1872 vice-presidential nominee)
docs.summary: Beyond his abolitionist career, Frederick Douglass was confirmed by the Senate on March 17, 1877, as U.S. Marshal for the District of Columbia, the first African American to hold the post, and in 1872 became the first Black person nominated for U.S. Vice President, on Victoria Woodhull's Equal Rights Party ticket.

notabilityBasis.note: sworn in on: The Senate confirmed Douglass as U.S. Marshal for the District of Columbia on March 17, 1877, making him the first African American to hold the post.. Cited from boundarystones.weta.org.

first to: In 1872, Douglass became the first African American nominated for Vice President of the United States, named without his knowledge as Victoria Woodhull's running mate on the Equal Rights Party ticket.. Cited from news.harvard.edu.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Edward R. Dudley, a civil rights lawyer recruited by Thurgood Marshall to the NAACP, was promoted to Ambassador upon the 1949 elevation of the U.S. mission in Liberia, becoming the first African American to hold the rank of U.S. Ambassador.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: In 1949, when the U.S. mission in Liberia was elevated to a full embassy, Dudley was promoted to Ambassador, becoming the first African American to hold the rank of U.S. Ambassador.. Cited from diplomacy.state.gov; britannica.com.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: James Wormley Jones, a Washington, D.C. police detective and decorated World War I infantry captain, was sworn in on November 19, 1919, as the first African American special agent of the Bureau of Investigation, later the FBI.

notabilityBasis.note: served as: As an undercover agent known internally as Agent 800, Jones's investigations included the case against Marcus Garvey and the African Blood Brotherhood before he left the Bureau in 1923.. Cited from blackpast.org.

first to: Jones was appointed the first African American special agent of the Bureau of Investigation on November 19, 1919, by director A. Bruce Bielaski, assigned to its General Intelligence Division.. Cited from fbi.gov.

docs.summary: Patrick H. Raymond, son of a formerly enslaved abolitionist, was appointed Chief Engineer of the Cambridge, Massachusetts Fire Department on January 5, 1871, becoming the first known Black fire chief in the United States. He commanded the department during the Great Boston Fire of 1872.

notabilityBasis.note: site of: As chief, Raymond commanded the Cambridge Fire Department's response to the Great Fire of Boston in 1872, working alongside Boston Fire Chief Damrell.. Cited from thecambridgeroom.wordpress.com.

first to: Cambridge Mayor Hamlin Harding appointed Raymond Chief Engineer of the Cambridge Fire Department on January 5, 1871, making him the first known Black fire chief in the United States.. Cited from cambridgema.gov.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Lee P. Brown led police departments in Atlanta, Houston, and New York City before becoming, in 1997, the first African American elected mayor of Houston, Texas, serving three terms through 2004.

notabilityBasis.note: elected on: In 1997, Brown was elected the first African American mayor of Houston, Texas, and was re-elected twice, serving through 2004.. Cited from watchtheyard.com.

first to: In 1982, Brown became the first African American appointed police chief of Houston, developing the department's Neighborhood Oriented Policing program during his tenure through 1990.. Cited from blackpast.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Willie L. Williams became Philadelphia's first African American police commissioner in 1988, then in 1992 the first Black chief of the Los Angeles Police Department, leading it through the department's post-Rodney King reform effort.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: Williams was appointed Philadelphia's police commissioner in 1988, the first African American to hold the position, serving four years and earning praise for improving police-community relations.. Cited from inquirer.com; nleomf.org.

docs.summary: Established February 25, 1837, through Quaker philanthropist Richard Humphreys' bequest of $10,000 to educate people of African descent and prepare them as teachers. Begun in Philadelphia as the Institute for Colored Youth, it is the nation's oldest historically Black institution of higher learning.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1837. Cited from Cheyney University of Pennsylvania.
first in nation: Oldest historically Black institution of higher education in the United States. Cited from Cheyney University of Pennsylvania.

docs.summary: Chartered April 29, 1854, as Ashmun Institute by John Miller Dickey and Sarah Emlen Cresson, becoming the nation's first degree-granting HBCU. Renamed for Abraham Lincoln in 1866, it educated Thurgood Marshall, Langston Hughes, and future heads of state across Africa.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1854. Cited from Lincoln University.
first in nation: Nation's first degree-granting historically Black college and university. Cited from Lincoln University.

docs.summary: Founded in 1856 at Tawawa Springs near Xenia, Ohio, by the Methodist Episcopal Church and purchased in 1863 by the A.M.E. Church under Bishop Daniel A. Payne, making it the nation's first college owned and operated by African Americans. Payne became the first Black president of an American university.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1856. Cited from Wilberforce University.
first in nation: First college owned and operated by African Americans, under the A.M.E. Church from 1863. Cited from Wilberforce University.

docs.summary: Chartered by Congress on March 2, 1867, and named for Freedmen's Bureau commissioner Gen. Oliver O. Howard. From a single frame building it grew into a comprehensive research university whose law school trained the litigators, Charles Hamilton Houston and Thurgood Marshall among them, who dismantled legal segregation.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1867. Cited from Howard University.
notable program: School of Law, established 1869, incubated the legal strategy behind Brown v. Board of Education. Cited from Howard University School of Law.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Convened its first classes January 9, 1866, in former Union Army barracks in Nashville under the American Missionary Association. In 1871 its Jubilee Singers toured to save the school, carrying spirituals to concert stages worldwide and funding Jubilee Hall; in 1930 Fisk became the first Black college accredited by SACS.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1866. Cited from Fisk University.
first in nation: First African American institution accredited by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools, 1930. Cited from Fisk University.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Founded February 14, 1867, as the Augusta Institute in the basement of Springfield Baptist Church in Augusta, Georgia, to educate Black men for ministry and teaching. Relocated to Atlanta in 1879 and renamed Morehouse in 1913, it remains the nation's only historically Black liberal arts college for men.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1867. Cited from Morehouse College.
founded by: Rev. William Jefferson White, at Springfield Baptist Church, Augusta. Cited from Morehouse College.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Founded in 1867 as the Centenary Biblical Institute by the Baltimore Conference of the Methodist Episcopal Church to train ministers. Renamed Morgan College in 1890 for trustee Rev. Lyttleton Morgan, it remained private until Maryland purchased it in 1939 to widen public higher education for Black citizens.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1867. Cited from Morgan State University.
founded by: Baltimore Conference of the Methodist Episcopal Church. Cited from Morgan State University.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Founded April 1, 1868, on the Virginia Peninsula by Brig. Gen. Samuel Chapman Armstrong with American Missionary Association backing. Its normal-and-agricultural model of learning by doing trained thousands of teachers for freedpeople's schools, among them Booker T. Washington, class of 1875.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1868. Cited from Hampton University.
founded by: Brig. Gen. Samuel Chapman Armstrong. Cited from Hampton University.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Formed July 1, 1988, by consolidating Atlanta University, founded 1865 as the nation's first institution to award graduate degrees to African Americans, with Clark College, founded 1869 as the first four-year liberal arts college serving a primarily Black student body. W. E. B. Du Bois taught on the Atlanta University faculty.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1988. Cited from Clark Atlanta University.
first in nation: Parent institution Atlanta University, 1865, was the nation's first to award graduate degrees to African Americans. Cited from Clark Atlanta University.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Founded in 1876 as the Medical Department of Central Tennessee College with a $30,000 gift from Samuel Meharry and his brothers, repaying a debt of kindness to a formerly enslaved family. It was the first medical school for African Americans in the South and received its independent charter in 1915.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1876. Cited from Meharry Medical College.
first in nation: First medical school for African Americans in the South. Cited from Meharry Medical College.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

docs.summary: Established August 14, 1876, by the Texas Legislature as the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas for Colored Youths, opening at Prairie View in 1878. It was the first state-supported college for African Americans in Texas and is the state's second-oldest public institution of higher education.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1876. Cited from Prairie View A&M University.
first in state: First state-supported college in Texas for African Americans. Cited from Prairie View A&M University.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Chartered by Louisiana's Legislative Act 87 of 1880 through the efforts of Reconstruction-era leaders including P.B.S. Pinchback, T.T. Allain, and Henry Demas, opening in New Orleans with 12 students. Relocated in 1914 to a bluff above the Mississippi at Scotlandville, it anchors the nation's only HBCU system.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1880. Cited from Southern University and A&M College.
founded by: P.B.S. Pinchback, T.T. Allain, T.B. Stamps, and Henry Demas. Cited from Southern University and A&M College.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Opened April 11, 1881, in the basement of Atlanta's Friendship Baptist Church by New England educators Sophia B. Packard and Harriet E. Giles as the Atlanta Baptist Female Seminary. Renamed for the Spelman family in 1884, it became the nation's foremost college for women of African descent.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1881. Cited from Spelman College.
founded by: Sophia B. Packard and Harriet E. Giles. Cited from Spelman College.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Opened July 4, 1881, in a one-room shanty near Butler Chapel A.M.E. Zion Church with Booker T. Washington as founding principal. Students made the bricks and raised the buildings themselves; by Washington's death in 1915 the campus counted about 100 buildings, 1,500 students, and a $2 million endowment.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1881. Cited from Tuskegee University.
founded by: Booker T. Washington, founding principal, 1881. Cited from National Park Service.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Opened October 3, 1887, as the State Normal College for Colored Students in Tallahassee with 15 students and two instructors under founding president Thomas DeSaille Tucker. Designated Florida's land-grant institution for Black students in 1891 under the Second Morrill Act.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1887. Cited from Florida A&M University.
land grant designation: Florida's land-grant institution for African Americans under the Second Morrill Act, 1891. Cited from Florida A&M University.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Founded March 9, 1891, as the Agricultural and Mechanical College for the Colored Race under the Second Morrill Act, moving from Raleigh to donated land in Greensboro in 1893. On February 1, 1960, four freshmen remembered as the A&T Four began the Greensboro sit-ins at a downtown Woolworth lunch counter.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1891. Cited from North Carolina A&T State University.
notable event: Four A&T freshmen launched the Greensboro sit-ins on February 1, 1960. Cited from North Carolina A&T State University.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: Founded in New Orleans by St. Katharine Drexel and the Sisters of the Blessed Sacrament, opening as a high school in 1915 and establishing its College of Liberal Arts and Sciences in 1925. It is the nation's only historically Black and Catholic university and a leading source of Black graduates who earn medical degrees.

docs.summary: Organized in Harlem on August 25, 1925 under A. Philip Randolph, the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters became the first predominantly African American labor union chartered by the American Federation of Labor and the first to win a collective bargaining agreement with a major U.S. corporation, the Pullman Company, in 1937.

notabilityBasis.note: held convention: In September 1940 Eleanor Roosevelt addressed the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters convention at the Harlem YMCA, where Randolph and porter delegates pressed for defense-industry jobs and desegregation of the armed forces. Cited from National Park Service.

docs.summary: More than 1,200 Black union officials and rank-and-file members from thirty-seven national unions met at Chicago's LaSalle Hotel on September 23–24, 1972 to form the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists, creating a permanent caucus to advance Black workers' interests inside the AFL-CIO.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: On September 23–24, 1972, more than 1,200 Black union officials and rank-and-file members from thirty-seven international and national unions met at the LaSalle Hotel in Chicago and established the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists as a permanent organization. Cited from Coalition of Black Trade Unionists.

organized by: Five Black labor leaders — William Lucy, Nelson Edwards, William Simons, Charles Hayes, and Cleveland Robinson — convened the founding conference after the AFL-CIO executive council took a neutral stance in the 1972 presidential election. Cited from African American Registry.

docs.summary: Black labor leaders convened the founding convention of the Negro American Labor Council at Detroit's Statler-Hilton Hotel on May 27–29, 1960, electing A. Philip Randolph president to press the AFL-CIO to end segregated locals and elect Black workers to union leadership.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Dissatisfied with AFL-CIO President George Meany's civil-rights record, A. Philip Randolph and other African American labor leaders formed the Negro American Labor Council in 1960 to carry out the federation's civil-rights program from a Black workers' organization. held convention: The NALC founding convention met May 27–29, 1960 at the Statler-Hilton Hotel in Detroit, electing Randolph president and Cleveland Robinson of District 65 as vice president. Cited from Walter P. Reuther Library.

docs.summary: In December 1869, 214 delegates met in Washington, D.C. to form the Colored National Labor Union as a counterpart to the white National Labor Union, petitioning Congress for land redistribution and credit for Black farmers excluded from most trade unions.

petitioned congress: The 1869 convention petitioned Congress to subdivide public lands in the South into forty-acre farms and provide low-interest loans to Black farmers, and sent a further memorial in January 1871 on conditions in the southern states. Cited from National Park Service.
