docs.summary: Settled by Great Migration arrivals as Detroit's Black population grew from about 5,700 in 1910 to 120,000 by 1930, Black Bottom and Paradise Valley became home to more than 300 Black-owned businesses and a thriving nightclub scene before both neighborhoods were demolished in the early 1960s for highway construction.

notabilityBasis.note: grew during migration: As Detroit's Black population rose from about 5,700 in 1910 to roughly 120,000 two decades later, Great Migration arrivals combined with redlining to make Black Bottom and adjoining Paradise Valley a majority-Black community; by 1942 the two neighborhoods together held more than 300 Black-owned businesses, including bars, restaurants, doctors' offices, and hotels. Cited from detroithistorical.org.

demolished: In the early 1960s, Black Bottom and Paradise Valley were demolished for slum clearance and the construction of the I-375 freeway, displacing the community that had built one of the Midwest's most prosperous Black business districts. Cited from savingplaces.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: San Francisco's African American population grew nearly ninefold during World War II as Great Migration arrivals filled homes vacated by Japanese American internment, building the Fillmore District into 'Harlem of the West,' a jazz district that drew Louis Armstrong, Billie Holiday, and Ella Fitzgerald before 1960s-70s redevelopment displaced thousands of Black families.

notabilityBasis.note: grew during migration: San Francisco's Black population grew from 4,836 to 43,460 between 1940 and 1950, with a large majority settling in the Fillmore District, drawn by wartime shipbuilding jobs and housing vacated by Japanese American families forced into internment camps in 1942. Cited from blackpast.org.

known as: In the 1940s and 1950s the Fillmore was known as the 'Harlem of the West' for its concentration of jazz clubs hosting performers including Louis Armstrong, John Coltrane, Ella Fitzgerald, Billie Holiday, and Charlie Parker, before 1960s-70s urban renewal demolished 883 businesses and 2,500 Victorian houses and displaced roughly 4,700 Black families. Cited from soulofamerica.com.

docs.summary: Anchored by William Grose's 1882 land purchase, Seattle's Central District grew from a few hundred Black residents in 1900 to become, by the 1970s, the heart of Black Seattle and the center of the city's civil rights movement, as Great Migration arrivals were channeled there by redlining.

notabilityBasis.note: settled by: Seattle's Black community began settling the Central District in the 1890s after William Grose, a formerly enslaved man, purchased 12 acres of Madison Valley land from Henry Yesler in 1882 and sold lots to Black families arriving in the late 1880s and 1890s. Cited from historylink.org.

grew during migration: The neighborhood's Black population grew tenfold from about 400 in 1900 to 3,789 by 1940, then accelerated further during the World War II-era Second Great Migration as war-industry jobs and redlining concentrated Black settlement there; by 1970, Black residents made up nearly 80 percent of the Central District. Cited from systemicjustice.org.

docs.summary: Pittsburgh's Hill District grew from about 10,000 Black residents in 1890 to more than 37,000 by 1920 as Great Migration arrivals came for steel-mill jobs, building a jazz scene that hosted Duke Ellington, Louis Armstrong, and homegrown talents Lena Horne, Billy Eckstine, and Earl 'Fatha' Hines.

notabilityBasis.note: grew during migration: Rising labor demand from Pittsburgh's steel mills and World War I fueled Great Migration arrivals to the Hill District, whose Black population grew from around 10,000 in 1890 to more than 37,000 by 1920. Cited from aaregistry.org.

became cultural center: From the 1930s to the 1950s the Hill District was one of the most prosperous and influential Black communities in America, its jazz clubs hosting national touring artists including Duke Ellington and Louis Armstrong alongside homegrown talents Lena Horne, Billy Eckstine, and Earl 'Fatha' Hines, leading Harlem Renaissance poet Claude McKay to call it the 'Crossroads of the World'. Cited from hilldistrict.org.

docs.summary: From the late 1800s, Rondo was the heart of St. Paul's African American community, home by the 1950s to 85 percent of the city's Black residents and institutions including Pilgrim Baptist Church and the Hallie Q. Brown Community Center, before Interstate 94's construction demolished its core between 1956 and 1968.

notabilityBasis.note: anchored community: From the late 1800s, Rondo was an important center of the Twin Cities' Black community; by the 1930s it was the heart of St. Paul's African American population and home to institutions including Pilgrim Baptist Church, the Hallie Q. Brown Community Center, and the Sterling Club, with about 85 percent of St. Paul's Black residents living there by the 1950s. Cited from mnhs.org.

demolished for highway: The core of Rondo was demolished between 1956 and 1968 to build Interstate 94 along a route chosen through the heart of the neighborhood rather than a less disruptive path along abandoned railroad tracks, displacing at least 650 families; in 2016 Minnesota's transportation commissioner and St. Paul's mayor formally apologized for how the project was handled. Cited from minnpost.com.

docs.summary: Home to more than 20,000 Black St. Louisans by the early 1950s and cultural institutions like the Phillis Wheatley YWCA, Mill Creek Valley was demolished by the city beginning in 1959, destroying 5,600 housing units, 800 businesses, and 40 churches to build a highway — one of the largest single acts of urban-renewal displacement in U.S. history.

notabilityBasis.note: anchored community: By the early 1950s more than 20,000 Black St. Louisans lived in Mill Creek Valley, a neighborhood in the city's central corridor whose Phillis Wheatley YWCA branch hosted the Fisk Jubilee Singers in 1916 and a W.E.B. Du Bois lecture in 1922, and whose residents included Josephine Baker and Scott Joplin. Cited from stlpr.org.

demolished: Beginning with Mayor Raymond Tucker's 1954 announcement, the city demolished Mill Creek Valley starting in 1959, destroying roughly 5,600 housing units, 800 businesses, and 40 churches across 54 city blocks to build a highway and new development, displacing virtually the entire neighborhood. Cited from pbs.org.

docs.summary: Settled around 1900 by Black Bahamian and Southern migrants who built Henry Flagler's railroad, Overtown grew into the 'Harlem of the South,' a thriving entertainment district that hosted Black performers barred from segregated Miami Beach hotels, before Interstate 95's construction displaced roughly 40,000 residents.

notabilityBasis.note: settled by: At the turn of the twentieth century, Black Bahamian immigrants and Southern Black migrants who built Henry Flagler's Florida East Coast Railway settled in what became known as Overtown, or 'Colored Town,' northwest of Miami's city core. Cited from visitflorida.com.

declined due to highway: In its golden age from the 1930s through the 1950s, Overtown was known as the 'Harlem of the South,' with clubs and hotels hosting performers including Count Basie, Ella Fitzgerald, Billie Holiday, and Nat King Cole, who were barred from staying in segregated Miami Beach; construction of Interstate 95 then forced the relocation of more than 12,000 residents, nearly all Black, dropping the neighborhood's population from roughly 50,000 to around 10,000. Cited from rachelsruminations.com.

docs.summary: Incorporated in 1903 as a mixed working-class community, Watts became two-thirds Black by 1945 as Second Great Migration arrivals, many recruited for wartime defense jobs, settled there under housing restrictions that barred Black Angelenos from most of the city, up from just 14 percent Black in 1920.

notabilityBasis.note: settled by: Watts, incorporated in 1903, had the highest concentration of African Americans in California by 1920 at 14 percent of its population, many working as Pullman porters and other railroad employees. Cited from blackpast.org.

transformed by migration: During the World War II-era Second Great Migration, tens of thousands of Black workers and families, mostly from Louisiana, Mississippi, Arkansas, and Texas, moved to Los Angeles for defense-industry jobs; by 1945 African Americans made up two-thirds of Watts's population, reflecting housing restrictions that barred Black Angelenos from most other neighborhoods. Cited from nmaahc.si.edu.

docs.summary: Born enslaved in Mississippi in 1862, Ida B. Wells became a pioneering investigative journalist whose 1892 anti-lynching crusade, launched after a mob murdered three of her Memphis friends, used data and firsthand reporting to challenge lynching's justifications and helped lay groundwork for the NAACP.

notabilityBasis.note: launched crusade: After a white mob lynched her friend Thomas Moss and two of his business partners in Memphis in March 1892, Ida B. Wells used her newspaper, Free Speech, to launch a data-driven anti-lynching crusade documenting hundreds of cases; a mob destroyed her printing office in retaliation while she was traveling in the North. Cited from nps.gov.

resided at: Wells settled permanently in Chicago in 1893, continuing her anti-lynching and civil rights organizing and co-founding what became the NAACP; she and her husband Ferdinand Barnett purchased the Romanesque Revival house at 3624 South King Drive in 1919, designated a National Historic Landmark in 1974. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Robert Sengstacke Abbott founded the Chicago Defender in 1905 and built it into the nation's most influential Black newspaper, using its editorials, cartoons, and job listings to actively recruit hundreds of thousands of Southern Black readers to migrate north during the Great Migration.

notabilityBasis.note: founded and promoted: Robert Sengstacke Abbott founded the Chicago Defender in 1905 and, through strong editorials and political cartoons promoting Chicago and advertising successful Black Chicagoans as models, played a major role in driving the Great Migration; he declared May 15, 1917 the date of a 'Great Northern Drive' urging Southern Black readers north. Cited from britannica.com.

resided at: Abbott lived from 1926 until his death in 1940 at the house at 4742 South Martin Luther King Jr. Drive in Chicago's Grand Boulevard neighborhood, designated a National Historic Landmark in 1976. Cited from loc.gov.

docs.summary: From late May to early July 1917, white mobs in East St. Louis, Illinois attacked Black residents whose Great Migration arrival for wartime industrial jobs had stoked labor tensions, killing at least 39 Black residents, burning entire blocks, and displacing about 6,000 people in one of the era's deadliest race massacres.

notabilityBasis.note: occurred: Racial tensions rose after February 1917, when 470 Black workers were hired to replace striking white workers at the Aluminum Ore Company amid a wave of 10,000-12,000 Black Southerners arriving in East St. Louis during 1916-1917; on July 2, 1917, white mobs beat and shot Black residents and set fire to their homes across the city. Cited from blackpast.org.

prompted protest: The massacre's official death toll was 39 Black residents and nine white residents, though some estimates run far higher, and about 6,000 Black residents were displaced; in response, the NAACP and W.E.B. Du Bois organized ten thousand Black New Yorkers in a Silent Parade down Fifth Avenue on July 28, 1917. Cited from britannica.com.

docs.summary: After a white man stoned Black teenager Eugene Williams to death for drifting into a segregated section of Lake Michigan on July 27, 1919, a week of mob violence swept Chicago's South Side, killing 38 people and leaving thousands of Black Chicagoans, whose Great Migration-era arrival had reshaped the city, homeless.

notabilityBasis.note: resulted in: Over the week of violence from July 27 to August 3, 1919, 38 people died (23 Black and 15 white), 537 were injured, and between 1,000 and 2,000 residents, most of them Black, lost their homes, making it the worst of the 'Red Summer' riots of 1919 and the deadliest race riot in Illinois history. Cited from en.wikipedia.org.

sparked by: On July 27, 1919, 17-year-old Eugene Williams drowned after a white man threw rocks at him for drifting on a raft across an invisible line separating a segregated 'whites only' section of a Lake Michigan beach; a white police officer's refusal to arrest the attacker or let a Black officer do so ignited the Chicago race riot. Cited from chicagohistory.org.

docs.summary: In September 1919, Black sharecroppers near Elaine, Arkansas organized the Progressive Farmers and Household Union to demand fair payment for their cotton; white mobs and federal troops responded with a massacre that killed an estimated hundreds of Black residents, one of the deadliest racial confrontations in U.S. history.

notabilityBasis.note: organized: In the vicinity of Elaine, in rural Phillips County, Arkansas, Black sharecroppers organized the Progressive Farmers and Household Union of America to demand fair prices for their cotton crops and challenge peonage in tenant farming; a shooting at a September 30, 1919 union meeting escalated into mob violence by area whites. Cited from encyclopediaofarkansas.net.

resulted in: Estimates of Black residents killed by white mobs and federal troops range into the hundreds, compared to five white deaths; more than 100 Black men were indicted afterward and 12 sentenced to death, but the NAACP's years-long legal campaign won their acquittal, including the landmark 1923 Supreme Court case Moore v. Dempsey. Cited from smithsonianmag.com.

docs.summary: During the first week of January 1923, white mobs destroyed Rosewood, Florida, a self-sufficient Black community of homeowners and business owners, killing at least six Black residents; in 1994 Florida became the first U.S. state legislature to pay reparations to massacre survivors and their descendants.

notabilityBasis.note: destroyed: Rosewood was a predominantly Black community in Levy County, Florida whose residents owned homes and businesses and supported a school, three churches, and a Masonic lodge; following an unsubstantiated accusation against a Black man in nearby Sumner on January 1, 1923, white mobs terrorized and killed Rosewood residents over the following week, and an official 1993 study documented at least eight deaths, six Black and two white. Cited from dos.fl.gov.

compensated by: In 1994, the Florida legislature passed a bill awarding $150,000 to each of the nine living Rosewood survivors along with smaller sums for descendants, the first reparations legislation of its kind passed by any U.S. state government; a Florida Heritage Landmark historical marker was dedicated at the site in 2004. Cited from history.com.

docs.summary: On November 10, 1898, a mob of white supremacists in Wilmington, North Carolina overthrew the city's legitimately elected biracial Fusionist government, killed as many as 60 Black residents, and destroyed Black-owned businesses including the city's only Black newspaper, in the only successful coup d'état in U.S. history.

notabilityBasis.note: resulted in: A 2006 state-commissioned report placed the death toll at as many as 60, all Black; roughly 2,100 Black Wilmingtonians left the city in the following weeks, and North Carolina did not elect another Republican governor until 1972 nor another Black congressman until 1992. Cited from britannica.com.

overthrew government: On November 10, 1898, a mob of roughly 2,000 white men led by Democratic Party organizers overthrew Wilmington's legitimately elected, biracial Fusionist government, expelled Black and white political leaders from the city, and destroyed Black-owned property including the offices of the Daily Record, the city's only Black newspaper. Cited from ncpedia.org.

docs.summary: In 1879, as many as 40,000 formerly enslaved 'Exodusters' left Mississippi, Louisiana, and other former Confederate states for Kansas, Oklahoma, and Colorado, organizing the first mass Black migration after Reconstruction's collapse to escape racial violence, debt peonage, and the loss of political rights.

notabilityBasis.note: motivated by: The Exodusters' main motivations were escaping racial violence and 'bulldozing' by white supremacist groups including the Ku Klux Klan and White League, along with discriminatory Black Codes that had rendered formerly enslaved people second-class citizens after federal Reconstruction troops withdrew from the South in 1877. Cited from history.com.

migrated: In 1879, tens of thousands of African Americans, later called Exodusters, left Mississippi, Louisiana, Texas, and other former Confederate states for Kansas and neighboring territories; estimates of participants range from 20,000 to 40,000 over the following two years, marking the first general Black migration following Reconstruction's end. Cited from archives.gov.

docs.summary: A formerly enslaved Tennessean who organized the Edgefield Real Estate and Homestead Association in 1874, Benjamin 'Pap' Singleton became the leading promoter of the 1879 Kansas Exodus, personally guiding thousands of Black Tennesseans to found the Singleton Colony near Dunlap, Kansas.

notabilityBasis.note: organized: Benjamin Singleton, who had escaped slavery near Nashville, Tennessee in 1846, organized the Edgefield Real Estate and Homestead Association in 1874 to encourage cooperative Black migration to Kansas, and in spring 1878 led settlers by steamboat from Tennessee to found a colony near Dunlap, on the former Kaw Reservation on the Morris-Lyon county line. Cited from plainshumanities.unl.edu.

led migration: More than 2,400 settlers from Davidson and Sumner counties, Tennessee eventually emigrated to the Dunlap colony that Singleton founded, part of the broader 1879 Kansas Exodus in which he was the most prominent individual organizer and promoter. Cited from legendsofkansas.com.

docs.summary: On June 2, 1863, Harriet Tubman guided Union gunboats up South Carolina's Combahee River, becoming the first woman to plan and lead an armed U.S. military expedition; the raid liberated more than 750 enslaved people and destroyed Confederate supplies and infrastructure along the river.

notabilityBasis.note: liberated: The raid's Union gunboats rescued and transported more than 750 formerly enslaved people from riverside plantations, many of whom went on to join the Union Army; South Carolina's legislature named a new Combahee River bridge the Harriet Tubman Memorial Bridge in 2006 in recognition of the raid. Cited from blackpast.org.

led: On June 1-2, 1863, Harriet Tubman guided Union Army Colonel James Montgomery and roughly 150 soldiers of the 2nd South Carolina Infantry, a Black regiment, on a raid up the Combahee River to clear torpedoes, seize plantation supplies, and destroy Confederate infrastructure, making her the first woman known to plan and lead a U.S. armed military operation. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Marcus Garvey organized the first U.S. chapter of the Universal Negro Improvement Association in Harlem in May 1917, and by 1919 its Liberty Hall headquarters on West 138th Street was filling with up to 6,000 members promoting Black economic self-sufficiency, racial pride, and pan-African nationhood.

notabilityBasis.note: headquartered at: On July 20, 1919, the UNIA dedicated Liberty Hall at 120 West 138th Street as its New York headquarters, a converted former Baptist tabernacle that filled with up to 6,000 people for weekly meetings and hosted the 1920 International Convention of the Negro Peoples of the World. Cited from hnba.nyc.

founded: Marcus Garvey, who had founded the Universal Negro Improvement Association in Jamaica in 1914, organized its first American chapter in Harlem in May 1917 with the help of his secretary Amy Ashwood; by 1918 the New York chapter had 3,500 members. Cited from britannica.com.

docs.summary: Conceived in 1936 by Harlem postal worker Victor Hugo Green, this annual guidebook listed hotels, restaurants, and service stations that welcomed Black travelers, helping African American motorists navigate Jim Crow-era travel safely for three decades until its final edition in 1966.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Victor Hugo Green, a Harlem mail carrier and World War I veteran, conceived The Negro Motorist Green Book in 1936, modeling it on a similar guide for Jewish travelers, to give Black travelers 'information that will keep him from running into difficulties, embarrassments and to make his trip more enjoyable'. Cited from loc.gov.

provided safety: Published annually from 1936 to 1966, the Green Book listed hotels, restaurants, and service stations across the country where Black travelers would be welcomed, a crucial resource as the emerging Black middle class purchased automobiles during the Great Migration era while facing arbitrary discrimination and danger on the road. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: In January 1941, A. Philip Randolph, founder of the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters, threatened to bring 100,000 Black Americans to march on Washington against discrimination in wartime defense industries; the threat pressured President Roosevelt into signing Executive Order 8802, the first presidential directive on race since Reconstruction.

notabilityBasis.note: organized: On January 25, 1941, A. Philip Randolph, president of the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters, proposed a March on Washington to protest discrimination against Black workers in the national defense industry and armed forces, organizing to bring an estimated 100,000 marchers to the capital that summer. Cited from civilrightsteaching.org.

resulted in: To avert the march, President Franklin Roosevelt signed Executive Order 8802 on June 25, 1941, banning discriminatory employment practices in the defense industry and federal government and creating the Fair Employment Practices Committee, the first presidential directive addressing race since Reconstruction; Randolph called off the march but continued the movement to pressure for enforcement. Cited from archives.gov.

docs.summary: After buying a small fraternal-order newsletter in 1910, publisher P.B. Young built the Norfolk Journal and Guide into the largest Black newspaper in the South by 1928 and the South's single largest Black-owned employer by World War II, chronicling Great Migration-era Black life in Virginia and beyond.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: P.B. Young borrowed $3,000 in 1910 to purchase a small fraternal-order newsletter, the Lodge Journal and Guide, and renamed it the Norfolk Journal and Guide; by 1928 it had grown into the largest Black newspaper in the American South. Cited from en.wikipedia.org.

grew influence: By the start of World War II, the Norfolk Journal and Guide was the single largest employer of Black people in the South and reached a circulation of more than 80,000 by the 1940s under Young, known as the 'Dean of the Negro Press'. Cited from pbs.org.

docs.summary: Believed to be the first Black woman to own and operate a newspaper in the United States, Charlotta Bass ran Los Angeles's California Eagle from 1912 to 1951, using it to confront the Ku Klux Klan, housing discrimination, and police brutality before becoming the first Black woman nominated for U.S. vice president in 1952.

notabilityBasis.note: confronted: Bass used the California Eagle to combat discriminatory hiring practices, restrictive housing covenants, and police brutality against Los Angeles's Great Migration-era Black community, and in 1925 published a letter exposing a Ku Klux Klan plot to frame local Black leaders, a case in which the courts sided with Bass and the paper. Cited from laul.org.

acquired and led: Charlotta Bass took over The California Owl, founded in 1879, after founder John J. Neimore's death in 1912, renaming it the California Eagle and running it until 1951; she is believed to be the first Black woman to own and operate a newspaper in the United States, and by the 1930s the Eagle was the largest Black newspaper on the West Coast with a circulation near 60,000. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Wyomia Tyus won the Olympic 100m in 1964 and again in 1968, setting a world record of 11.08 seconds in her Mexico City title defense to become the first person, male or female, to win back-to-back Olympic 100m titles. She was inducted into the U.S. Olympic Hall of Fame in 1985.

notabilityBasis.note: became first back to back 100m champion year: 1968, defending her 1964 Olympic 100m title in Mexico City with a world-record 11.08 seconds, the first person of either sex to win back-to-back Olympic 100m titles. Cited from worldathletics.org.
hall of fame induction year: 1985, inducted into the U.S. Olympic Hall of Fame. Cited from teamusa.com.

docs.summary: At the 2012 London Olympics, 16-year-old Gabby Douglas became the first Black woman to win the Olympic all-around title in gymnastics, edging Russia's Viktoria Komova, while also helping Team USA win team gold — making her the first American gymnast to win both titles at the same Games.

notabilityBasis.note: collection held by: The Smithsonian's National Museum of American History holds an exhibition on Douglas's career and 2012 Olympic leotard. Cited from americanhistory.si.edu.
became first black woman allaround gold year: 2012, winning the Olympic individual all-around gymnastics title in London, the first Black woman to do so. Cited from nbcsports.com.

docs.summary: Tommie Smith won gold in the 200m at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics in a world-record 19.83 seconds, then, with bronze medalist John Carlos, raised a black-gloved fist during the medal ceremony to protest racial injustice — one of the most enduring images in sports history. A San Jose State alumnus, Smith was inducted with Carlos into the U.S. Olympic and Paralympic Hall of Fame in 2019.

notabilityBasis.note: won gold and protested date: October 16, 1968, winning the 200m gold medal in a world record 19.83 seconds and raising a fist during the medal ceremony. Cited from sjsu.edu.
hall of fame induction year: 2019, inducted into the U.S. Olympic and Paralympic Hall of Fame. Cited from sjsu.edu.

docs.summary: John Carlos won bronze in the 200m at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics, then joined gold medalist Tommie Smith in raising a black-gloved fist during the medal ceremony to protest racial injustice, an act that led to their suspension from the U.S. team. A San Jose State alumnus, Carlos was inducted into the U.S. Olympic and Paralympic Hall of Fame alongside Smith in 2019.

notabilityBasis.note: won bronze and protested date: October 16, 1968, winning bronze in the 200m and joining Tommie Smith's raised-fist protest during the medal ceremony. Cited from sjsu.edu.

docs.summary: Gwendolyn Brooks became the first Black person to win a Pulitzer Prize on May 1, 1950, awarded for Poetry for 'Annie Allen,' a collection centered on a young Black girl coming of age in Chicago's Bronzeville neighborhood. She lived and wrote many of her best-known works, including 'The Bean Eaters' and 'In the Mecca,' at her South Side Chicago home from 1953 to 1994.

notabilityBasis.note: became first black pulitzer winner date: May 1, 1950, winning the Pulitzer Prize for Poetry for 'Annie Allen,' the first Black person to win a Pulitzer Prize. Cited from pulitzer.org.
site designation year: 2010, her home at 7428 South Evans Avenue, where she lived from 1953 to 1994 and wrote many major works, was designated a Chicago Landmark. Cited from southsideweekly.com.

docs.summary: Rita Dove won the Pulitzer Prize for Poetry in 1987 for 'Thomas and Beulah,' becoming the second Black person to win the award for poetry after Gwendolyn Brooks. In 1993, at age 40, she was appointed the first Black U.S. Poet Laureate Consultant in Poetry to the Library of Congress, and the youngest person to hold the post.

notabilityBasis.note: became first black poet laureate year: 1993, appointed the first Black U.S. Poet Laureate Consultant in Poetry, serving until 1995. Cited from loc.gov.
pulitzer prize year: 1987, won the Pulitzer Prize for Poetry for 'Thomas and Beulah'. Cited from britannica.com.

docs.summary: Alice Walker won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction on April 18, 1983, for 'The Color Purple,' becoming the first Black woman to win a Pulitzer for fiction; she also won the National Book Award for the same novel. Walker, the youngest of eight children of Georgia sharecroppers, grew up in Eatonton, the rural setting that shaped the world of her best-known book.

notabilityBasis.note: birthplace and upbringing: Born in and raised in Eatonton, Georgia, the youngest of eight children of sharecroppers; the town maintains a self-guided Alice Walker Driving Tour of sites from her childhood. Cited from visiteatonton.com.
became first black woman pulitzer fiction date: April 18, 1983, won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction for 'The Color Purple,' the first Black woman to win the fiction prize. Cited from pulitzer.org.

docs.summary: Enslaved as a child and brought to Boston around 1761, Phillis Wheatley became the first African American, and the first enslaved person in the American colonies, to publish a book of poetry: 'Poems on Various Subjects, Religious and Moral,' printed in London in September 1773 after Boston publishers declined it. She was freed shortly after the book's publication.

notabilityBasis.note: became first published black author date: September 1, 1773, publication in London of 'Poems on Various Subjects, Religious and Moral,' the first book of poetry published by an African American. Cited from nps.gov.
worship site commemorated at: A statue and first-edition copy of her book are displayed at the Old South Meeting House, where she was a worshiping member. Cited from npg.si.edu.

docs.summary: Jordan Peele won the Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay at the 90th Academy Awards on March 4, 2018, for 'Get Out,' becoming the first Black writer to win the award. The horror film, Peele's directorial debut, also earned him nominations for Best Director and Best Picture.

notabilityBasis.note: became first black screenplay oscar winner date: March 4, 2018, winning Best Original Screenplay for 'Get Out' at the 90th Academy Awards, held at the Dolby Theatre. Cited from cbsnews.com.
additional nominations: 'Get Out' also earned Peele Academy Award nominations for Best Director and Best Picture the same night. Cited from variety.com.

docs.summary: Denzel Washington won the Academy Award for Best Actor on March 24, 2002, for 'Training Day,' becoming the second Black man to win the category after Sidney Poitier's win 38 years earlier. Washington won his first Oscar, for Best Supporting Actor, in 1990 for 'Glory,' and the same 2002 ceremony saw Halle Berry become the first Black woman to win Best Actress.

notabilityBasis.note: became second black best actor date: March 24, 2002, winning Best Actor for 'Training Day,' 38 years after Sidney Poitier's 1964 win. Cited from oscars.org.
first oscar year: 1990, won Best Supporting Actor for 'Glory'. Cited from oscars.org.

docs.summary: Whoopi Goldberg won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress on March 25, 1991, for 'Ghost,' becoming the first Black performer to win an Oscar since Hattie McDaniel 51 years earlier and the first Black person nominated in both lead and supporting acting categories. By 2002 she completed an EGOT, becoming the first Black woman to win all four major American entertainment awards.

notabilityBasis.note: won oscar date: March 25, 1991, winning Best Supporting Actress for 'Ghost' at the 63rd Academy Awards, held at the Dorothy Chandler Pavilion. Cited from variety.com.
completed egot year: 2002, completed an EGOT (Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, Tony), the first Black woman to do so. Cited from britannica.com.

docs.summary: Signed to Motown as a child prodigy billed as 'Little Stevie Wonder,' he topped the Billboard Hot 100 on August 10, 1963 with 'Fingertips – Pt. 2' at age 13, becoming the youngest solo artist ever to have a number-one single, a feat also making its live album the youngest artist to top the pop albums chart. He went on to win 25 Grammy Awards, among the most of any solo artist.

notabilityBasis.note: became youngest number one solo artist date: August 10, 1963, 'Fingertips – Pt. 2' topped the Billboard Hot 100, making 13-year-old Stevie Wonder the youngest solo artist to reach number one. Cited from billboard.com.
signed to label as child: Signed to Motown Records as a child prodigy, recording and developing his early career at the label's Hitsville U.S.A. studio in Detroit. Cited from motownmuseum.org.

docs.summary: The Birmingham Campaign was a spring 1963 SCLC-led direct-action movement in Birmingham, Alabama, combining sit-ins, marches, and the Children's Crusade to desegregate the city, producing King's "Letter from Birmingham Jail" and images of Bull Connor's fire hoses and police dogs.

notabilityBasis.note: occurred in: The Birmingham Campaign ran from April 3, 1963 to May 10, 1963, when King and the SCLC joined Birmingham's existing local movement, the Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights (ACMHR), in mass direct action against the city's segregation system.. Cited from kinginstitute.stanford.edu.

led by: The campaign was led by Martin Luther King Jr. and Reverends James Bevel and Fred Shuttlesworth, with Shuttlesworth's ACMHR organizing the local groundwork before SCLC joined in April 1963.. Cited from blackpast.org.

key event: Arrested on Good Friday, April 12, 1963, for violating an anti-protest injunction, King was held in solitary confinement, where he wrote "Letter from Birmingham Jail" in the margins of a newspaper responding to eight white clergymen's public statement condemning the protests; he was released on bail April 20, 1963.. Cited from blackpast.org.

outcome: On May 2, 1963, over 1,000 Black students marched in the Children's Crusade; the following days Commissioner Eugene "Bull" Connor's police and firefighters used high-pressure fire hoses, clubs, and dogs against the young demonstrators, and on May 10, 1963, city and business leaders agreed to desegregate lunch counters, restrooms, and fitting rooms within ninety days and to hire Black employees in downtown stores.. Cited from blackpast.org.

docs.summary: Educator and reformer born enslaved in Virginia who founded and led Tuskegee Institute from 1881 until his death in 1915, becoming the era's most influential Black leader and author of Up from Slavery.

notabilityBasis.note: founded in: Washington and a small group opened the Tuskegee Normal and Industrial School on July 4, 1881, in Tuskegee, Alabama, with no initial buildings or land.. Cited from encyclopediaofalabama.org.
born in: Born into slavery on April 5, 1856, in Hale's Ford, Franklin County, Virginia; died November 14, 1915, in Tuskegee, Alabama, at age 59.. Cited from encyclopediaofalabama.org.

delivered: Delivered the 'Atlanta Compromise' address at the Cotton States and International Exposition in Atlanta, Georgia, in 1895, arguing Black and white Southerners could be 'as separate as the fingers, yet one as the hand' in social matters.. Cited from encyclopediaofalabama.org.
authored: Published his autobiography Up from Slavery in 1901, a bestselling account that shaped early 20th-century African American public discourse.. Cited from encyclopediaofalabama.org.

docs.summary: The Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) was founded in 1942 in Chicago by an interracial group of pacifists to pursue nonviolent direct action against segregation, later organizing the 1947 Journey of Reconciliation and the pivotal 1961 Freedom Rides under national director James Farmer.

notabilityBasis.note: organized: In April 1947, CORE and the Fellowship of Reconciliation jointly organized the Journey of Reconciliation, an interracial bus-riding test of the Supreme Court's Morgan v. Virginia ruling banning segregated seating in interstate travel; the two-week trip through Virginia, North Carolina, Tennessee, and Kentucky is considered a precursor to the 1961 Freedom Rides.. Cited from snccdigital.org.

founded in: Founded in 1942 by an interracial group of pacifists at the University of Chicago; members staged sit-ins at Chicago-area restaurants and challenged restrictive housing covenants before establishing a Southern presence in 1957.. Cited from blackpast.org.

led by: In 1961, CORE's newly installed national director James Farmer, Jr. organized the Freedom Rides to test enforcement of Boynton v. Virginia; interracial teams of riders left Washington, D.C. on May 4, 1961 and met mob violence in Anniston, Birmingham, and Montgomery, Alabama.. Cited from blackpast.org.

participated in: CORE cosponsored the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom, and in 1964 its volunteers took part in Mississippi Freedom Summer, a project to register Black voters that saw three CORE workers murdered by Klansmen in Philadelphia, Mississippi.. Cited from blackpast.org.

docs.summary: A flowering of African American literature, music, art, and intellectual life centered in Harlem, New York City, spanning roughly the late 1910s through the mid-1930s, defined by figures such as Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, and Duke Ellington.

notabilityBasis.note: centered in: The Harlem Renaissance was centered in the Harlem neighborhood of New York City, lasting roughly from the 1910s through the mid-1930s, and is widely regarded as a golden age of African American culture fueled by the Great Migration, which brought some 300,000 African Americans from the South to the North by 1920, with Harlem among the most popular destinations.. Cited from history.com.

published in: The NAACP's magazine The Crisis, where Jessie Redmon Fauset served as literary editor, and the National Urban League's magazine Opportunity, founded and edited by sociologist Charles S. Johnson, were key literary outlets that promoted Harlem Renaissance writers including Langston Hughes.. Cited from history.com.
featured performances by: Harlem's Cotton Club, one of the era's most successful nightspots, featured frequent performances by jazz musicians Duke Ellington and Cab Calloway.. Cited from history.com.

supported by: Beginning in 1925, bibliophile Arturo Alfonso Schomburg's donated collection of African diaspora materials at the New York Public Library's 135th Street branch in Harlem became a foundational intellectual resource for Harlem Renaissance figures including Langston Hughes, later growing into the Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture.. Cited from nypl.org.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

docs.summary: A clandestine network of routes, safe houses, and abolitionist "conductors" that helped enslaved people escape to freedom in the northern United States and Canada, operating most actively from the early 19th century until the Civil War's end in 1865.

notabilityBasis.note: active during: The Underground Railroad's resistance-through-escape efforts spanned from the era of enslavement through the end of the Civil War, with organization intensifying after passage of the Fugitive Slave Act of 1850.. Cited from nps.gov.

led by: Harriet Tubman served as an Underground Railroad conductor, making roughly 13 rescue trips into Maryland between 1849 and 1859 and personally guiding about 70 enslaved people to freedom.. Cited from nps.gov.

associated with: William Still, chairman of the Vigilance Committee of the Pennsylvania Anti-Slavery Society, helped nearly 1,000 freedom seekers pass through Philadelphia and documented 846 of their accounts in his 1872 book The Underground Railroad.. Cited from nps.gov.

commemorated at: Harriet Tubman Underground Railroad National Historical Park, at 4068 Golden Hill Road in Church Creek, Maryland, preserves the Eastern Shore landscapes Tubman traversed while conducting enslaved people to freedom.. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.displayName: National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP)
docs.nameLower: national association for the advancement of colored people (naacp)
docs.summary: Founded February 12, 1909, in New York City by an interracial group including W. E. B. Du Bois and Ida B. Wells-Barnett, the NAACP is the oldest and largest civil rights organization in the United States, pursuing legal, legislative, and public advocacy against racial discrimination.

notabilityBasis.note: led legal campaign resulting in: The NAACP's legal campaign culminated in the Supreme Court's 1954 decision in Brown v. Board of Education, which overturned the 'separate but equal' doctrine of Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) and declared school segregation unconstitutional.. Cited from naacp.org.

founded in: The NAACP was founded on February 12, 1909, in New York City by an interracial group of activists, including W. E. B. Du Bois and Ida B. Wells-Barnett, in response to the 1908 Springfield, Illinois race riot; the announcement was timed to coincide with the centennial of Abraham Lincoln's birth.. Cited from history.com.
published: The NAACP launched The Crisis in 1910 under founding editor W. E. B. Du Bois as the organization's official publication.. Cited from naacp.org.
headquartered in: The NAACP's national headquarters is located at 4805 Mt. Hope Drive, Baltimore, Maryland 21215, after originating from its 1909 offices in New York City.. Cited from naacp.org.

docs.summary: The Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC) is a civil rights organization officially inaugurated in Atlanta, Georgia on January 10-11, 1957, growing out of the Montgomery bus boycott, with Martin Luther King Jr. as its first president.

notabilityBasis.note: founded in: SCLC was officially inaugurated in Atlanta, Georgia, at Ebenezer Baptist Church on January 10-11, 1957, growing out of the Montgomery Improvement Association's bus boycott.. Cited from nps.gov.

organized: SCLC's 1963 Birmingham demonstrations, one of its most significant campaigns against segregation, were planned at the Dorchester Center in Liberty County, Georgia.. Cited from georgiaencyclopedia.org; nps.gov.
led by: Martin Luther King Jr. served as SCLC's first president from its founding in 1957 until his assassination in 1968; Ralph Abernathy succeeded him and led until 1977.. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: SNCC was a student-led civil rights organization founded in April 1960 at Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina, that coordinated sit-ins, Freedom Rides, and Southern voter registration drives before ceasing to exist by 1973.

notabilityBasis.note: founded in: Ella Baker, a Shaw University alumna and SCLC official, organized an April 1960 conference at Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina, out of which the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee emerged.. Cited from nps.gov.

organized: SNCC organized 1961 Freedom Rides to test desegregation of interstate travel, launched Southern voter registration campaigns from 1962 onward, and led the 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer project.. Cited from archives.gov.

dissolved in: After Stokely Carmichael's 1966 election as chairman and the 1967 expulsion of white members caused steep funding losses, SNCC lost nearly all of its staff and chapters by 1970, and by 1973 the organization no longer existed.. Cited from archives.gov.

led by: SNCC's founding conference drew 126 student delegates; the Nashville contingent of Diane Nash, John Lewis, and Marion Barry (SNCC's first chairman) shaped its early commitment to nonviolence, and the group began publishing the newsletter The Student Voice.. Cited from snccdigital.org.

docs.summary: W. E. B. Du Bois (1868-1963) was a sociologist, historian, and civil rights leader who co-founded the NAACP in 1909, founded and edited its magazine The Crisis from 1910 to 1934, and authored the landmark essay collection The Souls of Black Folk (1903).

notabilityBasis.note: born in: W. E. B. Du Bois was born William Edward Burghardt Du Bois on February 23, 1868, in Great Barrington, Massachusetts.. Cited from duboisnhs.org.
died in: Du Bois died on August 27, 1963, in Accra, Ghana, at the age of 95, one day before the March on Washington.. Cited from historians.org.
co founded: Du Bois co-founded the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) in 1909 and served on its board while directing publicity and research.. Cited from nps.gov.

founded and edited: Du Bois founded and edited The Crisis, the NAACP's official magazine, from 1910 until 1934, growing its circulation to roughly 100,000 by 1920 before resigning over disagreements with NAACP leadership.. Cited from naacp.org.

docs.summary: Re-established in 1867 on the San Antonio–El Paso Road, Fort Davis became one of the largest frontier posts where Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th and 10th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry garrisoned, patrolled, and built infrastructure across West Texas until the 1890s. The restored post is a National Park Service unit documenting African American service on the frontier.

notabilityBasis.note: garrisoned by: After the Civil War, Fort Davis was reoccupied in 1867 and became a major frontier post where Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th and 10th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry served through the 1880s, protecting travel on the San Antonio–El Paso Road. Cited from nps.gov.

designated national historic site: Fort Davis National Historic Site preserves one of the best surviving examples of a frontier military post in the Southwest, with restored and ruin buildings documenting Army life on the Texas frontier. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Established in 1877 in southeastern Arizona, Fort Huachuca became the headquarters of the 10th Cavalry and a long-term home for Buffalo Soldier regiments that patrolled the borderlands, fought in the Apache campaigns, and later served through two world wars. Its historic district and museum document more than a century of Black military service in the West.

notabilityBasis.note: headquarters of: Fort Huachuca served as headquarters of the 10th U.S. Cavalry and hosted Buffalo Soldier units including the 9th Cavalry, 24th Infantry, and 25th Infantry across the Apache Wars and border-service eras. Cited from nps.gov.

listed on national register: The Fort Huachuca Historic District was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1974, recognizing the post's role in frontier Army history including Buffalo Soldier service. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Built in 1867 at the junction of the North and Middle Concho Rivers in San Angelo, Fort Concho anchored U.S. control of West Texas. Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry garrisoned the post from 1869 to 1885, scouting, building roads, and protecting settlers on the frontier.

notabilityBasis.note: garrisoned by: Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry served at Fort Concho from 1869 to 1885, among the longest continuous Black garrisons on a single Texas frontier post. Cited from fortconcho.com.

designated national historic landmark: Fort Concho was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1961 for its exceptionally well-preserved frontier military architecture and documentation of post–Civil War western expansion. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Built in 1858 in the San Luis Valley, Fort Garland guarded settlers in southern Colorado. From 1876 to 1879 the 9th Cavalry — Buffalo Soldiers — garrisoned the post under Colonel Kit Carson's successor command structure, patrolling Ute country before the fort closed in 1883.

notabilityBasis.note: garrisoned by: Company G of the 9th U.S. Cavalry, a Buffalo Soldier unit, garrisoned Fort Garland from 1876 to 1879, replacing white troops withdrawn from the San Luis Valley. Cited from historycolorado.org.

listed on national register: Fort Garland was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1970; five of its original adobe buildings survive as a Colorado History Museum site. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Established in 1865 on the Rio Grande near Las Cruces, Fort Selden housed Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry who protected settlers, mail routes, and railroad construction in southern New Mexico. Ruins of the adobe post are preserved as a New Mexico Historic Site.

notabilityBasis.note: garrisoned by: Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th Cavalry and 24th and 25th Infantry served at Fort Selden from the late 1860s through the 1880s, guarding the Rio Grande valley and southern transcontinental routes. Cited from nmhistoricsites.org.
interpreted as: Fort Selden Historic Site preserves adobe ruins and interprets frontier Army life, including a dedicated focus on Buffalo Soldier service in the Mesilla Valley. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Built between 1919 and 1923 near the Arizona–Sonora border, Camp Naco is the last surviving adobe post from the U.S. Army's Mexican Border Defense network. Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th and 10th Cavalry and 25th Infantry garrisoned the cantonment from 1911 to 1924, patrolling a border crossed daily by revolution-era violence.

notabilityBasis.note: garrisoned by: African American troops from the 9th and 10th Cavalry and 25th Infantry — Buffalo Soldiers — were assigned to Camp Naco from 1911 to 1924 to patrol the border during the Mexican Revolution. Cited from nps.gov.

listed on national register: The Camp Naco Historic District was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 2012 as the only intact adobe border-defense camp from the 1919–1923 building program. Cited from npgallery.nps.gov.

docs.summary: Fort Craig guarded the Rio Grande crossing on the El Camino Real de Tierra Adentro south of Socorro. After the Civil War, Buffalo Soldiers of the 9th Cavalry and 38th and 125th Infantry rotated through the post, patrolling Apache country until the fort was abandoned in 1885.

notabilityBasis.note: garrisoned by: Following the Civil War Battle of Valverde fought at the post, Fort Craig was re-garrisoned by Buffalo Soldiers including units of the 9th Cavalry who patrolled southern New Mexico through the 1870s and 1880s. Cited from nps.gov.
managed by: Fort Craig National Historic Site is managed by the Bureau of Land Management and preserves ruins of one of the largest frontier forts in the Southwest. Cited from blm.gov.

docs.summary: Founded in 1903 on Creek Nation land in Okfuskee County, Boley grew from a railroad construction camp into one of the largest and most prosperous all-Black towns in the United States, with its own bank, schools, and businesses. Its surviving commercial core is a National Historic Landmark District.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: Boley was founded in 1903 on land allotted to Creek Freedmen, growing from a Black railroad construction camp into a self-governing town of several thousand residents by the 1910s. Cited from okhistory.org.

designated national historic landmark: The Boley Historic District was listed in the National Register of Historic Places and designated a National Historic Landmark in 1975, recognizing one of the nation's most significant surviving all-Black towns. Cited from npgallery.nps.gov.

docs.summary: Opened for settlement in April 1890 in Oklahoma Territory, Langston was promoted by Black town builders including Edward P. McCabe as a self-governing community where African Americans could own land and vote. Langston University, established in 1897, helped the town survive as one of Oklahoma's thirteen remaining historic all-Black towns.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: Langston was opened for settlement on April 22, 1890, by promoters including Edward P. McCabe and landowner Charles Robbins, who marketed the town to Black migrants from Kansas and the South. Cited from okhistory.org.

established institution: The Oklahoma Territorial Legislature established the Colored Agricultural and Normal University at Langston in 1897 — now Langston University, Oklahoma's only public HBCU. Cited from cityoflangstonok.gov.

docs.summary: Founded in 1910 by Oliver Toussaint Jackson on the dry plains of Weld County, Dearfield became the largest Black homesteading settlement in Colorado, reaching roughly 300 residents and profitable dryland farms before drought and the Dust Bowl emptied the town. Two buildings and the townsite survive as a National Register historic district.

notabilityBasis.note: founded by: Oliver Toussaint Jackson founded Dearfield in 1910 through the Negro Townsite and Land Company, drawing nineteen initial homesteaders who built dugouts and later frame houses on the high plains northeast of Denver. Cited from nps.gov.

listed on national register: The Dearfield historic district was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1995, preserving the founder's filling station, residence, and townsite ruins of Colorado's largest Black homesteading colony. Cited from nps.gov.

docs.summary: Incorporated in 1903 by Frank and Ella Boyer and twelve other African American founders, Blackdom was the first all-Black settlement in New Mexico, peaking at roughly 300 residents with a church, school, post office, and store on the Pecos Valley prairie south of Roswell before drought drove families out by the mid-1920s.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: The Blackdom Townsite Company was incorporated in 1903 with Frank Boyer as president; settlement began around 1908–1909 when homesteaders filed claims under the Homestead Act and related land laws. Cited from nps.gov.

marked by: The New Mexico Historic Preservation Division erected a state historical marker at the Blackdom townsite commemorating the community founded by Francis Marion and Ella Boyer. Cited from hmdb.org.

docs.summary: In 1879 Exodusters — many from Tennessee — purchased land in Topeka's King's Addition and built Tennessee Town, one of the first concentrated Black neighborhoods in Kansas's capital and a western anchor of the Exoduster migration. By the 1890s the community supported churches, schools, and the first Black kindergarten west of the Mississippi River.

notabilityBasis.note: settled by: In 1879 a colony of approximately 500 Exodusters purchased land in Topeka's King's Addition; many migrants came from Tennessee, giving the neighborhood its name. Cited from kansashistory.gov.

established institution: In 1893 Rev. Charles Sheldon opened the Tennessee Town Kindergarten in Central Congregational Church — documented as the first Black kindergarten west of the Mississippi River. Cited from tscpl.org.

docs.displayName: Bill Pickett Memorial, Fort Worth Stockyards
docs.nameLower: bill pickett memorial, fort worth stockyards
docs.summary: Born in 1870 in Texas's Williamson County, Bill Pickett invented bulldogging — wrestling steers by the lip — and became one of the most famous Black cowboys in the West, performing with the 101 Ranch Wild West Show. A bronze memorial at the Fort Worth Stockyards honors his legacy in the cattle-driving culture Black Texans helped build.

notabilityBasis.note: honored at: A Bill Pickett memorial stands in the Fort Worth Stockyards National Historic District, commemorating the Black cowboy who invented bulldogging and performed nationally with the 101 Ranch Wild West Show. Cited from nps.gov.

recognized as: Bill Pickett was the first Black cowboy inducted into the Rodeo Hall of Fame at the National Cowboy & Western Heritage Museum in 1971, decades after his death in 1932. Cited from nationalcowboymuseum.org.

docs.summary: In 1908 the Black cowboy and ranch foreman George McJunkin discovered ancient bison bones in a arroyo after a flood on Crowfoot Ranch near Folsom, New Mexico — evidence that led archaeologists to the 1926 Folsom excavation proving human presence in North America more than 10,000 years ago. The Folsom Site is listed in the National Register of Historic Places.

notabilityBasis.note: discovered by: George McJunkin, a formerly enslaved Black cowboy and ranch foreman at Crowfoot Ranch, found deeply buried bison bones exposed by a 1908 flood in Wild Horse Arroyo and recognized their antiquity. Cited from nps.gov.

listed on national register: The Folsom Site was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1978 after the 1926 excavation confirmed Folsom projectile points alongside extinct bison, rewriting the timeline of human settlement in the Americas. Cited from npgallery.nps.gov.

docs.summary: Buffalo Soldier regiments garrisoned Fort Bliss and the El Paso borderlands from 1869 to 1885, protecting routes through the Southwest. The 1999 bronze memorial titled The Errand of Corporal Ross, depicting 9th Cavalry trooper John Ross, stands at the post's Buffalo Soldier Road entrance.

notabilityBasis.note: garrisoned by: Several Buffalo Soldier regiments, including the 9th and 10th Cavalry, garrisoned Fort Bliss and nearby areas from 1869 to 1885 during the Army's westward expansion. Cited from army.mil.

memorialized at: The Errand of Corporal Ross, a bronze Buffalo Soldier memorial by artist Bob Snead, was erected at Fort Bliss in 1999 along what was renamed Buffalo Soldier Road in 2014. Cited from texastimetravel.com.
